cuisine: 十割そば（信州・善光寺門前）
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町46-1
geocode_note: Coordinates sourced from 4travel/navitime aggregator data; GSI geocoder returned non-matching results for 大門町. Point is ~703m from Zenkō-ji, within 1.61km target radius.
neighborhood: 大門町・善光寺表参道
seats_text: 49席（カウンター3席含む、テーブル・座敷）
hours_raw: 月〜火・木〜日 11:00〜15:00（そば売り切れ次第閉店）、水曜定休（GW・年末年始・お盆は営業）
closed_days: 水曜日（GW・年末年始・お盆は営業）
cultural_comfort_note: Staff are polite but English is minimal; ordering is straightforward with a numbered ticket system and visual menu.
gf_detail: Soba noodles are 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder — genuinely wheat-free. Tsuyu (dipping broth) uses wheat-free tamari soy sauce (田舎たまり醤油) aged 3 months in ceramic vessels. This is a well-established GF claim for soba + tsuyu. However, the menu also includes tempura (wheat batter) and fried items, which represent a cross-contamination risk at a shared kitchen. Diners avoiding gluten should order plain soba and skip tempura/fried sides; staff do not appear to have a formal allergy protocol beyond the wheat-free soy sauce.
vegan_detail: Dashi is fish/seafood-based (魚介系); no vegan broth option documented. Menu also includes 肉そば (beef soba) and egg-containing tempura. Not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: A specialist soba shop on the Zenkō-ji Omotesando pilgrimage approach in Nagano, in business long enough to have been featured on TV Asahi's travel programme 'Ii Tabi Yume Kibun' (2006). The current proprietor is described in food writing as a young master bringing a new-generation ambition to Shinshu soba tradition.
bio.specialty: 手打ち十割そば — 100% buckwheat soba noodles with no wheat binder, made fresh daily from 霧下そば (fog-grown buckwheat) sourced from the slopes of Mt. Kurohime in Shinano Town.
bio.origin: 長野市大門町（善光寺表参道）
bio.philosophy: Freshly ground, freshly rolled, freshly boiled (挽きたて・打ち立て・茹でたて) — the shop runs until the day's soba is sold out, never holding over stock.

anecdotes.text: One food writer noted: 'The 100% soba has exceptional smoothness and texture — clearly made with considerable skill' (十割なのにのど越しがすごくよく、ボソボソ感もなく、かなりな技術で打たれている).
The tsuyu uses a house kaeshi aged three months in ceramic jars using wheat-free tamari soy sauce — an intentional choice that also makes the broth safe for wheat-allergy diners.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and fried side dishes (wheat batter) are prepared in the same kitchen as the GF soba. No dedicated fryer or separate preparation area is documented. Celiac diners should stick to plain soba and wheat-free tsuyu and skip all fried items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsuyu uses wheat-free tamari soy sauce (田舎たまり醤油 / 本醸造たまり醤油) aged 3 months — this is explicitly wheat-free and is the restaurant's stated GF credential.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi is fish/seafood-based (魚介系). No vegan broth alternative is offered. All soba dishes are served with this dashi-based tsuyu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No formal allergy protocol documented. The wheat-free soy sauce is a built-in kitchen choice, not a made-to-order accommodation. Staff speak limited English; communicating specific celiac needs will require Japanese or a written card.

positives.text: Soba noodles are 100% buckwheat (no wheat binder) and the tsuyu uses wheat-free tamari soy sauce — both the noodle and broth are intrinsically wheat-free by design, not as a special accommodation.
The wheat-free tamari tsuyu is house-made and aged 3 months using additive-free ingredients, giving both dietary safety and depth of flavour.

caveats: Hours updated to 11:00-15:00 as of April 2025 (previously 10:30). Shop closes early when soba sells out — arriving by 13:00 recommended. Tempura on the menu poses cross-contamination risk for celiac diners despite the wheat-free soba and tsuyu. Dashi is fish-based; not suitable for vegans. No chef name confirmed from available sources.

cuisine: 精進料理（仏教式精進食・信州地産）
vegan_detail: Shojin ryori is fully plant-based by Buddhist tradition: no meat, fish, seafood, eggs, or dairy. The kitchen also excludes the "five pungents" (garlic, leeks, scallions, chives, onion) per monastic rule. The restaurant explicitly states that vegetarian and vegan guests can dine with confidence (「ベジタリアン、ヴィ―ガンの方も安心してお召し上がりいただけます」). Dashi is vegetable- or mushroom-based, not fish-based. Soba noodles appear on some courses — advance notice can yield substitution.
gf_detail: No gluten-free certification or dedicated GF protocols are in place. Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is used in shojin cooking and no tamari substitution is confirmed. Soba noodles appear in several course menus — traditional soba blends often contain wheat flour, and the kitchen explicitly mentions soba-allergy substitutions as available on request, which implies wheat is present. The restaurant asks guests to notify them of all allergies at the time of reservation; modifications with possible surcharge are offered. Cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out. Not safe for celiac without a direct advance conversation in Japanese.
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野元善町462
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch on 長野市大字長野元善町462 returned [138.187668, 36.657806], approximately 400m from Zenkoji (36.6614, 138.1872) — well within 1.61km.
neighborhood: 善光寺門前（元善町）
hours_raw: 昼食11:30〜、夕食17:00〜（要予約）。月〜日営業。Tabelog掲載は11:00-14:00だが公式サイトは11:30〜。夕食は宿泊者または要相談。
closed_days: 不定休（要問合せ）
seats_text: 7つの個室（2〜20名用）と大広間。食事のみの場合も全席個室（和室）。最大200名の食事対応可。
cultural_comfort_note: A working Buddhist temple lodging; staff communication is primarily in Japanese and the experience is centered on traditional pilgrimage hospitality — prior Japanese-language contact for reservations and allergy notice is strongly advised.
caveats: Hours vary; dinner service is primarily for overnight guests or by special arrangement. Elevator temporarily out of service due to construction (around July 2026). Always call ahead — Tabelog notes hours may change.

bio.background: 淵之坊は善光寺に属する39の宿坊のひとつで、古くより「縁起堂」とも称されてきた歴史ある寺院。住職は若麻績享則師（Wakaomi Takanori）。400年以上の歴史をもち、全国からの参拝者・巡礼者を精進料理でもてなしてきた。
bio.origin: 長野市元善町462、善光寺境内そば。善光寺下駅より徒歩3分。
bio.specialty: 「満月」（信州松代産の山芋で包んだ椎茸・くるみ入り蒸し饅頭）と胡麻豆腐。信州産地元野菜をふんだんに使った季節の精進懐石。
bio.philosophy: 「野菜も生き物。いただいた命を生かし」——素材の命を丁寧に扱うことを料理の根本に置く。修行食としての質素な精進と、参拝者をもてなす「おもてなし精進」の両面を体現する。
roles: ceremonial cuisine for weddings and memorial services

anecdotes.text: 総料理長の小川雅弘氏は約30年にわたり淵之坊の厨房を取り仕切り、善光寺の祭礼食をベースにした季節の献立を手がけてきた。
精進料理を食べた参拝者から「肉を使っているのかと思うほどおいしい」と言われるほど、植物性素材で豊かなコクを生み出す技術が評判。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen protocols are documented. Soba noodles (which often contain wheat flour in standard Japanese blends) appear across multiple course tiers. Cross-contamination in a shared temple kitchen cannot be excluded.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat is the default in shojin cooking at this establishment; no mention of tamari or wheat-free soy sauce substitution was found on the official site or in any reviewed source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The cuisine is 100% plant-based by religious rule — no animal products are used in the kitchen. Vegan cross-contact risk is effectively zero within the shojin framework.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant requests that all allergies and dietary restrictions be communicated at the time of reservation (事前にお知らせください). Substitutions are available — soba allergies are specifically cited as an example — and additional charges may apply for special accommodations.

positives.text: The kitchen is entirely free of meat, fish, seafood, eggs, and dairy — a structural advantage for vegan diners and those avoiding animal-derived dashi or broth.
The restaurant explicitly states 「ベジタリアン、ヴィ―ガンの方も安心してお召し上がりいただけます」 (vegetarian and vegan guests can dine with confidence).

cuisine: 十割そば（信州蕎麦）、くるみつゆ、とろろつゆ
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町67番地
geocode_note: GSI API did not resolve 大門町 in Nagano City directly — returned only city-level results. Coordinates estimated from documented Zenkoji monzen approach location (大門町 runs south from Zenkoji at 36.6614, 138.1872); restaurant is clearly within 1.61km of target.
neighborhood: 善光寺門前・大門町
seats_text: 64 seats across multiple seating styles: counter, table, raised (hori-gotatsu), and tatami. Second floor seats approximately 20.
hours_raw: 11時00分〜15時00分（14時30分ラストオーダー）／火曜日・木曜日・不定休（祝祭日・繁忙期は除く）
closed_days: 火曜・木曜（祝日・繁忙期を除く）、不定休あり
cultural_comfort_note: Traditional Edo-period soba-ya atmosphere with tatami seating; English website exists but in-person service is conducted in Japanese, and staff are unlikely to be conversant in dietary terminology in English.
vegan_detail: The soba noodles themselves are made from 100% buckwheat with no animal ingredients, but the standard dipping sauces (tsuyu) are built on a dashi of katsuobushi (dried bonito) and Rishiri kombu — not vegan. The walnut broth (kurumi-jiru) and tororo (mountain yam) broth are also served alongside bonito-based tsuyu as part of the signature Gokuraku set. A diner willing to eat plain soba without dipping sauce (soba-mizu style) could avoid fish dashi, but this is not a menu accommodation the restaurant advertises. No vegan-specific options are listed.
gf_detail: The soba noodles are 十割 (100% buckwheat, no wheat binder) and are inherently wheat-free. However, the dipping sauces use standard Japanese soy sauce (醤油), which is brewed with wheat — the restaurant's own allergen table lists wheat among the 28 allergens present in the menu. The buckwheat tea served at the end also poses a risk for those with buckwheat allergies. No dedicated gluten-free tsuyu or wheat-free soy sauce is confirmed. The facility mills and handles buckwheat on-site; the official site warns of potential cross-contamination and asks guests with allergies to notify staff immediately. Celiac diners should communicate their need for tamari or wheat-free soy sauce before ordering, and should accept that cross-contamination risk from shared premises cannot be fully ruled out.

bio.background: Founded in 1827 (Bunsei 10) during the reign of the 11th Tokugawa shogun, Fujikian has stood on the main approach to Zenkoji Temple for nearly 200 years, passing through eight generations of the Fujisawa family. A well from the Edo period — one of Zenkoji's storied 'Seven Pools' — once provided the quality water used in soba production.
bio.specialty: Towari soba (100% buckwheat, no binder) hand-milled from Kirishita buckwheat grown in Shinano Town, served with a choice of dipping sauces including the house walnut broth (kurumi-jiru) and grated mountain yam (tororo) from Matsushiro — combined as the signature Gokuraku Soba.
bio.philosophy: The 8th-generation owner studied soba craft at the renowned Issaian school under master Katakura Yasuo, explicitly rejecting intuition-only methods in favour of quantified, reproducible technique. He sources Kirishita buckwheat (grown in areas of high diurnal temperature variation and morning fog in Shinano Town) for its complex fragrance, stone-mills it on-site, and adjusts water ratios daily by observing the flour.
bio.origin: 善光寺門前、長野市大門町

anecdotes.text: Current owner Fujisawa Koichi was working as a bank employee when he decided in 1999 to leave his career and take over the family soba business as the 8th generation. He enrolled at the Issaian school specifically to learn a data-driven approach to soba-making rather than relying on craft intuition passed down informally.
The Gokuraku Soba — featuring three different dipping sauces served in progression from lightest to richest — was devised by the 7th generation and became especially popular with female diners.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Buckwheat flour is milled and hand-processed on-site. The restaurant's official site warns of potential cross-contamination from shared preparation surfaces and asks guests with any allergy to notify staff before ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping tsuyu and sauces are made with standard Japanese soy sauce (醤油), which is brewed with wheat. The restaurant's own allergen disclosure lists wheat among the 28 allergens present on the menu. No wheat-free or tamari soy sauce alternative is confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The primary dashi stock is made from katsuobushi (dried bonito) and Rishiri kombu. All standard dipping sauces contain bonito-based dashi. No vegan broth option is confirmed on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site explicitly states that guests with allergies must inform staff immediately upon arrival, as cross-contamination is possible from on-site buckwheat milling and noodle preparation.

positives.text: The soba noodles themselves are made from 100% buckwheat (十割) with no wheat binder (つなぎなし), so the noodle strand itself is free of gluten-containing ingredients.

caveats: Hours list Tuesday and Thursday as closed, but the official site notes exceptions for national holidays and busy periods (irregular schedule). Thursday closure is confirmed by the official shopinfo page but not all aggregator sites reflect it — call ahead. Coordinates are estimated; GSI API did not resolve 大門町 in Nagano City. Cash-only status reported by one older source (Nagano Saijiki); the official website currently lists credit cards, electronic money, and QR codes as accepted — verify on arrival. Seat count varies by source (58 per blog, 64 per Tabelog).

address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町463
geocode_note: GSI address-search API returned only city-level coordinates (長野県長野市). Coordinates estimated from known address position: 元善町 is the street directly in front of Zenkoji temple's Sanmon gate, placing 元善町463 immediately within the temple precinct. Zenkoji main hall is at 36.6614, 138.1872; this address is within meters of that point.
neighborhood: 善光寺門前 (Zenkoji Monzen)
cuisine: 精進料理 (Shojin Ryori — Buddhist vegetarian kaiseki)
vegan_detail: Shojin ryori excludes all meat, fish, and animal products by Buddhist principle. Dishes use vegetables, grains, seaweed, tofu, yuba, and mushrooms. Alliums (garlic, onion, leek) are also excluded per Buddhist tradition. However, standard Japanese soy sauce (used in sauces such as grilled miso-radish dipping broth) contains wheat, and wheat gluten (fu) is a featured ingredient — the cuisine is fully vegan but not wheat-free. No katsuo/bonito dashi; vegetable-based dashi throughout.
gf_detail: No gluten-free designation and significant wheat exposure: (1) standard soy sauce (醤油) contains wheat and appears in sauces; (2) millet wheat gluten (fu) is a named signature ingredient in the Muryoju course; (3) hand-pulled soba is served — cross-contamination risk in the noodle preparation. The kitchen does note that menu content can be adjusted per request, so it may be worth calling ahead (026-234-6677), but there is no stated GF protocol. Celiac diners should not assume safety without direct confirmation.
seats_text: Up to 60 guests across 12 rooms; private room dining for meal guests. Minimum 2 persons for meal reservations per official Zenkoji listing.
hours_raw: Lunch: 11:30, 12:00, 13:00 seatings (by reservation); check-in 15:00–17:30, check-out by 10:00. Meals for non-lodging guests available at lunch only, by reservation 3 days in advance.
closed_days: No fixed closed days confirmed; reservation required 3 days in advance so effective availability varies
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Zenkoji temple lodge (shukubo) operating for centuries — meals are served in tatami private rooms with a personal explanation of each dish from the proprietress; basic Japanese helps greatly, though the welcoming atmosphere is genuine even for first-time visitors.
caveats: Meal hours (days open for non-lodging guests) are reservation-dependent with 3-day advance booking required — call to confirm availability. Exact daily hours not published. Seats figure (60) reflects maximum lodge capacity across all rooms; actual dining group size may vary. GF status unconfirmed — wheat-containing ingredients are integral to the menu.

bio.background: 兄部坊 (Konokonbou) is one of the 39 shukubo (temple lodgings) of Zenkoji, the most visited Buddhist temple in Japan. The name 'Konokonbo' refers to a ceremonial role overseeing Buddhist affairs at Zenkoji, historically tied to the lodge's participation in the traditional New Year's ritual 'Dodoji.' The lodge has hosted pilgrims for centuries, sitting at 元善町463 — literally on the front approach of the Sanmon gate.
bio.specialty: Unagi yuba — a signature mock-eel dish crafted from yuba (tofu skin), silken tofu, and nori, presented in the style of grilled eel kabayaki; a plant-based recreation pioneered here in the 1950s–60s. Also notable: Hohan, a sacred multi-day-broth rice, and hand-pulled Shinshu soba with walnut garnish.
bio.philosophy: Muryoju (無量寿 — Infinite Life): cooking with gratitude, honoring the life in every ingredient and minimizing waste. The cuisine philosophy was established roughly 50 years ago by the previous head priest's wife, who championed seasonal Shinshu vegetables at a time when other temple lodges had not yet formalized shojin ryori. Current executor Wakamasume Taeko continues that tradition, described as transforming humble ingredients into refined cuisine through knowledge, effort, and creativity.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Zenkoji precinct (Monzen area)
roles: Shojin ryori restaurant (lunch, by reservation for non-guests)

anecdotes.text: A reviewer traveled 1.5 hours from Karuizawa specifically for lunch, after a previous failed walk-in attempt — the ¥3,150 course included mock eel, sesame tofu, winter melon yuba sashimi, corn shinjo, eggplant dengaku, hand-pulled soba, and kuzukiri dessert, served in a private tatami room with the proprietor personally introducing each dish.

兄部坊 was a pioneer among Zenkoji's 39 temple lodges in formalizing shojin ryori for guests about 50 years ago, when the head priest's wife believed it was time to focus on seasonal, local ingredients for vegetarian cuisine — a move that predated the modern vegan/vegetarian dining trend by decades.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hand-pulled soba is prepared and served on-site; no information about dedicated equipment or separation from wheat-containing ingredients. Soba flour itself is gluten-free but cross-contact with wheat during noodle making is a real risk in Japanese temple kitchens.
Millet wheat gluten (fu) is named as a signature ingredient in the flagship Muryoju course, meaning wheat gluten is actively cooked in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat is used — the menu references a 'grilled miso and spicy radish dipping sauce' (焼味噌と辛味大根つゆ) for soba, and soy sauce is a standard shojin ryori condiment. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No animal products are used in shojin ryori cooking — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy. Buddhist culinary rules prohibit animal ingredients kitchen-wide, so cross-contact with animal products is extremely unlikely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official contact page notes 'menu content adjustable based on requests and budget' (料理内容はご要望・ご予算に応じて変更可能), suggesting the kitchen can accommodate some dietary modifications. Phone reservations (026-234-6677) allow for direct conversation about needs. No formal allergy protocol is documented.

positives.text: Fully plant-based kitchen by centuries-old Buddhist rule — no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, or bonito dashi used in any dish. Vegan diners can eat with confidence across the entire menu.
Alliums (garlic, onion, leek, chives) are excluded from all dishes per Buddhist culinary tradition, making this suitable for diners avoiding these ingredients.

cuisine: 信州蕎麦（さらしな手打ち蕎麦）
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野大門町504
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 長野県長野市大字長野大門町 returned [138.187943, 36.656933]. Approximately 500m south of Zenkoji main hall — well within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 大門町（善光寺表参道入口）
closed_days: 不定休（Irregular — no fixed day off; confirm by phone before visiting）
seats_text: 約150席（テーブル席・座敷あり）。混雑時は名前を書かず並んで待つスタイル。
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional temple-gate soba shop with no English-language support noted; menus and signage are in Japanese, though ordering is straightforward — point at laminated photos or the short menu.
vegan_detail: The tsuyu (dipping broth) is described across multiple sources as a sweet, bonito-flake (katsuo) based broth — confirmed non-vegan. There are no vegan alternative broths listed. Tempura items use wheat batter. No plant-based options are indicated anywhere on the menu.
gf_detail: Soba noodles here are hand-made on the premises from buckwheat flour, but there is no information on whether the same workspace handles wheat flour or whether wheat is mixed in (a common practice in many soba shops). The tsuyu contains standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-bearing). Tempura is battered in wheat flour and fried in shared oil. No gluten-free claim is made anywhere, and cross-contamination risk in a traditional soba kitchen is high. Not safe for celiac diners.

bio.background: Founded in 1703 (Genroku 16) at the entrance to Zenkoji Temple's main approach, Kadono Daimaru is one of Nagano's oldest surviving soba establishments — a so-called monzen soba (temple gate noodle shop) where pilgrims have stopped for a bowl before or after paying respects at Zenkoji for more than three centuries. The present building dates to 1871. The shop occupies a prominent corner plot on Daimon-cho, its two connected storefronts housing a sprawling 150-seat interior of tatami halls and table seating, with the noodle-making bench visible through a glass window at street level.
bio.origin: 長野市大門町, 1703年（元禄16年）創業
bio.specialty: さらしなそば — Sarashina soba made from the soft inner portion of the buckwheat kernel, producing characteristically pale, fine noodles with a delicate aroma and smooth texture. Served cold on a bamboo rack (zaru) or with tempura (shrimp, eggplant, sweet potato).
bio.philosophy: Freshly made and freshly boiled (打ち立て・茹でたて): noodles are rolled and cut on-site daily, and the shop opens early — well before most competitors — so the first bowl of the day is always from that morning's batch.

anecdotes.text: Visitors frequently pause at the shop's street-level window to watch an artisan rolling and cutting soba by hand — the shop has become as much a visual landmark on the Zenkoji approach as it is a dining destination.
The broth is described by multiple diners as 'sweetish with abundant bonito (katsuo) flavor' — a gentler, more approachable style than the sharp, salty tsuyus found at more austere soba specialists.

Despite often having queues during peak temple-visit season, the shop's large capacity and efficient service means waits are typically short — a reviewer tested it at 12:30 PM on a Saturday and was seated quickly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information is published about the soba kitchen's wheat-handling practices. Traditional soba shops often mix in wheat flour (up to 20–30%) to help noodles bind; it is unknown whether this shop does so or processes wheat products on the same surfaces as its soba.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu (dipping sauce) is a standard Japanese bonito-based soy sauce broth. Standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned at this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito (katsuo) is the base of all broth at this restaurant; there is no plant-based broth option. Tempura is fried in shared oil. Complete avoidance of fish-derived ingredients is not possible from the standard menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy inquiry process, or staff allergy-handling protocol has been documented at this restaurant across any reviewed source.

positives.text: Noodles are made fresh on-site daily (打ち立て・茹でたて), so the buckwheat itself is freshly milled — a positive for quality, though it does not imply any allergen controls.

caveats: Hours vary across sources (ranging from 9:00 to 18:30 in some older listings); the most current Tabelog data shows 09:30–16:45. Closed days are genuinely irregular — call 026-232-2502 before visiting. No official restaurant website is operating (the zenkoji-daimon.com link returns connection refused). Cash and PayPay only; no credit cards. Founded 1703 but under30 assessed as no given over 300 years of history.

lead: 善光寺表参道 たき澤
name_ja: 善光寺表参道 たき澤
cuisine: 蕎麦、かしわ料理、信州料理
address_ja: 長野県長野市長野大門町530
neighborhood: 大門町（権堂駅徒歩5分）善光寺表参道
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 長野県長野市長野大門町530 returned [138.187103, 36.65509]. Distance from Zenkō-ji (~36.6614, 138.1872) is approximately 0.7 km — well within 1.61 km radius.
seats_text: 95 seats total: indoor table seating, counter, private group space, and covered terrace (28 seats, pet-friendly). Courtyard with pond visible from interior.
hours_raw: Lunch: Mon–Fri 11:00–16:00, Sat/Sun/holidays 10:30–16:00; Dinner: daily 16:00–22:00 (last order 20:30). Open year-round; irregular closures possible for Obon, year-end/New Year, and Golden Week.
closed_days: No regular closed days (open year-round; may close temporarily for Obon, year-end/New Year, and Golden Week)
cultural_comfort_note: English menu available at the table; the location along the main pilgrimage approach to Zenkō-ji means staff are accustomed to foreign visitors, though communication beyond ordering basics will require Japanese or gestures.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is not vegan-friendly. Soba broth is made from bonito and mackerel dashi, and chicken (かしわ) is a pillar of the menu at every daypart. No plant-based broth alternatives or vegan dishes were identified across all sources reviewed.
gf_detail: The restaurant serves two soba styles side by side: nihan soba (二八, 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour) and juwari soba (十割, 100% buckwheat — theoretically wheat-free). However, both are produced and served in the same kitchen with no documented separation protocols, creating meaningful cross-contamination risk. The chicken tempura (かしわ天) contains wheat batter and shares the same fryer status as unknown. Soba broth (dashi) contains bonito and mackerel but no wheat was specifically noted in the tsuyu, though standard Japanese tsuyu commonly includes wheat-based soy sauce. No allergy menu, celiac accommodation, or staff allergy-handling protocol has been documented by any source. Celiacs should call ahead (026-217-2415) before visiting.

bio.background: Opened June 2, 2025, in a century-old building on the main approach to Zenkō-ji Temple — the former Takizawa Glass Shop (瀧澤商店), whose original calligraphed sign by Nagano artist Akiyama Hakugan still hangs at the entrance. The restaurant is a new brand created by Fons Co., Ltd., the Karuizawa-based operator behind the well-regarded soba restaurant Kawakamian (川上庵). Manager Hiroyuki Masukura steers the Nagano location with an eye toward revitalizing the evening dining scene along an approach that has historically gone quiet after temple visiting hours.
bio.origin: Karuizawa / Nagano City
bio.specialty: Stone-ground Shinshu buckwheat soba — thin nihan (80% buckwheat) as the house style, plus a daily-limited quantity of thick juwari (100% buckwheat) — paired with Sanada Maru–breed local chicken dishes
bio.philosophy: Rooted in the Edo-period soba-ya sake tradition: guests begin with seasonal Shinshu appetizers and local sake, then close the meal with soba. The space — courtyard pond, converted warehouse, terrace — is designed as a gathering place for pilgrims and locals alike, honoring the building's past while welcoming the neighborhood's future.

anecdotes.text: The original shopfront sign, hand-lettered by celebrated Nagano calligrapher Akiyama Hakugan for the glass shop that occupied the building for generations, was kept and repurposed as the restaurant's own signage — an intentional bridge between the building's Taisho-era identity and its new life.

Manager Masukura has said his goal is to keep the lights on along the omotesando into the evening, filling a gap left by nearby businesses that close early — positioning Takizawa as the place where a Zenkō-ji pilgrimage day ends rather than begins.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Nihan soba (wheat-containing) and juwari soba (100% buckwheat) are both made and served in the same kitchen. No source documents any separation of wheat and buckwheat preparation areas, utensils, or cooking water.
Chicken tempura (wheat batter) is a flagship menu item; dedicated fryer status is undocumented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soba tsuyu typically contains wheat-based soy sauce (醤油). No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned by any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi (bonito and mackerel) is used in the soba broth. Chicken is present throughout the menu at every service period. No plant-based broth alternative is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen sheet, or staff allergy-handling protocol has been documented by any reviewed source. Phone: 026-217-2415.

positives.text: Juwari soba (十割そば, 100% buckwheat) is available daily in limited quantities — wheat-free at the noodle level, subject to kitchen cross-contamination caveat.
Cards accepted (VISA, Mastercard, JCB, AMEX, Diners, Suica/IC cards, iD, QUICPay) — not cash-only.

caveats: Hours differ by day: Mon–Fri lunch starts 11:00; Sat/Sun/holidays lunch starts 10:30. Dinner 16:00–22:00 (LO 20:30) applies daily. The restaurant is operated by Fons Co., Ltd. (Kawakamian group), not a single independent owner, so the MOM_AND_POP category reflects its local-character positioning rather than strict independent ownership. GF safety level is low — the presence of tempura, wheat-flour nihan soba, and undocumented kitchen protocols mean celiacs should call ahead. No vegan options confirmed.

cuisine: 信州蕎麦（二八）、鴨南蛮、鴨せいろ、天ざるそば
address_ja: 長野県長野市東之門町367
geocode_note: Coordinates from official website access page (kosuge-tei.com/access/). Approx. 410m from Zenkoji — well within the 1.61km radius. GSI geocoder was unable to resolve 東之門町 precisely and returned a non-matching district; official site coordinates used instead.
neighborhood: 東之門町（善光寺裏参道）
seats_text: 64席（テーブル席・畳座席）
closed_days: 不定休（要確認）
cultural_comfort_note: Traditional Showa-era interior with tatami seating, retro posters, and a large collection of beckoning cat figurines; menus and staff communication are in Japanese, though the setting is welcoming and unhurried for any visitor.
vegan_detail: The dashi (broth) is built from urume-bushi (dried flying fish), saba-bushi (mackerel), and katsuobushi (bonito flakes) along with Rishiri kombu — fish stock is foundational to every dish. Duck (kamo) is a signature protein. No plant-based broth alternative is offered. Vegans have no viable options here.
gf_detail: The soba is ni-hachi (approximately 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour) — not gluten-free. The restaurant also serves udon (100% wheat) and tempura batter; udon is cooked in a separate pot when allergens are declared, but the kitchen handles wheat constantly. The dipping tsuyu (soba sauce) is brewed with wheat-based soy sauce. No dedicated GF preparation or gluten-free soba is offered. Celiacs should not eat here.

bio.background: Kosugetei was founded in 1895 (Meiji 28) and has operated as a temple-gate soba shop (門前そば屋) at Zenkoji for over 130 years. Originally the restaurant operated within the Zenkoji Temple grounds themselves, but relocated to its current address at Higashi-no-mon-cho in 1941 following fire-prevention regulations — the backstreet behind the main approach. Over its long history it earned the distinction of serving soba to the Showa Emperor and Imperial Prince Takamatsu, and was designated an official supplier to Zenkoji's Great Encouragement pilgrimage events.
bio.origin: 長野市東之門町（善光寺裏参道）
bio.specialty: 鴨せいろ・鴨南蛮そば — duck seiro and duck nanban soba, featuring charcoal-seared duck with negi (green onion) in a rich fish-dashi broth, alongside house-made ni-hachi buckwheat noodles from domestically sourced flour.
bio.philosophy: Faithful to methods unchanged since founding: in-house ni-hachi soba blended from locally-sourced Nagano and Hokkaido buckwheat, a dashi made without chemical additives or preservatives from Rishiri kombu and three varieties of fish-dried flakes, and a kitchen ethos of asking about allergies before preparing each order.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant originally operated inside Zenkoji's temple grounds; it was relocated to Higashi-no-mon-cho in 1941 when fire-prevention regulations required commercial kitchens to move outside the precincts.
Kosugetei served soba at imperial events, providing 御蕎麦御奉仕 to the Showa Emperor and Imperial Prince Takamatsu, and held official supplier status for Zenkoji's Great Encouragement pilgrimage events.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen routinely handles wheat flour in soba (ni-hachi blend), udon, and tempura batter. Shared cooking surfaces and utensils make cross-contamination unavoidable. This is not a safe environment for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping tsuyu is a concentrated soy-sauce-based broth (shoyu is wheat-containing); no tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi (katsuobushi, saba-bushi, urume-bushi) is the foundation of all broths and dipping sauces. Duck is a signature ingredient. No plant-based broth or vegan-specific preparation is available.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff ask about allergies before preparing udon orders and cook udon in a separate, dedicated pot when an allergy is declared — indicating awareness of cross-contamination risk.

positives.text: The house dashi is made without chemical additives or preservatives, using only natural dried fish and Rishiri kombu.

caveats: Hours are irregular (不定休) — confirm before visiting. The 定休日 pattern is unconfirmed; some sources say no regular closing day, others note Monday evening closures; lunch hours 11:00–15:00 appear consistent across all sources. Payment: PayPay/QR accepted; credit cards and IC cards reportedly not accepted per Tabelog, though policies may have changed. Seat count varies between sources (64–68); 64 used from Gnavi. Coordinates sourced from official website access page, not a geocoder API result.

cuisine: Japanese home-style cafe; weekly-rotating meat and fish lunch sets, waffles, seasonal desserts
address_ja: 長野県長野市東町142-2 SHINKOJI北棟1F
geocode_note: GSI returned chō-level match for 長野県長野市大字長野東町 — building-level precision unavailable; coordinates are representative of the district. Distance to Zenkoji ~693m, well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 東町 / Gondo Station (416m)
seats_text: Counter seats, sofa seating, round tables, long tables, and outdoor terrace; approximately 25 seats total in a renovated 1960s stationery warehouse with high ceilings
hours_raw: 火〜日 10:00〜17:00（ランチ 11:30〜14:00、ティータイム 14:00〜17:00）。定休日：月曜日、第2・第4火曜日。祝日は営業、翌日休業。
closed_days: Mondays; 2nd and 4th Tuesdays of each month; day after a public holiday if open on the holiday
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy neighborhood cafe run by local housewives with no English signage or menus noted; basic Japanese or pointing at the daily board should be sufficient as the menu is simple and visual.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat and fish set lunches. Sides include miso soup (house-made miso, dashi likely fish-based), pickles, stir-fried chicken, mackerel, and kōya-dōfu dressed with miso. No plant-based or vegan option is listed anywhere across multiple sources. Waffles and desserts contain dairy and eggs. There is no vegan path here.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese home-style cooking with miso, conventional soy sauce (wheat-containing), pickles, and a wheat-based waffle menu. No gluten-free option or allergen menu exists. Cross-contamination risk is high in this small open kitchen. Not suitable for celiac diners.

bio.background: Shinkoji Cafe opened in April 2014 inside a renovated 1960s stationery wholesale warehouse (the SHINKOJI North Building) in Nagano's Higashimachi district, just a short walk from Zenkoji Temple. The building's high ceilings and weathered timbers were preserved and fitted out with bookshelves, local pottery, and handmade accessories for sale — part cafe, part community living room. Walls display historical maps of the area from 1912.
bio.specialty: Weekly-rotating lunch sets (a meat option and a fish option, ~¥880–¥1,500) built around Niigata Agano-region Koshihikari rice, house-made miso prepared by staff at relatives' homes in Niigata, hand-pickled seasonal vegetables, and daily-delivered produce from a dedicated partner farm. Four waffle varieties and a seasonal apple pie anchor the tea-time menu.
bio.philosophy: The cafe is run by three housewives who apply a 'main housewife perspective' to cooking — prioritising food safety, quality local sourcing, and the kind of careful preparation rarely found in restaurants. High-protein, low-calorie dishes and homemade condiments reflect a commitment to wholesome everyday food rather than restaurant showmanship.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture, Japan

anecdotes.text: The cafe occupies a building once used as a stationery wholesale company. Its high ceilings and open plan give it a warehouse-meets-living-room feel that regulars describe as a 'healing space' — with books to read and local craft objects displayed on shelves.

During the COVID-era takeout period, the cafe offered four bento options (meat, fish, mixed, curry rice) all at ¥650, each featuring the same house-made rice, miso-dressed kōya-dōfu, and pickled vegetables that appear in the dine-in lunch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen or cross-contamination information is published by the restaurant. The kitchen is small and shared; wheat is present throughout (conventional soy sauce, miso, pickles, waffle batter). Cross-contamination risk must be assumed high.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu uses conventional Japanese miso and implied soy sauce (miso-dressed dishes, pickles, stir-fries). Neither tamari nor gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All lunch sets contain animal protein (chicken, mackerel). Miso soup is made with house-made miso and almost certainly fish-based dashi. There is no plant-based preparation pathway.

caveats: Hours vary: the 2nd and 4th Tuesdays are closed, so Tuesday is not a reliable open day without checking. Payment: cash and electronic money accepted; credit cards not confirmed. Seat count varies across sources (25–30); 25 is the most commonly cited figure. The official site (rifare-web.com) has a TLS certificate issue and could not be directly fetched — information sourced from third-party listings instead. Closed-days pattern should be verified directly before visiting.

cuisine: Kominka cafe; rotating handmade cakes and desserts, Japanese-Western lunch plates (curry, taco rice, hamburger steak), tea service
address_ja: 長野県長野市長野桜枝町812
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 長野県長野市長野桜枝町812 returned [138.184204, 36.659378]. Approximately 250m from Zenkō-ji, well within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 桜枝町 / Zenkōjishita
closed_days: 毎週水曜日・毎週木曜日
seats_text: Spacious seating with sofa areas across both Japanese tatami and Western-style sections; exact count not published. 2 parking spaces.
cultural_comfort_note: All signage and menus are in Japanese only; no English-language staff reported — basic Japanese or a translation app is strongly recommended.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling exists. Lunch plates include meat (hamburger steak, taco rice with meat, keema curry) and dairy appears in cakes and desserts. Hidden animal-derived ingredients (dashi, dairy, eggs) are almost certainly present throughout. No source confirms any vegan-friendly item.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options are advertised or documented. The menu contains wheat throughout: sandwiches (croque monsieur, bacon-egg), curry roux, taco rice seasoning, and baked goods/cakes. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is very likely used in cooking. No dedicated fryer or allergen information found. Celiacs should treat this as a high-risk environment and call ahead.

bio.background: The cafe occupies a converted old Japanese house that was formerly a calligraphy school run by the owner's grandfather. The building retains its original tatami rooms and scroll decorations, giving it the feel of a schoolhouse from 50–60 years ago. Previous calligraphy students occasionally drop in to revisit their old classroom.
bio.specialty: Rotating daily selection of 4–5 handmade cakes (matcha roll cake, pistachio roll, caramel apple tart, clafoutis, cheesecake), brewed tea prepared with careful technique
bio.philosophy: A deliberately unhurried, hidden-gem neighbourhood cafe — the owner makes her own cups and dishware as well as the cakes, and the space welcomes solo diners, families, and anyone wanting to linger without pressure.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture

anecdotes.text: The building was once a calligraphy school run by the owner's grandfather; former students still stop by to reminisce, giving the space an unusually personal sense of history.
Staff are noted for meticulous tea preparation — vigorously drawing fresh water and heating it properly before service — a small but telling sign of the care put into every cup.
The owner handcrafts both the cups/dishware used in the cafe and the daily rotation of cakes, making every visit slightly different.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen or cross-contamination information is published anywhere. The kitchen prepares wheat-heavy items (baked cakes, sandwiches, curry roux) alongside all other dishes, making cross-contact highly probable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce mentioned. Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is assumed given the menu style (taco rice seasoning, curry, stewed dishes).

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat and dairy are used throughout the kitchen in multiple dishes simultaneously; no vegan-safe prep area or labeling exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy is documented on any source. The cafe is a small owner-operated establishment where the single owner/cook handles all preparation; direct inquiry by phone (026-232-7863) before visiting is the only way to assess safety.

positives.text: Small, owner-run kitchen means fewer hands and potentially more flexibility to accommodate requests — calling ahead in Japanese gives the best chance of a custom accommodation.

caveats: Tabelog shows no closure notice and the most recent review is from April 2024, but web search surfaced a reference to a Nagano closure list for December 2024 (the note.com page was inaccessible). Operating status cannot be fully confirmed beyond April 2024 — call ahead on 026-232-7863 before visiting. Blog sources describing menu and atmosphere are from 2019; offerings may have changed.

cuisine: Fermented Shinshu specialties, creative Japanese izakaya
neighborhood: 東後町 / Zenkoji gate area (Monzen)
address_ja: 長野県長野市東後町16-1 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoder did not return a match for 東後町 specifically; coordinates sourced from Navitime/search snippet (36.653683, 138.187496), consistent with Zenkoji gate area and ~0.86km from Zenkoji temple — within the 1.61km radius.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu. The cuisine centers on fermented Shinshu foods — miso, soy sauce, sake lees, fish sauce — virtually all of which contain wheat. Standard Japanese soy sauce is used throughout. No dedicated fryer information found. Cross-contamination risk is high; celiac diners should call ahead and expect very limited safe options.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. The concept is omnivorous: pork (sake-lees grill), chicken karaage, mackerel, fermented butter, yogurt, and fish sauce all feature prominently. Some vegetable-forward dishes appear (tofu-avocado spring roll, koji butter potato fries, salads) but hidden dashi or bonito in sauces and miso soup bases is likely and not disclosed on the menu. Vegetarians should ask per dish.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:00-15:00 (LO 14:30) / 18:00-22:00 (food LO 21:30, drinks LO 22:00); Sun 11:00-15:00 (LO 14:30); closed Mon and Sun evenings
closed_days: Monthly: Mondays (and the following Tuesday when Monday falls on a holiday). Sunday dinner service not offered.
seats_text: 35 seats across counter and table seating; a semi-private raised tatami-style room fits 6–8. Standing events up to 50.
cultural_comfort_note: Full English website and originally conceived to introduce Shinshu food culture to international visitors; English-language engagement is part of the brand identity.
caveats: Hours confirmed from multiple sources but Sunday dinner closure and Monday holiday rules should be verified directly before visiting. Seat count varies between 35 and 40 across sources (HotPepper: 35; Tabelog: 40); 35 used as the more conservative figure.

bio.background: Operated by 合同会社U.I.international, led by Tomoki Kimishima, a Nagano native. The company's first location opened in Yudanaka in 2016, targeting foreign visitors drawn to the nearby ski resorts and hot springs. The Zenkoji Monzen branch opened September 20, 2019, inside a painstakingly renovated century-old former tabi (traditional Japanese sock) merchant's house — preserving original architectural elements including beams and earthen walls while adding striking neon signage reading 'Fermentation Ism.'
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture (Zenkoji gate area)
bio.specialty: Fermented curry — a rotating blend of onion sweetness, multiple spices, Shinshu miso, fish sauce, fermented butter, vinegar, and yogurt, varying by season.
bio.philosophy: To transmit Shinshu's fermentation culture to the world through enjoyable dining rather than wellness mandate. Staff member Isazaki Saizo put it simply: 'I'd like visitors to use this place for memorable travel moments and time with loved ones, then discover fermented foods naturally afterward.' The kitchen leans on Nagano Prefecture's extraordinary fermentation heritage — Shinshu miso alone accounts for roughly 50% of Japan's national miso output — and connects diners directly with local producers through seasonal collaborations and occasional talk events.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies what was once a tabi (Japanese sock) shop built in the Taisho era (circa 1926). The renovation kept the original structure intact while installing bold neon 'Fermentation Ism' signage that has become a landmark on the Monzen approach.

Staff are reportedly in their early twenties — a young team that developed and opened this second location, embracing fermentation as both culinary philosophy and personal discovery. Isazaki Saizo began as a part-time university student, rejected other job offers after graduating, and stayed on as recipe developer.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination protocols is available from any source reviewed. The kitchen handles miso, soy sauce (standard wheat-containing), sake lees, and fish sauce across all dishes, making a wheat-free environment highly unlikely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce is a core ingredient across the menu. Miso (which contains wheat in most Shinshu varieties) and fermented condiments are foundational to the entire concept. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Animal-derived fermented ingredients — fish sauce, fermented butter, yogurt, bonito-based sauces, sake-lees pork — are used throughout the kitchen. Vegan cross-contact in a shared kitchen is highly probable and no vegan protocols are disclosed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or allergen menu is mentioned on the official website, Tabelog, HotPepper, or any blog reviewed. The English website exists but contains no allergen disclosure section.

positives.text: Full English website and international visitor orientation mean staff are likely more accustomed to dietary questions from non-Japanese guests than a typical izakaya. Calling ahead is feasible.

lead: いろは堂 善光寺仲見世店
name_ja: いろは堂 善光寺仲見世店
cuisine: Oyaki (regional stuffed dumplings)
address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町486
geocode_note: GSI geocoder on full street address; 1,584m from Zenkō-ji — within 1.61km radius. Within bounding box lat 36.647–36.676, lng 138.169–138.205.
neighborhood: 元善町 / Zenkōji Nakamise arcade
vegan_detail: Most standard fillings — nozawana, green onion miso, vegetable mix, kiriboshi daikon, mushroom, pumpkin, bean paste — are plant-based with no dashi, bonito, eggs, or dairy found in published ingredient lists. Dough uses wheat flour, buckwheat flour, soybean oil, yeast, and salt. Miso seasoning is Shinshu miso (soy-based, no fish). Ingredient lists do show "seasoning (amino acids)" which may warrant a check; overall the lineup reads as incidentally vegan across most varieties, but staff should be asked if strict adherence is needed. The red bean paste variety also appears animal-free.
gf_detail: The dough is explicitly a blend of wheat flour and buckwheat flour — gluten is a core ingredient, not a contaminant. Declared allergens on product packaging include wheat, buckwheat, and soy, with a shared-line note for sesame and apples. There is no gluten-free option and no dedicated fryer context for GF; the fry-bake process uses the same soybean oil for all varieties. Celiac diners should avoid entirely.
closed_days: Wednesdays (December through March, except March 20); irregular closures possible — confirm before visiting
seats_text: Takeout-only counter on the Nakamise arcade; no indoor seating. Oyaki are eaten while walking the temple approach.
cultural_comfort_note: Counter ordering at a busy temple-street stall; pointing at the oyaki in the display case works perfectly, and some signage is in English — no Japanese required beyond a greeting.

bio.background: Irohado was founded in December 1925 in the mountain village of Kinasa, on the outskirts of Nagano City, originally producing bread and Japanese confections before pivoting to oyaki in the 1960s as the dish shifted from home-cooked staple to commercially sold street food. The company is now led by Hiromu Ito, the fourth-generation president, who has grown the operation to 130 employees while continuing to hand-wrap every vegetable oyaki — a skill that takes roughly a year to master and runs at about 20 seconds per piece.
bio.origin: Kinasa, Nagano City (founded 1925); Zenkoji Nakamise branch opened May 2020
bio.specialty: Oyaki — chewy wheat-and-buckwheat dough stuffed with seasoned Nagano vegetables (nozawana pickles, green onion miso, kiriboshi daikon, bunashimeji mushrooms), fried briefly in soybean oil then baked at high heat to achieve a crisp, fluffy shell
bio.philosophy: Ito's stated aim is to make oyaki an everyday food enjoyed in any setting — with beer, with coffee, as a meal — while preserving its identity as a distinctly Nagano dish rooted in mountain villages where rice could not be grown.

anecdotes.text: Irohado won the Grand Prix at the 75th Japan Food Selection (May 2024) for its vegetable mix oyaki, the highest award category in that competition.

The company hand-wraps all vegetable oyaki one by one, requiring up to a year of training to perfect; with daily output of 8,000–14,000 units at Oyaki Farm, the speed and consistency demanded of each worker is considerable.

Fourth-generation president Hiromu Ito said: 'I'd like people to enjoy them in many different settings. Oyaki are an easy way to get more vegetables. They go well with beer, and they pair nicely with coffee too.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Dough contains wheat flour as a primary ingredient — there is no gluten-free option and no separation from wheat. The fry-bake process uses the same soybean oil for all varieties. Celiac diners must avoid.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce appears in published ingredient lists. Seasoning is Shinshu miso (soy-based) and salt. Miso inherently contains soy but the declared allergens are wheat, buckwheat, and soy — soy sauce as a separate wheat-bearing additive is not listed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All standard varieties appear plant-based; no animal products identified in ingredient lists. A shared production line note mentions sesame and apple but no mention of dairy or egg lines. Cross-contact with animal products is not documented as a risk, but the facility is not certified vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No documented allergy-handling protocol for the Nakamise stall. Counter service is fast-paced temple-street takeout; detailed allergen queries are best directed to Irohado's main customer service line (026-217-6636) before visiting.

positives.text: Published ingredient lists for standard varieties — nozawana, green onion miso, vegetable mix, kiriboshi daikon, mushroom, pumpkin, bean paste — show no dashi, bonito, eggs, or dairy; all fillings are vegetable and miso-based.
Miso seasoning is a customized Shinshu miso sourced from a local established miso maker; no fish-based dashi listed as an ingredient in any variety.

caveats: Hours split between weekday (until 16:00) and weekend/holiday (until 17:00); Wednesday closures apply December–March only. Irregular closure days occur — phone ahead (026-217-6636) for day-of confirmation. Shared production line for sesame and apple; no animal product cross-contact documented but facility is not vegan-certified. The "amino acids" seasoning note in some ingredient lists is unspecified and could theoretically be animal-derived — strict vegans should confirm directly.

cuisine: Soba, udon, seasonal kakigori (shaved ice), traditional Shinshu sweets
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野元善町468
geocode_note: GSI geocode exact match on 元善町468; 350m from Zenkoji main hall — well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 元善町 / Zenkoji Nakamise (beside the Niomon Gate)
seats_text: 30 seats across counter, table, and tatami-style seating; tables are closely spaced.
hours_raw: Summer 10:30–18:00 / Winter 10:30–16:00 (Tabelog lists 11:15 open); closed Wednesdays; hours may extend on festival days at Zenkoji.
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A local pilgrimage-town shop with no English menu or signage; staff are accustomed to pilgrimage visitors but communication will be in Japanese.
vegan_detail: The broth is described as a naturally sweet dashi — almost certainly katsuobushi (bonito flake) based, which is standard for Shinshu soba shops. Mountain vegetable toppings (sansai soba) are plant-based but arrive in fish broth. No plant-based dashi or vegan options are advertised. A vegan guest would need to ask specifically about the dashi before ordering.
gf_detail: Soba here is almost certainly nii-hachi style (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour) — the industry norm for this style of shop; no 100% buckwheat (juwari) claim is made. Udon is pure wheat. Tempura batter (on tenzaru and kakiage) contains wheat. The kitchen handles both soba and udon; one reviewer noted separate cooking pots for soba and udon, which suggests some awareness of cross-contact, but dedicated gluten-free protocols are not documented. Anyone with celiac disease should call ahead (026-233-2286) to ask about buckwheat ratio and shared fryer/pot practices.

bio.background: Nagatoya Shokudo has operated beside Zenkoji Temple's Niomon Gate since the Meiji era — the official site states the shop dates to 1888, making it a pilgrimage-town institution. It sits on the Nakamise approach that pilgrims have walked for centuries, and longtime locals recall the kakigori shaved ice being sold here in their grandparents' generation.
bio.specialty: Morisoba (cold zaru-style buckwheat noodles) and seasonal kakigori shaved ice made from block ice with hand-prepared syrups; mountain vegetable soba (sansai soba) is a year-round menu item.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated goal is to preserve '昔ながらの信州の味' — the traditional flavors of Shinshu — while welcoming both temple pilgrims and local repeat customers. The emphasis is on natural broth and unhurried, simple preparation.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture, Japan
roles: member of the Monzen Soba Association (gate-district soba cooperative)

anecdotes.text: A TripAdvisor reviewer noted that a family member recalls the kakigori being sold at Nagatoya 'even when grandmother was young,' pointing to a tradition stretching well beyond living memory.
The restaurant uses separate cooking pots for soba and udon, a practice at least one regular noted when asking about allergen management.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares both soba (likely nii-hachi — 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) and wheat udon; shared water and surfaces are probable. No dedicated gluten-free prep area or protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) is expected in the tsuyu dipping sauce and soup broth; no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Katsuobushi (bonito flake) dashi is strongly implied by the 'natural broth' description; plant-based broth is not offered. All soba and udon soups should be assumed to contain fish-derived dashi.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Separate cooking pots for soba and udon were noted by one reviewer, suggesting the kitchen has some allergen awareness. However, no formal allergy protocol or English-language allergen menu is documented. Guests with serious needs should phone ahead in Japanese.

positives.text: Separate pots used for soba and udon cooking, which reduces cross-contact between wheat-heavy udon water and soba.

caveats: Opening time discrepancy: the official site states 10:30, Tabelog and Hitosara list 11:15. Hours also shift between summer and winter seasons, and may extend on major Zenkoji festival days. Confirm by phone before visiting. The founding-year figure (1888) appears on the official site but is phrased loosely; treat as approximate. No English-language menu confirmed.

cuisine: 信州郷土料理・せいろ蒸し (Shinshu Regional Cuisine, Seiro-Mushi)
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町503
geocode_note: Coordinates from geocoding.jp for 〒380-0841 長野県長野市長野大門町; approximately 0.6km southwest of Zenkoji, well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 善光寺下駅周辺・門前町 (Zenkōji Gate Area, Daimon-chō)
seats_text: 60 seats total: 35 tatami seats and 25 chair seats including counter seating; private rooms for groups of 2–25.
hours_raw: 11:30–14:00 (LO 13:30) / 17:00–20:30 (LO 20:00); closed Tuesdays, 2nd Wednesday, 3rd Monday
closed_days: 火曜日・第2水曜日・第3月曜日 (Tuesdays, 2nd Wednesday, 3rd Monday)
cultural_comfort_note: English menus are available and English-speaking staff are on hand, making this a comfortable stop for international visitors to Zenkoji.
vegan_detail: The menu revolves around meat-based seiro-mushi (Shinshu beef, chicken, shrimp, eel) and fish dishes. The restaurant cites influence from Zenkoji's shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) tradition and aims for healthy, plant-forward preparations — so vegetable-only steamer options may be negotiable — but no vegan dishes are listed explicitly. Dashi base is almost certainly katsuobushi (bonito) in broth and dipping sauces. Call ahead if plant-based eating is a priority.
gf_detail: No gluten-free labeling anywhere on the menu. Steamed udon is a menu item (wheat). The ponzu and dipping sauces almost certainly contain conventional soy sauce (wheat). Soba miso thin pancake (そば味噌うす焼き) contains soba which may be processed alongside wheat. The restaurant notes allergen-friendly consultation is available upon request, but dedicated GF preparation is not confirmed. Celiacs should call in advance — the core steaming format is otherwise compatible if sauce substitutions are possible.

bio.background: 門前茶寮彌生座 opened in December 1995 as the dining arm of 武井工芸店 (Takei Kōgei-ten), a local crafts merchant, inside a lovingly restored 170-year-old machiya that had previously served as 中島屋惣兵衛・中惣商店, a historic tatami wholesaler. The building's soaring ceilings, heavy timber beams, earthen-floor corridor (doma), and antique staircase tansu were preserved as the centrepiece of the dining room, giving guests a sense of stepping into Edo-era merchant life just steps from Zenkoji's gate.
bio.origin: 長野市大門町503, 善光寺表参道 (Zenkoji Omotesando, Nagano City)
bio.specialty: Yama no Seiro-mushi (山のせいろ蒸し) — a 30cm square steamer crafted from Kiso-sawara cypress, packed with seasonal Shinshu vegetables and Shinshu beef, cooked at the table in a style the kitchen calls 'mushi-shabu-shabu.' The format draws on steaming techniques described as rooted in Yayoi-period culinary tradition.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen pursues 'slow food, slow life' — sourcing seasonal, locally grown Shinshu ingredients and weaving together three traditions: regional home cooking, Zenkoji Temple's shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) sensibility, and creative Japanese cuisine. The goal is healthy, unfussy food that honours the land.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name carries two layers of meaning: 'Yayoi' honours the name of the wife of Honda Zenkoji, the founder of Zenkoji Temple, linking the establishment symbolically to the temple's origins; 'Za' (座) evokes a gathering place — a circle of people meeting, talking, and sharing food.

The 170-year-old building had been a tatami wholesale shop (中惣商店) before being rescued and repurposed. Its structural features — thick beams, high ceilings, and an earthen corridor — were retained intact, creating a dining atmosphere described by visitors as 'nostalgic countryside charm' in the heart of the temple gate district.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls is publicly available. Steamed udon (wheat) and soba items are prepared in the same kitchen as all other dishes, making inadvertent gluten exposure a real risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping sauces and ponzu for the seiro-mushi almost certainly use standard soy sauce (containing wheat). No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce option is mentioned anywhere on the menu or website.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen simultaneously prepares Shinshu beef, chicken, shrimp, and eel dishes alongside any vegetable preparations, so animal-product cross-contact during steaming cannot be ruled out without direct confirmation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The Nagano tourism board listing notes that 'allergen-friendly options are available' and 'religious dietary requirements can be accommodated upon consultation,' suggesting staff are willing to discuss needs if contacted in advance.

positives.text: Steaming is inherently a low-fat, low-additive cooking method that preserves natural ingredient flavours without deep frying — the core preparation is free of heavy sauces or coatings if dipping sauces are set aside.
The restaurant emphasises seasonal, locally sourced Shinshu produce and explicitly draws on Zenkoji's shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) culinary tradition as one of its guiding philosophies.

caveats: Hours have an irregular closure pattern (Tuesdays plus rotating Wednesday and Monday closures) — always verify before visiting. The 3rd Monday closure was confirmed across multiple sources but the 2nd Wednesday closure appeared in some sources without the 3rd Monday — call ahead. GF and vegan assessments are based on publicly available menu and description data only; no first-hand allergy inquiry was made.

cuisine: 信州味噌・郷土料理 (Shinshu Miso, Local Specialties)
address_ja: 〒380-0845 長野県長野市西後町625
geocode_note: GSI geocoder did not resolve 西後町 directly; coordinates derived from MapFan and NAVITIME chō-level centroid for 長野市西後町 (36.6523, 138.1856). Spot is ~1.03km from Zenkō-ji, within the 1.61km target radius. Address is within the broader 善光寺門前 gate-town district.
neighborhood: 善光寺門前 (Zenkoji Monzen)
seats_text: 17 seats across counter (9) and table seating (8); intimate space fills quickly at lunch
cultural_comfort_note: A local miso-specialist lunch counter with no stated English-language support; primarily serves Japanese visitors and regulars in a traditional merchant-house setting.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around traditional miso-based Japanese cooking. Dashi in miso soup and sauces almost certainly contains katsuobushi (bonito) or niboshi (dried sardine). Signature dishes include dengaku (grilled konnyaku/tofu with miso), grilled rice balls, and miso-marinated fish and pork — the vast majority are not vegan. No plant-based or vegan menu is offered. The miso paste itself is vegan (soybeans, rice, salt), but its use in cooked dishes with animal-based broths makes verification essential; ask in advance.
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims or allergen menu found. Shinshu miso is traditionally made from soybeans and rice (potentially wheat-free), but the kitchen uses regular soy sauce (醤油) which contains wheat, and wheat-based dishes such as miso udon are on the menu. Cross-contamination from udon and other wheat items in a small kitchen is a serious concern. Dedicated GF fryer and allergen controls are not documented. Celiacs should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.

bio.background: Founded in Meiji 35 (1902) by the Suzukame family at the gate of Zenkō-ji Temple, the business has been making Shinshu miso for over 120 years across three generations. The white-walled merchant-house building with its large indigo noren curtain is a landmark of the Zenkoji monzen district. What began as a miso brewery evolved into a full miso-specialist merchant, eventually adding the 食味処 dining room so visitors could eat the miso rather than just take it home.
bio.origin: Nagano, Japan
bio.specialty: 焼きむすび籠 (grilled rice ball basket) — hand-shaped rice balls brushed with house miso and grilled until lightly charred, served with three rotating seasonal miso soups with free refills, house pickles, and small dishes
bio.philosophy: The company describes its mission as 'みそを食す人の幸せを願った120余年' — 120-plus years devoted to the happiness of those who eat miso. Management pursues a philosophy of quality over volume: carefully selected domestic soybeans, rice, and salt, traditionally fermented, used across every dish in the restaurant.

anecdotes.text: The grilled rice balls sell out every day — the kitchen prepares only what the morning allows, and once they're gone, they're gone. Lunch regulars know to arrive at opening.
The miso soft-serve ice cream, originally a bold experiment pairing fermented miso with frozen dessert, became a crowd favourite and earned the shop recognition as a top dessert spot in Nagano.

A visiting blogger noted the monthly-rotating miso soup — the day they visited it contained pumpkin, shiitake mushrooms, and cheese alongside the signature house Kogane miso — as emblematic of the restaurant's willingness to experiment within tradition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Miso udon is a core menu item served in the same small kitchen (approximately 17 seats). Wheat contamination risk from shared prep surfaces and cookware is unverified and undocumented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere on the official site or in any reviews. Standard Japanese soy sauce (醤油, containing wheat) is the expected seasoning for grilled rice balls, dengaku glaze, and marinades.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu includes miso-marinated fish (銀だら定食, silver cod), miso-braised pork, and egg dishes alongside any plant-forward options; shared cookware contact is undisclosed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy handling policy, or staff allergy training is mentioned on the official website, Tabelog, or any review source found.

positives.text: The core product — Shinshu miso — is traditionally made from soybeans, rice, and salt with no wheat in the paste itself, which is a positive baseline if wheat-free soy sauce were used. However, this has not been confirmed for the kitchen's cooking.

caveats: The 食味処 dining room is at the main store (本店) in 西後町625, not on Nakamise-dori itself — the Nakamise-adjacent location is a grab-and-go stall (善光寺店/仲見世店) without table dining. The Tabelog listing (ID 20000288) correctly maps to the main store restaurant. Coordinates are chō-level centroid (~1.03km from Zenkō-ji), within the 1.61km radius. Hours vary seasonally; always confirm before visiting. Cash-only status unconfirmed — cards (VISA/JCB/AMEX/Diners) were listed on the Tabelog page.

cuisine: 信州蕎麦（Shinshu Soba）
address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町482
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 長野県長野市元善町482. Approx 1.59 km from Zenkoji (36.6614, 138.1872) — within the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 仲見世通り、善光寺下駅周辺
seats_text: 90 seats on the second floor soba restaurant; large groups and tour buses accommodated
hours_raw: 蕎麦処（2F）10:30〜15:00（売り切れ次第終了）。1F土産処・おやき 9:00〜17:00。年中無休（不定休あり）。
closed_days: 不定休（irregular holidays; generally open year-round）
cultural_comfort_note: English-speaking staff confirmed; the shop is a well-trafficked tourist spot on the Zenkoji Nakamise arcade and accustomed to international visitors.
vegan_detail: The soba tsuyu (broth/dipping sauce) is confirmed to be made from katsuobushi (bonito), sodabushi (striped jack mackerel), and sabobushi (mackerel), alongside kombu from Rishiri, Rausu, and Hidaka. There is no vegan broth option listed. Oyaki fillings include vegetables and Shinshu miso but are served in the same kitchen. No vegan accommodations are noted anywhere.
gf_detail: The soba is niko-hachi style (二八蕎麦 — 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour), so the noodles themselves contain gluten. The tsuyu/dipping sauce uses soy sauce, which typically contains wheat. Tempura (wheat batter) is a featured menu item. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu, no mention of allergen accommodation beyond general consultation, and cross-contamination risk is high in an active soba kitchen that handles wheat flour constantly.

bio.background: Founded in Taisho 7 (1918), Maruhachi Takiya has occupied a prime spot on the Zenkoji Nakamise shopping arcade for over a century, serving pilgrims and visitors to one of Japan's most revered temples. The business spans two floors: a ground-floor souvenir shop selling hand-made oyaki, kinako mochi, hachimi togarashi, and soba mochi — a tea-ceremony sweet the shop revived from historical recipes — and a second-floor soba restaurant where skilled craftsmen hand-pull noodles daily.
bio.origin: 長野市元善町、善光寺仲見世通り
bio.specialty: Hand-pulled niko-hachi soba made from contracted North Shinano buckwheat (Shinano Town, Takayama Village, Iiyama), stone-milled and cut in the Togakushi tradition. Paired with a slightly sweet tsuyu built from Rishiri, Rausu, and Hidaka kombu plus katsuobushi, sodabushi, and sabobushi.
bio.philosophy: No preservatives or food additives are used anywhere in the shop. Buckwheat is sourced exclusively from contracted farms in northern Nagano Prefecture and stone-milled in-house, reflecting a commitment to traceable, additive-free ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The shop revived soba mochi — a confection historically served at tea ceremonies — using archival recipes, making it one of the few places in Nagano where this preparation is still available.
Oyaki here are hand-made one by one by local women using locally sourced vegetables and Shinshu miso, then steamed to produce a plump, chewy skin — a living snapshot of North Shinano home cooking.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The soba restaurant is an active niko-hachi (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour) kitchen. Wheat flour is handled continuously throughout service, making cross-contamination unavoidable for celiac or wheat-allergic diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu (dipping sauce and hot broth) is made with soy sauce, which in standard Japanese production contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Katsuobushi (bonito), sodabushi (striped jack mackerel), and sabobushi (mackerel) are used in the soba tsuyu. Tempura is a menu centerpiece. No plant-based broth option is offered.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The Nagano Tourism Board listing notes that 'allergy-friendly options and religious dietary requirements are available upon consultation' — but this appears to reflect general tourist-facing language rather than a documented allergy protocol.

positives.text: No preservatives or food additives (防腐剤など添加物不使用) are used in any products, including the noodles and tsuyu ingredients.
Buckwheat is sourced exclusively from named contracted farms in Nagano Prefecture and stone-milled, providing full ingredient traceability for the noodles themselves.

caveats: Soba restaurant hours (10:30-15:00) are subject to early closure when soba sells out — arrive by midday. The 1F shop stays open 9:00-17:00. Closed days listed as 不定休 (irregular); confirm by phone before visiting. The geocode resolves to the 元善町 block level; the shop's actual entrance faces the Nakamise arcade.

cuisine: 精進料理 (Shōjin Ryōri)
address_ja: 〒380-0851 長野県長野市元善町463
neighborhood: 善光寺門前町 (Zenkō-ji Monzenchō)
geocode_note: Coordinates from geocoding.jp for 長野県長野市元善町463; approximately 300m south-southwest of Zenkō-ji main hall, well within the 1.61km target radius.
seats_text: Up to 140 total across private tatami rooms and a large hall; chair seating with elevated trays available on request. Private room use standard for meal guests.
hours_raw: Primarily a temple lodging; meals served to overnight guests (dinner and breakfast). Non-staying guests may book lunch/dinner with at least 3 days advance reservation, minimum 2 people. No walk-in dining. Specific daily hours not published — confirm by phone.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closures published; availability depends on lodging bookings and advance meal reservations.
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Buddhist temple lodging with formal tatami rooms; English support is not advertised, and interaction with the keeper (若女将) is conducted in Japanese — a Japanese-speaking companion or phoning ahead in Japanese is strongly recommended.
vegan_detail: Shōjin ryōri by doctrine excludes all meat and fish. Konokonbou explicitly states its cuisine uses no meat or fish, relying on vegetables, grains, mushrooms, and seaweed. Dashi is described as "精進だし" (vegetarian/shōjin dashi), typically kombu- or shiitake-based rather than katsuobushi. However, because specific broth recipes are not published and no strict vegan certification exists, hidden bonito or fish-based seasoning cannot be ruled out with certainty — vegans should confirm the dashi base when reserving.
gf_detail: Shōjin ryōri is not inherently gluten-free. Wheat is present in standard Japanese soy sauce (koikuchi shōyu) used in many preparations, and the soba course (hand-pulled buckwheat noodles) contains wheat unless otherwise specified. Tempura and fried preparations such as the signature mock-eel yuba dish may use wheat-based batter. No gluten-free menu or certification is mentioned anywhere on the official site or Zenkoji's own listings. Celiacs should contact the establishment (026-234-6677) at least a week before visiting to discuss ingredients; modification may be possible given the made-to-order nature of the cuisine, but cannot be assumed.

bio.background: Konokonbou is a shukubo (temple lodging) on the main approach to Zenkō-ji, one of Japan's most visited Buddhist temples. The establishment traces its historical role to 'konokoube,' a traditional administrative position it held during Zenkō-ji's 七年に一度 (once-in-seven-years) Maenastan ceremony. Roughly 50 years ago, Konokonbou became the first shukubo at Zenkō-ji to formally offer shōjin ryōri as a restaurant-style service to non-lodging guests.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture; Zenkō-ji temple precinct
bio.specialty: Unagi Yuba — a mock-eel creation fashioned from yuba (tofu skin), tofu, and nori, pan-glazed to mimic the appearance and umami of grilled eel. Also celebrated for hand-pulled buckwheat soba served with roasted miso and walnut, and 'Hohan' (法飯), a tea-poured rice dish topped with five condiments and kombu broth.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen follows the shōjin principle of 'using the life of vegetables without waste' — a cooking ethic of gratitude and mindfulness. The signature multi-course dinner is named 'Muryōju' (無量寿, boundless life), reflecting the temple's dedication to Amida Buddha.
roles: Buddhist experience host (朝のお勤め, sutra copying)

anecdotes.text: A 2012 diner described being welcomed at the entrance by staff and guided to a private tatami room with the tray already set — the proprietor then personally explained each dish before serving: 'Simple yet heartfelt cooking, with modest portions per item but abundant courses that satisfy both appetite and spirit.'

The Go Nagano prefectural tourism site credits Konokonbou's keeper with pioneering the formal shōjin meal service at Zenkō-ji's shukubo district approximately five decades ago, a tradition now carried on by the current young proprietress.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares traditional Japanese multi-course cuisine including battered and fried dishes (the mock-eel yuba is described as deep-fried). Cross-contamination risk from shared frying oil with wheat-battered items is unknown — no dedicated GF fryer is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shōjin ryōri preparations typically use standard koikuchi soy sauce, which contains wheat. The establishment does not advertise tamari or wheat-free soy sauce. Wheat in soy sauce is a likely but unconfirmed risk.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi used is described as '精進だし' (shōjin dashi), indicating intent to avoid fish-based stock. However, the specific ingredients of the dashi are not published and cannot be independently confirmed as free of katsuobushi (bonito flakes).

staff_allergy_handling.text: Meals are made to order with advance reservations required (minimum 3 days for non-guests). This advance-notice structure offers an opportunity to discuss dietary needs directly with the kitchen at booking time — call 026-234-6677.

positives.text: Shōjin ryōri is structurally free of all meat, poultry, and fish by doctrine — making it a strong starting point for those avoiding animal proteins.

The cuisine is built around seasonal Nagano vegetables, tofu, yuba, mushrooms, and seaweed — many naturally gluten-light ingredients. The make-to-order, reservation-only format means the kitchen can potentially accommodate specific requests if communicated in advance.

caveats: Hours_week shown as all "closed" because this is a reservation-only establishment with no published daily open hours — it operates on an advance-booking model, not regular restaurant hours. Prospective diners (non-guests) must reserve at least 3 days ahead by phone (026-234-6677) and meet a minimum party of 2. GF confidence is "ask" rather than higher because shōjin ryōri inherently uses wheat-containing soy sauce and the soba course contains wheat; no GF menu exists. All safety assessments are inferred from cuisine type and published ingredients — no direct staff statements on allergen handling were found.

cuisine: 蕎麦 (Kuichi Soba — 90% buckwheat)
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字南長野西後町1637-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 長野県長野市大字南長野西後町１６３７番地 → [138.187119, 36.654221]. Approx 800m south of Zenkoji center — well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 善光寺参道 (Zenkoji Sando)
gf_detail: Kuichi soba is 90% buckwheat with a 10% binder that is almost certainly wheat flour — not declared gluten-free. Standard tsuyu (dipping broth) at Japanese soba restaurants contains wheat-based soy sauce. Tempura items (天ざるそば) use wheat batter. No dedicated GF preparation or allergen declaration found on any source. Celiac diners should not assume safety and must contact the restaurant directly.
