vegan_detail: Traditional Japanese soba tsuyu is made with katsuobushi (bonito flake) dashi — almost certainly the case here. No source confirms a kombu-only or plant-based broth option. Mountain vegetable soba (山菜そば) and soba-leaf seiro are on the menu but dashi composition is unconfirmed. Vegan diners must ask about the broth base before ordering.
hours_raw: 月・水〜金 11:00–19:00、土日祝 11:00–18:00、火曜定休。材料切れ次第閉店あり。※一部資料では平日を11:00–14:30 / 17:30–20:30の二部制と記載。
closed_days: 火曜日 (Tuesday)
seats_text: Seat count not published. The space includes a small raised tatami seating area and is described as private-room-capable for 4–18 people.
cultural_comfort_note: No English-speaking staff, but an English menu is available; a basic greeting in Japanese will help.

bio.background: Founded in Meiji 23 (1890) along the Zenkoji temple approach, Imamura Soba Honten has served pilgrims and locals for over 130 years. The shop trades under the kuichi (九一) banner, referring to soba made with 90% Shinshu-grown buckwheat and only 10% binder — a stricter-than-average ratio that preserves the grain's earthy aroma.
bio.specialty: 御膳そば (Gozensoba) — a composed cold soba presentation meant to showcase the noodle's natural flavor and silky mouthfeel; also そばの葉せいろ, made with a dough incorporating soba leaf and served with walnut dipping sauce.
bio.philosophy: 挽きたて・打ちたて・茹でたて — freshly stone-ground, freshly rolled, freshly boiled. The shop uses stone-milled Shinshu buckwheat and limits the binder to below 10%, keeping faith with a formula unchanged since the Meiji era.
bio.origin: 長野市西後町 (Nagano City, Nishi-Ushiro-machi), on the historic Zenkoji pilgrimage road

anecdotes.text: The dining room walls display calligraphy by the manga artist Fujio Akatsuka (creator of Osomatsu-kun) alongside period photographs, giving the space the feel of a living archive of the Showa era.
The shop publishes a small booklet, 御膳そばのお召し上がり方, explaining the correct way to enjoy Gozensoba — a detail that reflects the house's pride in ritual and craft.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen or cross-contamination information is published. The kitchen prepares tempura (wheat batter) alongside soba, making shared-fryer cross-contact plausible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsuyu (dipping sauce) at traditional Japanese soba restaurants is made with wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu). No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu includes chicken (Shinshu Golden Shamo), duck (鴨せいろ), and tempura — animal products are central to the kitchen, making vegan cross-contact likely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The Nagano City tourism board notes that the restaurant can 'consider religious dietary requirements upon consultation' and provides an English menu, suggesting some willingness to discuss dietary needs — though no English-speaking staff are confirmed.

positives.text: The soba itself is 90% buckwheat (kuichi ratio), meaning it contains less wheat binder than standard soba — a marginal benefit for wheat-sensitivity (not celiac safety).

caveats: Hours conflict across sources: Tabelog lists continuous service (11:00–19:00 weekdays, 11:00–18:00 weekends), while nagano-cvb lists a split shift (11:00–14:30 / 17:30–20:30 weekdays). Confirm by phone before visiting. Founding year listed as Meiji 23 (1890) by most sources; one source incorrectly states 1868. The official website (kuichi.co.jp) was unreachable during research.

cuisine: 郷土料理・おやき (Oyaki, Local Shinshu Pastries)
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町53 ぱてぃお大門 蔵楽庭内
geocode_note: Coordinates from Mapple spot page (spot ID 20012131). Target brief listed 大門町56-1 but all primary sources confirm the address as 大門町53 inside Patio Daimon (Kurakutei). Distance from Zenko-ji (36.6614, 138.1872) is approximately 630m — well within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 大門町・善光寺門前 (Daimon-cho, Zenko-ji Monzen)
closed_days: 水曜日（祝日の場合は翌平日）
seats_text: Seating around a traditional irori hearth; capacity not disclosed publicly. Walk-ins welcome; reservations recommended for the oyaki-making experience.
cultural_comfort_note: Staff are warm local grandmothers and senior craftspeople; the experience is welcoming to visitors and product photos assist ordering, but menus and signage are entirely in Japanese with minimal English.
gf_detail: Oyaki dough is made with domestic wheat flour (小麦粉) — wheat is listed as a primary allergen on the company's allergen declaration alongside soybeans, sesame, dairy, pork, and apple. There is no gluten-free alternative dough offered. Not suitable for celiac or wheat-allergic diners.
vegan_detail: Standard fillings (nozawana/pickled mustard greens, azuki red bean, pumpkin, mushroom-vegetable) appear plant-based, but the nozawana filling is seasoned with house-made miso and soy sauce — both of which typically contain wheat and may contain fermented fish byproducts depending on the brand. No explicit vegan certification or animal-free declaration exists. The dough itself contains no eggs or dairy per available information, but the wheat flour base is a firm barrier for gluten-sensitive vegans. Vegan travelers who tolerate wheat should ask staff about miso and soy sauce sources before ordering.

bio.background: Founded on May 10, 1986, in Ogawa Village (小川村) in the Northern Alps foothills of Nagano Prefecture by Ichiro Gonda and six members of the Kodama-kai — a mutual-aid society formed in the 1950s by young villagers who had left to study and work, pledging to return and revitalise their depopulating home. The Daimon branch brings that village spirit to the heart of Nagano City, five minutes on foot from Zenko-ji Temple, inside the historic Patio Daimon complex.
bio.origin: Ogawa Village (小川村), Nagano Prefecture — now with city outposts along the Zenko-ji approach
bio.specialty: Jomon oyaki (縄文おやき) — hand-shaped wheat-dough pastries filled with seasonal local vegetables and grilled over a live-coal irori hearth
bio.philosophy: The founders believed the quality of an oyaki is determined entirely by the vegetables inside it, and that vegetables are their own supreme seasoning. The company sources from small farms in Ogawa Village and the Nobeyana plateau, prioritises minimal pesticide use, and has built its entire business model on hiring workers from age 60 with no upper retirement limit.

anecdotes.text: When the original Oyaki Village opened in November 1986, it attracted immediate national media attention for its innovative model of putting elderly grandmothers to work as the primary production force — a deliberate response to rural depopulation that earned the company recognition as a national model for senior employment.

After founder Ichiro Gonda's death in 2006, Abbot Tamaizumi of Senso-ji Temple composed the 'Five Precepts of Oyaki' to preserve his philosophy: the first precept holds that a good oyaki, like a good person, values inner substance over outward appearance.

Oyaki-making at the Daimon branch uses a large earthenware pan (horoku 焙烙) that has been in continuous use since the shop opened. Visitors shape the dough, fold in the filling, and sear it on the pan before slow-roasting over embers — a process that replicates the cooking method traced archaeologically to the Jomon-period site at Ikadagahara in Ogawa Village.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is based on wheat-flour dough. Wheat is listed as a primary declared allergen. There is no separate preparation area or alternative dough for gluten-free orders. Cross-contamination risk is inherent and unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The nozawana filling is seasoned with house-made miso and soy sauce (醤油). Standard Japanese soy sauce and many miso varieties contain wheat as a fermentation ingredient. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork and dairy are listed as declared allergens on the company's allergen sheet, suggesting some product variants contain these ingredients. Shared irori grilling surface and preparation areas mean contact with non-vegan items cannot be excluded.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No documented allergy protocol or staff allergy-handling procedure found in public sources. The experience-oriented format (guests cook alongside staff) may allow for some dialogue, but formal allergen accommodation is not offered.

positives.text: Standard fillings — nozawana greens, azuki red bean, pumpkin, mushroom-vegetable mix — are vegetable-based with no meat. No fish or meat ingredients are documented in the core lineup.
The company explicitly avoids preservatives and artificial colourings, sourcing from low-pesticide, small-scale local farms.

caveats: Address in brief (大門町56-1) differs slightly from address confirmed in all primary sources (大門町53, inside Patio Daimon / Kurakutei). Same facility confirmed by phone number and description. Hours vary slightly by source (some list 9:00–17:00, Mapple lists 10:00–17:00); 9:00–17:00 is the most consistently cited and matches the official CVB listing. Cash-only status is uncertain: credit cards are reportedly not accepted but QR-code payments (PayPay etc.) appear to be accepted per the Tabelog listing. Oyaki-making experience pricing also varies by source (¥540–¥1,200); contact shop for current pricing.

lead: 根元 八幡屋礒五郎 本店
name_ja: 根元 八幡屋礒五郎 本店
cuisine: 七味唐辛子専門店・カフェ (Shichimi Spice Shop & Café)
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町83
neighborhood: 善光寺表参道 / 大門町 (Zenkoji Omotesando, Daimoncho)
geocode_note: Coordinates from Mapion POI data (36.65727686, 138.18766272). Approximately 0.46 km south of Zenkoji main hall — well within 1.61 km radius on the main temple approach road.
seats_text: 26 seats in the attached Yokocho Cafe; the main spice shop is retail/takeaway only
hours_raw: Main store: 9:00–18:30 daily (無休). Yokocho Cafe: 10:00–17:00 (last order 16:30), daily (no regular holidays; reservations not taken during Golden Week, Obon, New Year 1/1–8).
closed_days: No regular closing days (無休) for either the main store or the café.
cultural_comfort_note: Located on the main Zenkoji temple approach, a major tourist corridor; staff are accustomed to international visitors and the shop has English signage and an English Tabelog listing.
vegan_detail: The spice gelato is vegan (confirmed: all other desserts contain eggs, but the gelato does not). The green curry can reportedly be made without prawns on request, yielding a vegetarian option, but fish-based dashi or shrimp paste may still be present in base sauces — not confirmed vegan. Curries contain dairy (butter chicken, coconut milk). Most café food is omnivore. The spice products themselves (shichimi blends) are plant-based.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or certification. Curries likely use wheat-containing soy sauce or roux. Japanese shichimi spice blends are generally wheat-free, but cross-contamination in a food-service kitchen cannot be ruled out. Gelato ingredients not confirmed GF. Visitors with celiac disease should ask staff directly; communication may be limited to Japanese.

bio.background: Founded in 1736 (Genbun 1) by a merchant named Kanemon who began selling chili peppers on the grounds of Zenkoji Temple, Yawataya Isogoro is one of Japan's three great shichimi (seven-spice) makers, alongside Tokyo's Yagenbori and Kyoto's Nanayake. The business was passed down through generations of the family; by the third generation under Giemon, the shop had earned prime placement near the temple notice board. In 1924, the sixth-generation owner Eisuke invented the now-iconic tin can packaging that replaced glass bottles and wooden containers — a design still recognizable today. The current main store on Daimoncho was established in 1952, and a full renovation was completed in 2008.
bio.specialty: Custom-blended shichimi togarashi (seven-spice chili) — visitors choose from eight spice components and have their blend mixed to order. The attached Yokocho Cafe serves spice-forward dishes including curries made with proprietary spice blends, handmade spice gelato, and seasonal sweets.
bio.philosophy: From farm to plate (畑からお皿まで): Yokocho Cafe uses vegetables grown on the company's own agricultural fields alongside ingredients from Shinshu regional producers. Eight varieties of shichimi are placed at each cafe table so diners can explore flavor pairings.
bio.origin: Nagano, Nagano Prefecture (Zenkoji temple grounds, est. 1736)
roles: Spice Shop (280+ years, retail + custom blending)

anecdotes.text: The shop's name uses the unusual kanji '礒' (not the more common '磯'), a distinction the company actively preserves — it regularly distributes notices requesting correct spelling of '八幡屋礒五郎'.

In 1924, the sixth-generation owner invented the distinctive illustrated tin can that became the shop's signature packaging — replacing the glass bottles and wooden containers that had been standard until then.

The Yokocho Cafe celebrated its 10th anniversary in December 2023 with a limited Kerala-style chicken curry made from 10 anniversary spices, offered for just three days in quantities of 20–30 portions per day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or fryer confirmed. The café serves wheat-containing curries and baked goods (macarons, cheesecake, pancakes) alongside the gelato, raising cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Curries and savory dishes at Yokocho Cafe likely use standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned on any menu or source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The gelato is the only confirmed egg-free item; it is prepared in a kitchen that also handles dairy-based cheesecakes, butter chicken curry, and egg-containing baked goods. No vegan-only prep area is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff at the Yokocho Cafe confirmed to one visitor that the green curry can be modified to remove prawns for vegetarian guests, suggesting some willingness to accommodate requests — but no formal allergy protocol or English allergen menu has been documented.

positives.text: The spice gelato is confirmed to contain no eggs (unlike all other desserts on the menu), making it the clearest plant-based option for vegan visitors.
The shichimi spice blends sold in the retail shop are plant-based ingredients (chili, sesame, hemp, citrus peel, etc.) with no animal products.

caveats: The Tabelog record covers the main spice store (大門町83); the Yokocho Cafe is technically at 横町86-1 but is part of the same Yawataya Isogoro complex. Hours above are for the main store; the café operates 10:00–17:00. GF/vegan data is thin — no formal allergen documentation found; safety ratings are conservative. The 'gelato is vegan' claim comes from a single travel blog that received staff confirmation; independently verify before recommending to strict vegans.

lead: 甘味処 鎌倉 善光寺仲見世通り店
name_ja: 甘味処 鎌倉 善光寺仲見世通り店
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野元善町486
geocode_note: GSI address-search matched 長野県長野市大字長野元善町４８６番地; coordinates are within ~280m of Zenkō-ji, well inside the 1.61km target radius.
neighborhood: 元善町（Motozen-chō）、仲見世通り
cuisine: 甘味処・和スイーツ専門店（Traditional Japanese sweets — warabi mochi specialist）
vegan_detail: The core warabi mochi is made from bracken starch (hon-warabi-ko), kuromitsu (Okinawan brown sugar syrup), and kinako (roasted soy flour) — no animal products listed in core items, but the shop provides no vegan certification. Most beverages (warabi mochi drinks, matcha latte) contain dairy/milk. Ask staff to confirm kinako is the only topping and request no vanilla ice cream add-on. Hidden dashi is not a concern for sweets-only items, but cross-contact risk from shared equipment is unknown.
gf_detail: The core warabi mochi lists allergens as soy only (from kinako); no wheat appears in the core product's allergen declaration on the official product page. However, the brand publishes no gluten-free certification and does not address cross-contamination from shared equipment or surfaces. The "proprietary blend" added to hon-warabi-ko is undisclosed — composition unconfirmed. Some beverages (warabi mochi drinks with milk) list only milk and soy allergens. Shoyu and wheat-based dashi are not relevant to this sweets-only menu, but celiacs should call ahead to ask about the undisclosed blend and kitchen cross-contact before visiting.
closed_days: None (年中無休 — open year-round)
seats_text: Approximately 9 seats in a compact machiya-style interior
cultural_comfort_note: Located on the busy tourist approach to Zenkō-ji, so staff are accustomed to foreign visitors; basic order-pointing is sufficient, though menus are in Japanese only.

bio.background: A branch of the national warabi mochi chain 甘味処鎌倉, operated by 株式会社K&S (K&S Corporation, founded 2004, headquarters Niigata). The Zenkoji Nakamise branch opened on April 30, 2021, occupying a compact machiya-style space on the temple approach shopping street. The chain takes its name and brand story from the Kamakura period, when warabi mochi was refined under the influence of Zen Buddhism and tea ceremony culture brought from China; historically the sweet was reserved for people of high status due to the cost of warabi flour.
bio.origin: Niigata (corporate); Nagano branch opened 2021
bio.specialty: 鎌倉わらびもち — handmade daily from rare domestic hon-warabi-ko (true bracken-root starch) with the brand's proprietary blend, served with pure Okinawan brown-sugar kuromitsu and high-roast kinako. The chain sells approximately 5,000 pieces nationwide each day.
bio.philosophy: Minimal processing and ingredient purity: the kuromitsu uses only Okinawan brown sugar with no corn syrup or additives; the kinako is roasted at higher temperature and for longer than standard commercial varieties.
roles: chain branch of 甘味処鎌倉 (K&S Corporation)

anecdotes.text: Staff at the opening wore traditional kimono, and the interior features Kyoto-umbrella-inspired lighting with paper and bamboo elements, giving a quietly festive atmosphere inside a bustling tourist street.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No cross-contamination information is published by the brand. The 'proprietary blend' added to hon-warabi-ko is undisclosed, leaving open whether any wheat-containing starch is included. Celiacs should call 026-217-7515 before visiting.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce or wheat-based dashi is used in a sweets-only menu; the core warabi mochi allergen declaration lists soy (kinako) only — no wheat allergen declared.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vanilla ice cream topping and all milk-based warabi mochi drinks contain dairy; ordering plain warabi mochi with kuromitsu and kinako avoids dairy, but shared equipment risk is unknown.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocol is described on the official site or any fetched source; direct inquiry by phone or in person is necessary for celiac or severe soy-allergy guests.

positives.text: Core warabi mochi (hon-warabi-ko + kuromitsu + kinako) lists only soy as an allergen — no wheat allergen declared across the standard eat-in range, making it a notable wheat-free option among traditional Japanese sweets.
Kuromitsu is made from pure Okinawan brown sugar with no additives or corn syrup, and kinako is a single-ingredient roasted soybean powder — minimal ingredient lists reduce hidden-additive risk.

caveats: Payment: conflicting sources — the web-komachi opening article (2021) says PayPay only/no credit cards, while the Tabelog listing says cards accepted; situation may have changed since opening. Verify on arrival. Seat count varies across sources (7–9); 9 is from the most detailed listing (gorp.jp). The "proprietary blend" in the warabi mochi recipe is not disclosed, so GF status cannot be confirmed without calling ahead.

cuisine: Soba & sauce katsudon (tonkatsu)
address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町486
geocode_note: GSI returned chō-level coordinates for 長野県長野市大字長野元善町; street-level not resolved. Point is 108m from Zenkoji reference, well within radius.
neighborhood: 元善町（Motozen-chō）、善光寺仲見世通り
seats_text: Approximately 52–54 seats; tatami seating available on upper floor
hours_raw: 11:00～15:00、水曜定休（祝日の場合は営業）。複数の情報源により閉店時間に差異あり（15:00〜18:00）。直接確認推奨。
closed_days: Wednesday (open if Wednesday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: Located on the tourist-facing Nakamise-dori shopping street leading to Zenkoji; an English menu is available and staff are accustomed to foreign visitors, but ordering is still largely counter-style in Japanese.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around pork cutlet (tonkatsu) and soba served with animal-based toppings — duck (kamo), herring (nishin), tempura, and egg. Soba broth is almost certainly fish-based (katsuo dashi). There are no plant-based options documented anywhere, and the kitchen concept is fundamentally meat- and fish-forward.
gf_detail: Wheat is present throughout: pork cutlets are panko-breaded, tempura uses wheat batter, and soba — while buckwheat-based — is typically made with a wheat blend (juwari/100% soba not claimed) and served in broth made with wheat-containing soy sauce (standard shoyu). No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation area, and no allergen declarations are published. This restaurant is not safe for celiac diners.

bio.background: The family originally ran a restaurant called Tsubame near Tsubame Pond in Nagano. Wartime disruption forced them to lend the building — which later became the first schoolhouse for Seisen Women's Academy. After relinquishing that property, the family relocated to Motozen-cho on Nakamise-dori in Showa 29 (1954) and opened a tonkatsu shop, drawing on the father's cooking background. Roughly 50 years ago, inspired by a television drama featuring hand-cut soba, the restaurant expanded into buckwheat noodles. Soba has since become its equal partner to the cutlet.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nakamise-dori near Zenkoji Temple
bio.specialty: Sauce katsudon — breaded pork cutlet over rice, dressed with a sweet house sauce unchanged since founding — paired with stone-ground, hand-cut soba noodles
bio.philosophy: Consistency rooted in place: the same sweet sauce recipe, the same stone-ground buckwheat, and the same kura-style shopfront on the temple approach for over 70 years.

anecdotes.text: The family's original restaurant, Tsubame, was requisitioned during wartime and its building became the first schoolhouse for Seisen Women's Academy — a local historical footnote tied to the restaurant's lineage.

The decision to add hand-cut soba about 50 years ago came directly from the influence of a television drama — a small spontaneous pivot that has since defined the restaurant's identity as much as the tonkatsu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Panko-breaded tonkatsu and tempura are core menu items, meaning wheat flour and breadcrumbs are in active use throughout service. Cross-contamination with soba and rice dishes must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) containing wheat is used in soba broth and likely in all savory preparations. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Animal products — pork, duck, herring, egg, and fish-based dashi — are present in virtually every dish. There is no plant-only preparation pathway available.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy, allergen chart, or staff allergy communication procedure is documented on any platform reviewed. Diners with wheat allergy or serious dietary restrictions should call ahead: 026-232-5776.

caveats: Hours vary significantly across sources (Tabelog: 11:00-15:00; Rurubu: 11:00-15:30; Web-Komachi: 11:00-17:30; Gnavi: 11:00-18:00) — confirm directly before visiting. The official website marusei-soba.com returned 403 during research. Geocode is chō-level only (GSI could not resolve to street number); coordinates [138.186813, 36.66048] represent the block centroid, placing the restaurant 108m from Zenkoji, well within the 1.61km target radius. The nakamise.org owner profile page (primary source for history) had a TLS certificate error and could not be fetched directly — history details were extracted from search engine summaries of that page.

cuisine: Cafe / Cake shop
address_ja: 長野県長野市上西之門町608
geocode_note: Resolved via geocoding.jp for 長野県長野市上西之門町608. GSI AddressSearch API failed to match this chome-level address and returned unrelated results; geocoding.jp returned lat 36.659352 / lng 138.186431, which falls within the target box (lat 36.647–36.676, lng 138.169–138.205) and approximately 0.3 km from Zenkō-ji — well within the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 上西之門町（Kami-Nishi-no-Mon-chō）、善光寺下 (Zenkoji-shita)
vegan_detail: No vegan options confirmed. The signature item is a Gorgonzola cheesecake (dairy-heavy), and other offerings include butter-based tarts and roll cakes. No plant-based or dairy-free alternatives are mentioned in any source.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options offered. The shop explicitly sources wheat flour locally from Nagano Prefecture as a featured ingredient. All items — cheesecakes, tarts, quiches, roll cakes, chiffon cakes — contain wheat. No dedicated GF preparation, no GF menu items, and no allergen accommodations are mentioned in any source. Coeliacs should not eat here.
hours_raw: 11:00–19:00 (closes earlier when items sell out; dine-in only until 16:00, takeout only after 16:00); 不定休 (irregular closures)
closed_days: Irregular (不定休); check Instagram @haruhi20130215 for current schedule — owner operates on reduced/irregular hours while managing a possible future relocation
seats_text: 16 seats; mostly counter seating by the windows plus a handful of small tables. Dine-in limited to one hour. Solo diners welcome.
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu or signage noted in any source; the shop is a quietly tucked-away local spot on a back street near Zenkō-ji with no tourist-facing accommodations — basic Japanese or gesturing to displayed items will be necessary.
caveats: Operating status is uncertain: the owner's Instagram bio (as summarized by search results) states the shop is in irregular operation while preparing a possible relocation to Kyoto (the owner's mother's hometown). No formal closure notice appears on Tabelog as of research date, but hours are highly variable — confirm via Instagram before visiting. All baked goods contain wheat; no GF or vegan options exist.

bio.background: Tomoko Yazawa grew up baking at home, improvising with aluminum bento boxes as cake molds. She trained formally at culinary school and spent years in Tokyo making cakes for various establishments before deciding city life was too crowded and returning to her roots in Nagano.
bio.origin: Opened February 15, 2013, in a renovated old house on a quiet back street behind Zenkō-ji Temple. Yazawa spent roughly six months making wishes at Zenshoji Temple for the right property before a vacant-property broker introduced her to the current building — she reportedly knew the moment she saw the exterior: 'I saw myself opening the door and welcoming customers.'
bio.specialty: NY-style cheesecake made with Gorgonzola (¥480) — peppery, tangy, and precisely balanced. Seasonal offerings rotate with what is fresh: persimmon tarts in autumn, matcha gateau au chocolat in winter, housemade kakigori (shaved ice) in summer with creative toppings like salted okra or balsamic-black pepper.
bio.philosophy: Yazawa insists on letting ingredient quality speak for itself rather than layering on decoration. She makes all accompanying items — jams, syrups, cake bases — in-house. 'We cannot create something delicious from what farmers have carefully cultivated' is how she frames her sourcing approach. Wheat flour is sourced locally from Nagano Prefecture; fruit comes from direct-sale farm outlets.

anecdotes.text: Yazawa used aluminum bento boxes as improvised cake molds as a child — a detail that captures the scrappy, self-taught spirit she carried into her professional training and eventually into her own shop.

After about six months of regular visits to Zenshoji Temple praying for the right property, she was introduced to the building that became Haruhi by a vacant-property specialist. Her immediate reaction upon seeing the exterior was a vision of herself greeting customers at the door.
The shop requires customers to order a cake alongside any beverage — a policy the owner enforces to keep the focus on the baking, not just coffee.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No GF items exist; the entire menu is wheat-based. Cross-contamination risk is high by default in a small kitchen producing wheat-flour cakes, tarts, and quiches.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All items contain dairy (butter, cream, cheese). No plant-based preparation space exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen handling policies or staff training information is mentioned in any available source. The shop has no menu labeling for allergens.

name_ja: 手打ちそば屋 かんだた
cuisine: 手打ちそば (Hand-pulled buckwheat soba)
address_ja: 長野県長野市鶴賀権堂町2320
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch first-feature coordinates for 長野県長野市鶴賀権堂町2320. Approx 963m south of Zenkoji (36.6614,138.1872), well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 権堂町 (Gondo-cho), Nagano City — alley off Gondo Shopping Street, ~12 min walk from Zenkoji
seats_text: 22 seats across two floors: counter seating on the ground floor (welcoming solo diners) and tatami-mat seating upstairs.
hours_raw: 月火木金土日 11:30〜14:30（LO14:00）／定休：水曜・第1第3火曜（祝日は営業）
closed_days: 水曜・第1・第3火曜（祝日は営業）
cultural_comfort_note: An English picture menu is available, but ordering and any dietary conversation requires basic Japanese or patience with gestures; staff are described as warm and kind.
vegan_detail: The dashi (broth) is made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes), kombu, and soda-gatsuo (striped bonito). No vegan broth alternative is mentioned anywhere. Side dishes include duck (鴨ネギそば) and other non-vegan items. The restaurant does use pesticide-free seasonal vegetables grown by the owner, but the core tsuyu is fish-based and unsuitable for vegans.
gf_detail: Soba noodles are hand-made from a custom-blended domestic stone-ground buckwheat flour — the blend ratio is not published, so wheat contamination in the noodles themselves cannot be ruled out (most Japanese soba is 80:20 buckwheat:wheat). The tsuyu (dipping broth) uses soy sauce from Shioya Brewing in Suzaka, a traditional wheat-brewed soy sauce. No tempura is served (fryer cross-contamination not a concern), but shared preparation surfaces and the wheat soy sauce in the broth make this unsuitable for celiac diners without direct inquiry. Those with mild sensitivity should call ahead.

bio.background: A husband-and-wife soba shop tucked into a narrow alley behind Gondo Shopping Street, a short walk south of Zenkoji Temple. The restaurant occupies a converted old warehouse with a retro interior — an antique clock that chimes at one o'clock sets the atmosphere.
bio.specialty: Thin, delicate hand-pulled soba — the house style is described as 'hosoku, karuku, ikina' (thin, light, refined). The signature Gokuraku Set pairs the soba with 'su-gomori' (lightly fried soba nests) and soba inari. No tempura is offered so that the buckwheat noodles remain the undivided focus.
bio.philosophy: The name かんだた comes from Akutagawa Ryunosuke's short story 'Kumo no Ito' (The Spider's Thread) — the spider's silk thread being an image of something thin and easily broken, just like fine soba. The owner draws the same parallel: follow the thread to the end and you reach paradise (gokuraku). Soba flour is a specially blended domestic stone-ground variety; the tsuyu is made entirely from natural ingredients with no added sugar, using soy sauce from Shioya Brewing, an heirloom-method producer in Suzaka city. Pesticide-free vegetables are grown by the owner and appear as seasonal side dishes.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture

anecdotes.text: The restaurant name references Akutagawa's 'Kumo no Ito': just as the spider's thread is thin and fragile, so is fine soba — but following it leads to paradise (gokuraku), the very word used for the restaurant's signature set meal.
Staff have been observed offering umbrellas to guests leaving in the rain, a small gesture reviewers cite as emblematic of the warm hospitality.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba flour blend ratio is not disclosed; most Japanese soba shops use an 80:20 buckwheat-to-wheat blend. Without confirmation of juuwari (100% buckwheat) noodles, wheat cross-contamination in the noodles must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu is made with soy sauce from Shioya Brewing (塩屋醸造) in Suzaka — a traditional wheat-brewed soy sauce. Wheat is present in all broth-based dishes.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi is made from katsuobushi (bonito), kombu, and soda-gatsuo. Fish-based broth is used throughout; no plant-based broth alternative is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocols or allergen menus are mentioned in any source reviewed. An English picture menu exists for ordering, but allergy-specific communication would require Japanese or direct phone inquiry (026-232-3718).

positives.text: No tempura is served, eliminating the most common fryer cross-contamination risk at soba shops.
Tsuyu is made with no added sugar and all-natural ingredients; vegetables are pesticide-free and grown by the owner.

caveats: Hours follow an irregular Tuesday closure pattern (1st and 3rd Tuesdays closed; other Tuesdays open); confirm before visiting. The official site (kandata.jp) uses a self-signed certificate and could not be fetched directly — information was cross-referenced from Tabelog, Gnavi, Web-Komachi, and Tripadvisor. Seat count varies across sources (17–22); Tabelog figure of 22 used. Soba flour blend ratio undisclosed; juuwari (100% buckwheat) status unconfirmed.

cuisine: Shojin ryori (Buddhist vegetarian)
address_ja: 〒380-0851 長野県長野市元善町463
geocode_note: GSI address-search API returned city-level coordinates only for 元善町463. Coordinates set to Zenkoji temple precinct centroid (36.6614, 138.1872) — the shukubo is on the Omotesando approach immediately adjacent to the main hall, well within the target radius.
neighborhood: 善光寺表参道 (Zenkoji Omotesando)
vegan_detail: Authentic shojin ryori: strictly no meat, fish, eggs, or animal products. Dashi is vegetarian (kombu/vegetable-based). Dishes include sesame tofu, yuba, seasonal vegetables, hand-pulled soba, and the signature mock-eel unagi yuba. This is by definition a fully plant-based cuisine tradition, though standard shoyu (wheat-containing soy sauce) is almost certainly used in cooking.
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims made anywhere on official site or in any reviews. Standard Japanese shojin ryori uses wheat-containing shoyu (soy sauce), and miso may contain wheat. The hand-pulled soba noodles are a featured course dish. No dedicated GF menu or allergen accommodation procedures are disclosed. Celiac diners should call ahead (026-234-6677) to discuss; cross-contamination risk from soba must be assumed. Not recommended for celiac without prior explicit confirmation from the kitchen.
hours_raw: Dinner from 18:00; breakfast for overnight guests; lunch available by reservation. Specific lunch hours require consultation at time of booking. Check-in 15:00-17:30, check-out by 10:00.
closed_days: No fixed closed days published; confirm when booking
seats_text: Up to 160 dining guests; 12 Japanese-style guest rooms for overnight stays; private tatami rooms available for meals
cultural_comfort_note: Traditional temple lodging with minimal English presence; reservation by phone (Japanese) or online form; no reports of English-speaking staff, though the meal experience itself is wordless and immersive.
caveats: Hours and closed days are not publicly posted and must be confirmed at time of reservation. Lunch is available to non-staying guests by reservation. Gluten-free status unconfirmed; wheat-containing shoyu and soba are standard components of the menu.

bio.background: 兄部坊 is one of the 39 shukubo (temple lodgings) lining the Omotesando approach to Zenkoji, one of Japan's most visited Buddhist temples. The name 'konokonbo' refers to a hereditary role overseeing Buddhist ceremonial affairs — a position this establishment has held for generations in Zenkoji's traditional New Year ritual called Doudouji.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture — temple precinct of Zenkoji
bio.specialty: Unagi yuba: a mock grilled-eel preparation made from yuba (tofu skin), tofu, and nori — a centerpiece dish invented when the temple pivoted to full shojin cooking. Also known for hohan (法飯), a medicinal herb-topped rice dish with vegetarian dashi broth, and hand-pulled soba served with burnt walnut miso.
bio.philosophy: To prepare seasonal Shinshu ingredients with care and creativity so that humble plant-based ingredients carry the full depth of flavor — using knowledge, effort, and devotion rather than animal products.
roles: Shojin ryori restaurant (day visitors welcome by reservation)
Overnight accommodation with morning temple ritual

anecdotes.text: About 50 years ago, when most Zenkoji shukubo still served fish to pilgrims, the then-head priest's wife declared 'this is the era for shojin ryori using local seasonal ingredients' and redirected the kitchen entirely to plant-based Buddhist cuisine — making 兄部坊 the pioneer of formal shojin dining at Zenkoji.

The signature unagi yuba dish was conceived at the moment the temple committed to shojin ryori — a creative answer to the challenge of making vegetarian food feel satisfying and visually striking, using yuba, tofu, and nori to mimic the appearance and texture of kabayaki grilled eel.

A 2012 diner described being shown to a private tatami room overlooking a courtyard, with the proprietress personally explaining each dish — 'simple yet flavorful, featuring seasonal vegetables and local Nagano specialties.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hand-pulled soba is a standard course dish — cross-contamination with gluten must be assumed. No dedicated GF preparation area is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard shojin ryori uses regular Japanese shoyu, which contains wheat. The official site mentions 'shojin dashi' broth but does not specify tamari or wheat-free soy sauce.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy is disclosed on the official site or any reviewed third-party source. Reservations are required and can be made by phone (026-234-6677), making it possible to discuss dietary needs directly with staff before arrival.

positives.text: Shojin ryori is by tradition entirely free of meat, fish, eggs, and animal-derived stocks — the kitchen's core philosophy eliminates all animal products, so accidental meat/fish inclusion is extremely unlikely.
The proprietress personally explains dishes to dining guests, suggesting a hands-on hospitality style where direct questions about ingredients could be raised.

name_ja: 信州善光寺 薬王院
cuisine: Shojin ryori (Buddhist vegetarian) — chef-foraged wild mushrooms and mountain vegetables, creative seasonal dishes, all plant-based dashi
address_ja: 〒380-0851 長野県長野市元善町657
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned only city-level coordinates for this address. Lat/lng are approximate based on Zenkoji temple precinct reference (36.6615, 138.1877) — Yakuoin is confirmed to be a shukubo directly on the Omotesando approach to Zenkoji, well within the 1.61km target radius.
neighborhood: 元善町 / Zenkoji temple precinct, Omotesando approach
seats_text: Up to 100 for dining; private rooms for groups of 2–4 also available
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (last order 14:30), daily; dinner served to overnight lodging guests only
closed_days: No regular closing day listed
cultural_comfort_note: No English-speaking staff noted across multiple sources; expect a fully Japanese-language environment.
vegan_detail: Shojin ryori here is strictly plant-based by tradition: dashi is made from kombu and shiitake mushroom soaking water — no bonito, no fish, no meat anywhere in the kitchen. Dishes include mountain vegetable sesame salad, vegetable tempura, creative soba preparations, sesame tofu, simmered dishes with plant-based broth, and a mock eel dish (unagi yuba) made without any seafood. Hidden animal products are not a concern at a properly observed shojin kitchen, but confirm at booking if you have specific concerns.
gf_detail: Gluten is present in multiple core dishes: soba noodles (buckwheat + wheat blend is standard in Japan unless stated otherwise), tempura batter (wheat flour), and conventional soy sauce (contains wheat) likely used in simmered dishes. No gluten-free menu or dedicated GF preparations are mentioned on the official site or in any reviewed source. Guests with celiac disease or serious wheat allergy must contact the temple directly at least two days before their visit (026-232-8901) and ask explicitly — the temple does note it can accommodate allergy needs by prior arrangement.

bio.background: Yakuoin is one of the Zenkoji temple lodgings (shukubo) that line the Omotesando approach to Zenkoji, one of Japan's most revered Buddhist temples in Nagano. The building itself survived the great Zenkoji fire of 1891, preserving Edo-period architecture including cedar beam ceilings and a 400-year-old hearth with smoke-darkened timber.
bio.specialty: Chef-foraged wild mushrooms and mountain vegetables prepared as creative shojin ryori — signature dishes include seasonal mountain vegetable tempura (large-cut lotus root and sweet potato), creative soba variations (ankake-style, salad-style), sesame tofu, a mock eel dish made entirely from yuba and plant-based tare, and preserved plum products from the temple's own trees.
bio.philosophy: The head priest Hara goes into the mountains himself to gather wild mushrooms and mountain greens, then personally prepares every dish. His approach: use familiar Shinshu ingredients but present them in ways guests have never encountered — always rooted in the shojin rule of no meat, no fish, and plant-based dashi (kombu and shiitake soaking water only).
bio.origin: Nagano, Japan — Zenkoji temple precinct

anecdotes.text: The head priest personally enters the mountains to harvest wild mushrooms and mountain vegetables, then handles all cooking himself — a rare dual role of forager-chef within a working Buddhist temple.
Yakuoin's building is one of the few Zenkoji shukubo to survive the devastating 1891 Zenkoji Great Fire, preserving Edo-period cedar ceilings and a 400-year-old hearth.
The 'unagi yuba' (mock eel) dish — a classic shojin 'modoki' preparation — is made entirely without seafood, including the sauce (tare), through careful layering of yuba and plant-based seasonings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on dedicated fryers or cross-contamination protocols is publicly available. Tempura is a core menu item, so a shared fryer with wheat-battered items must be assumed unless confirmed otherwise.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat) is the default in shojin ryori kitchens. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are mentioned anywhere in public sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shojin ryori is inherently plant-based; cross-contact with animal products is structurally unlikely in this kitchen, though no formal certification exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The temple's tourism board listing states allergy accommodations are available ('アレルギー食対応可') and dietary/religious needs can be discussed on inquiry. Contact at least two days in advance.

positives.text: Dashi is made exclusively from kombu and shiitake soaking liquid — no bonito, no fish stock, no meat-based broth anywhere in the kitchen. This is structurally safer for fish-allergic guests than most Japanese restaurants.
The menu is entirely plant-based by religious tradition, eliminating the main animal-product cross-contact risks common in omnivore kitchens.

caveats: Lunch is open to non-lodging guests but requires a reservation at least 2 days in advance and a minimum of 4 people for meal-only visits. The meal-only lunch window (11:00–15:00) confirmed on Tabelog and Gnavi; dinner is for overnight guests only. Hours listed reflect the lunch service. No English staff: phone reservations and any allergy discussions will need to be in Japanese or via a Japanese-speaking intermediary. Coordinates are approximate (Zenkoji precinct reference) — GSI geocoder did not resolve the street-level address.

cuisine: 信州手打ち蕎麦（二八・十割）
address_ja: 長野県長野市東之門町393番地
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 長野市長野東之門町393; result [138.189346, 36.659149] is approximately 314m from Zenkoji reference point, well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 東之門町 / 善光寺下駅
seats_text: 72 seats across main floor (40) and two second-floor tatami rooms (8 and 24); private room configurations available for 2–30+ guests
closed_days: 火曜日（繁忙期は変更あり）
cultural_comfort_note: Traditional Japanese soba-ya atmosphere with tatami rooms and Japanese-language menus; no evidence of English menus or staff, though located on a popular temple pilgrimage route visited by international tourists.
vegan_detail: The dipping broth (tsuyu) is fish-based throughout — made from soda-gatsuo (soda bonito) and kame-bushi shaved in-house, combined with a fermented kaeshi sauce maintained for over a century. There are no vegan broth alternatives listed on the menu or mentioned in any reviewed source. Tempura is available but cooked in shared oil. Not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: Standard soba is 二八 (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) — not gluten-free. A 十割 (100% buckwheat) soba is available, limited to 10 servings per day, and contains no wheat in the noodle itself. However, the tsuyu (dipping sauce) is made with traditional Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned. The kitchen produces wheat-containing noodles alongside buckwheat noodles, meaning cross-contamination risk is high. Celiacs should not assume safety; call ahead to ask about the 十割 noodle and tsuyu ingredients specifically.

bio.background: Founded in 1900 (Meiji 33) by Shichiroemon on the eastern approach to Zenkoji Temple, Kitanoya Honten has operated as a four-generation family soba-ya for over 125 years. The shop began in Hoshina district before relocating to its current address at the foot of the temple precinct, where it has become a fixture for both pilgrims and Nagano locals.
bio.specialty: 純手打ち細切り蕎麦 — hand-cut thin soba made from stone-ground Shinshu buckwheat (sourced from Togakushi and the Yatsugatake region), and a limited daily run of 十割そば (100% buckwheat, 10 servings only) using stone-milled Shinano-machi flour.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant centres on two heirlooms: a kaeshi (sweet soy reduction) maintained in the same ceramic vessels for over 100 years, and a twice-monthly in-house bonito-shaving ritual using soda-gatsuo and kame-bushi that takes more than two hours per batch. The ethos is continuity — the soup stock, the noodle cut, and the flour blend have remained essentially unchanged across generations.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture
roles: pilgrimage-route dining institution near Zenkoji Temple

anecdotes.text: The restaurant maintains two antique ceramic vessels holding the original kaeshi sauce, described by the family as 'the history of the shop itself' — the reduction has been refreshed and topped up continuously since the Meiji era.

Three to four times a month, staff shave fresh bonito in-house using two types of aged katsuo through a multi-stage steam-and-shave process lasting over two hours — a practice unchanged since founding.
A food blogger visiting in September 2024 described the 十割そば as having a distinctive elasticity rather than brittleness, served on a square ceramic plate with a thick, glossy dipping broth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen produces wheat-containing 二八 soba (20% wheat noodles) alongside the 十割 (100% buckwheat) option. No source documents separate preparation surfaces, water, or equipment for GF noodles. Cross-contamination risk must be assumed until the restaurant confirms otherwise.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu is made with traditional Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), which contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is listed anywhere in available menu or review sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is made from soda-gatsuo and kame-bushi (bonito varieties) shaved in-house. No plant-based broth option is documented. All soba dishes appear to be served with fish-stock tsuyu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information on allergy-handling protocols is available from any reviewed source. The restaurant does accept reservations by phone (026-232-2492), which would be the appropriate channel to ask about dietary needs in advance.

positives.text: A daily limited run of 十割そば (100% buckwheat noodles, wheat-free ingredient) is available, making it a starting point for discussion with the kitchen for gluten-sensitive diners who can tolerate shared-kitchen risk.

caveats: Hours split into two services (lunch 11:00-15:00, dinner 17:30-20:30); the restaurant is closed between services. The 十割そば (100% buckwheat) is limited to 10 servings per day and sells out early — arrive at opening if this is a priority. Parking available (8 spaces). Credit cards and PayPay accepted.

cuisine: 信州蕎麦 (Shinshu soba)
neighborhood: 大門町 / 善光寺下駅
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町587-3
geocode_note: Geocoded via geocoding.jp: lat 36.657432, lng 138.186688. GSI chō-level for 大字長野大門町 returns [138.187943, 36.656933]. Both consistent. Distance from Zenkoji (36.6614, 138.1872) ≈ 0.5 km, well within 1.61 km radius.
seats_text: Approximately 30 seats across two floors, with tatami seating upstairs
hours_raw: 月・水〜日 11:00-14:30、金〜日のみ夜営業 17:00-19:00（または19:30）、火曜定休。麺がなくなり次第閉店。
closed_days: 火曜日定休
cultural_comfort_note: Staff do not speak English; cash-only payment; a Japanese-speaking companion or phrasebook will help, though ordering from a soba-focused menu is straightforward.
vegan_detail: All soba dipping and soup broths are made with bonito (katsuobushi) dashi — a thick-shaved, fully-cured dried bonito — rendering them non-vegan. There are no plant-based broth alternatives listed. Tempura items use egg in the batter. No vegan-adapted dishes are available.
gf_detail: The soba noodles are 二八そば (niju-hachi) — 80% buckwheat flour blended with 20% wheat flour — so the noodles themselves contain gluten. Tempura items (tendon sets, vegetable tempura) are wheat-battered. The dipping soy sauce (tsuyu) is also standard wheat-containing shoyu. There are no dedicated GF options, no soba-100 (juwari) noodles mentioned, and no cross-contamination controls documented. Not safe for celiac or wheat-sensitive diners.

bio.background: そば処 元屋 is a long-established handmade soba shop situated just off the main Zenkoji temple approach in Nagano's Daimoncho district, drawing both temple pilgrims and local regulars for decades. The restaurant mills its own buckwheat coarsely (自家製粉の粗挽き粉) and blends it with three varieties of buckwheat flour to produce thin, pale noodles with a notably smooth throat-feel and balanced sweetness. The establishment closes each day as soon as the day's soba runs out.
bio.origin: 長野市大門町, 善光寺門前
bio.specialty: 鴨せいろ (duck seiro) — cold soba served with warm bonito-and-duck broth — and きのこおろしせいろ (kinoko oroshi seiro), a refreshing preparation with mushroom dashi and grated daikon. Generous portions at affordable prices are a defining trait.
bio.philosophy: Serve hearty quantities at stable, accessible prices — the restaurant is known locally for portion sizes that far exceed expectations for the cost.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant closes each day when the hand-pulled soba runs out, sometimes before the posted closing time — a mark of fresh-made-daily production rather than pre-made stock.
One review notes the broth is prepared with 厚削りの本枯れ節 (thick-shaved, fully-cured bonito flakes), giving the tsuyu a distinctly sweet and deep umami character that regular visitors return for.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The soba noodles are 二八 (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat); wheat flour is used in-house for noodle production, so the entire kitchen environment involves wheat. No cross-contamination controls have been documented by any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing shoyu) is used in the tsuyu dipping broth; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito dashi (厚削りの本枯れ節) is the foundation of all broth preparations; there is no plant-based broth option, and fish-based dashi permeates even light menu items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One tourism listing notes the restaurant 'welcomes consultation on religious dietary requirements,' but no specific allergy protocol or trained allergy-handling procedure is documented.

positives.text: Soba is made fresh daily and sold out when it runs out — no pre-made, stored product.

caveats: Evening hours (17:00-19:00 or 19:30, Fri-Sun only) vary by source and may have changed; confirm by phone before a dinner visit. Seat count varies between sources (30 vs 50); 30 used as the more consistent figure. The restaurant may close early when the day's soba sells out. No official website exists — all data is from third-party tourism and review listings.

cuisine: おやき・郷土料理
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町518
geocode_note: Coordinates from geocoding.jp for 長野県長野市大門町518. Approximately 560m south of Zenkoji, well within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 大門町 / 善光寺表参道
seats_text: 8席。小さなイートインスペース。
closed_days: 年中無休（12月31日のみ休業）
cultural_comfort_note: Neighborhood counter shop on the Zenkoji temple approach — tourists are welcome but all signage and interaction is in Japanese; pointing at the display case works fine.
vegan_detail: All oyaki fillings are vegetable-based — eggplant with miso, spicy daikon with miso, nozawana (pickled greens), pumpkin with red bean — and no obvious meat or fish is used. However, the shop does not advertise as vegan, and miso-based seasonings can occasionally contain hidden dashi. Ask staff about specific fillings on the day.
gf_detail: Oyaki dough is made from wheat flour (中力粉) — this is the core of the dish and cannot be omitted. Nothing on the menu is gluten-free. Not suitable for celiac or wheat allergy.

bio.background: A JAながの (Nagano Agricultural Cooperative) operated shop on the Zenkoji temple approach, run collectively by local farming women since the mid-1990s. The shop was established as a direct sales outlet for local farm produce, with the oyaki operation growing into its main draw.
bio.specialty: 辛大根おやき — a steamed dumpling of thin wheat dough packed with thick-cut daikon radish in a sweet-spicy miso; a signature item since shortly after opening.
bio.origin: 長野市大門町 / 善光寺表参道
bio.philosophy: 「お母さんの手造り」— mothers' handmade. Local farming women prepare everything by hand daily using in-season vegetables sourced directly from cooperative farms, with no additives. The aim is the kind of nostalgic, gentle flavor that feels like home cooking.

anecdotes.text: On a hot summer visit a reviewer noted that staff offered cold water unprompted to customers waiting at the counter — 'a charm beyond the food itself.'

The shop carries seasonal rotating varieties — spring brings butterbur miso (ふきみそ) and sakura oyaki, mid-summer brings Japanese ginger (みょうが), winter brings apple (りんご) — meaning the display case looks different on every visit.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oyaki dough is wheat flour — wheat is the primary ingredient throughout. No gluten-free items exist. Unsuitable for celiac.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Miso is used as the main seasoning in oyaki fillings. Traditional Japanese miso is fermented with wheat and/or barley; specific miso sourcing for this shop is not documented.

positives.text: The shop emphasizes no additives (無添加) and hand-preparation with local seasonal vegetables — a positive sign for ingredient simplicity.

caveats: Hours shift in winter (Dec–Feb close at 17:00 instead of 17:30). Tabelog lists 不定休 (irregular closures) alongside the Dec 31 holiday — call ahead for holiday periods. Cash only; QR code payments (PayPay etc.) also accepted per Tabelog. The shop is a retail counter, not a full sit-down restaurant — 8 seats are for eating oyaki on the spot.

lead: おやきと漬物の専門店 あづみ堂
name_ja: おやきと漬物の専門店 あづみ堂
name_en: Oyaki to Tsukemono no Senmonten Azumido
cuisine: おやき・漬物
address_ja: 長野県長野市長野元善町486
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 長野県長野市大字長野元善町486番地 → [138.187317, 36.659286]; ~235m from Zenkoji reference point, well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 善光寺仲見世通り（参道）
closed_days: 年中無休（無休）
seats_text: テイクアウト専門。店頭のベンチで立ち食い可。イートイン席はなし。
cultural_comfort_note: 参道沿いの観光地にあり指さし注文は可能だが、メニュー・スタッフ対応は日本語のみ。
vegan_detail: 野沢菜・なす・かぼちゃなど野菜系の餡もあるが、味噌・醤油（小麦・大豆含む）が使われており、製造工場では乳・卵も扱う。豚・牛・鶏肉入りの品も多数ある。ビーガン対応の表示は一切なく、個々の餡の動物性原料を店頭で確認する必要がある。
gf_detail: 全品に小麦粉または小麦グルテンが含まれる。「米粉もっちりおやき」も原材料に「小麦グルテン」を使用（アレルゲン表示：小麦含む）。製造工場ではそば・乳・卵・くるみも扱い、交差汚染リスクあり。グルテンフリー品・セリアック対応品はなし。

bio.background: 安曇野市に本拠を置く信州の郷土食専門店。長野・善光寺の仲見世通り（参道）に漬物老舗・高橋商店の店舗内テナントとして出店している。本店は安曇野のスワンガーデン安曇野内にあり、囲炉裏でおやきを焼く体験が名物。
bio.specialty: おやき（野沢菜・なす・かぼちゃなど信州伝統の餡を包んだ蒸し焼き饅頭）と信州産漬物。
bio.philosophy: 信州の郷土食を土産として広く届けることを軸に、うす皮・そば皮・米粉・十六穀など複数の皮バリエーションを展開。添加物を抑えた漬物にもこだわる。
bio.origin: 長野県安曇野市

anecdotes.text: 善光寺参道の中程に位置し、焼きたてのおやきをベンチで食べ歩きできる。犬連れでも店頭購入可能と口コミで紹介されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 製造工場ではそば・乳成分・卵・くるみを含む製品を製造しており、交差汚染のリスクがある（全品のアレルゲン表示に共通記載）。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: そば皮おやき野沢菜の原材料に「醤油」「味噌」が含まれ、アレルゲンとして小麦・大豆を表示。米粉もっちりおやきも「小麦グルテン」を使用しており、全品小麦を含む。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 豚・牛・鶏を使った餡と同じ製造設備を共用。野菜系の餡でも工場内での交差汚染が否定できない。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルゲン情報は公式サイトの品目別ページで公開されているが、店頭でのアレルギー対応については情報なし。予約不可・対応スタッフへの事前相談は困難な業態。

positives.text: 公式サイトに全品目のアレルゲン一覧ページが整備されており、原材料を事前に確認できる。

caveats: 善光寺仲見世通りの支店（元善町486）についての直接の営業時間一次情報は公式サイト店舗ページ（9:00-17:00、年中無休）による。食べログには詳細時間の記載なし。現地確認を推奨。カード不可・電子マネー（交通系等）・QRコード決済は可。

lead: 信州善光寺 薬王院
name_ja: 信州善光寺 薬王院
cuisine: 精進料理
address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町657
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned only city-level (Nagano City) for 元善町657. Coordinates set to Zenkoji Temple reference point (36.6614, 138.1872); Motoyoshicho is the immediate precinct in front of Zenkoji, well within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 元善町 - 善光寺参道
seats_text: 80 seats for dining guests; private rooms for 2–4 also available; overnight capacity 70
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30); breakfast ~7:00–9:00 for overnight and advance-reserved guests
closed_days: None listed; appears open daily
cultural_comfort_note: A working temple lodging (shukubo) serving meals primarily in Japanese; no English-language service is advertised, though staff have been described as friendly to foreign guests.
vegan_detail: Shojin ryori is traditionally plant-based — no meat, fish, or dairy — and the head priest uses kombu and shiitake dashi rather than bonito or fish stock, making the cuisine genuinely vegan at its base. However, one Tabelog reviewer noted fish (iwana char) appearing on a menu, which could reflect a non-standard course or reviewer error; treat this as unresolved. The allium-free tradition (no garlic, onion, leeks) is observed. Confirm current fish usage directly with the temple before visiting.
gf_detail: Shojin ryori is not inherently gluten-free. Yakuoin's documented menu includes fu (wheat gluten), which is a standard shojin ingredient — this alone disqualifies it for wheat-avoiders unless modified. Soba (buckwheat noodles) also appears on the menu; in a temple kitchen shared with wheat-based ingredients, cross-contamination cannot be ruled out. Standard Japanese soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) contains wheat. No gluten-free adaptation, dedicated fryer, or allergen-screening protocol is mentioned anywhere. The temple can reportedly accommodate allergy requests if notified in advance, but there is no public record of confirmed celiac-safe preparation.

bio.background: Yakuoin is one of the historic shukubo (temple lodgings) of Zenkoji, founded approximately 1,000 years ago at the time of Zenkoji's establishment, originally built as an Amidado hall. The current building dates to the Edo period and survived the great fire of 1891 that destroyed much of the precinct. In 2023 the temple relaunched its dining program for day visitors, combining centuries-old monastic practice with a contemporary dessert experience.
bio.specialty: Shojin Teppachi Ryori — a Buddhist vegetarian iron-bowl meal built around seasonal Shinshu mountain vegetables, wild mushrooms, and handmade tofu. The head priest personally forages for wild vegetables and mushrooms in the surrounding mountains and handles much of the cooking himself. Signature preparations include vegetable tempura, ankake-style soba, sesame tofu, and a vinegared dish made with ume vinegar pressed from two plum trees growing on the temple grounds.
bio.philosophy: Strict adherence to shojin principles: no meat, fish, or animal-derived stock. Dashi is drawn from kombu and shiitake soaking water. No garlic or onion. Ingredients are sourced from the temple's own garden plot and nearby mountain harvests, celebrating the agricultural calendar of the Shinshu highlands.

anecdotes.text: The head priest forages personally in the mountains for wild vegetables and mushrooms used in the shojin meals — an unusual hands-on practice that connects the cuisine directly to the surrounding landscape.

Since summer 2023 the temple has added a seasonal Mont Blanc-making experience ('甘露の法雨') for afternoon visitors, blending the monastic setting with a participatory dessert workshop — a new draw that has attracted day visitors beyond the traditional overnight temple-stay crowd.

bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture (元善町657, immediately in front of Zenkoji main hall)

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba (buckwheat noodles) features on the shojin menu. In a temple kitchen that also uses fu (wheat gluten) and standard soy sauce, cross-contamination of buckwheat dishes with wheat cannot be excluded. No separate preparation space for gluten-free orders is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned. Standard Japanese koikuchi shoyu — which contains wheat — is the default condiment in this cuisine style and is not noted as substituted at Yakuoin.

vegan_cross_contact.text: One Tabelog reviewer noted fish (iwana char) on the menu, which would contradict the shojin ryori framework. This is unresolved — it may reflect a special course or an error — and should be confirmed directly with the temple before a strict vegan visits.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Multiple aggregator sources (including the Zenkoji official shukubo listing and travel portal summaries) note that the temple can accommodate allergy-related requests if guests contact them in advance. No formal allergy protocol or written guarantee is published.

positives.text: The head priest uses only kombu and shiitake-based dashi — no bonito, no fish stock — making the base broth genuinely animal-product free, unlike many Japanese restaurants where hidden dashi is the main vegan pitfall.
Homemade ume (plum) vinegar from the temple's own trees is used in all vinegared dishes, replacing commercial seasonings and keeping sour preparations simple and plant-derived.

caveats: Meal-only lunch requires a minimum party of 4 and advance reservation (ideally 1 week ahead; 2 days minimum). Breakfast is available to individuals but only with an advance reservation and is primarily oriented toward overnight guests. The Mont Blanc dessert experience (afternoons, 14:00–16:00) is bookable separately for smaller groups. Credit cards (Visa, Mastercard, JCB, Amex, Diners) accepted; no IC card or QR-code payment.

lead: 信州善光寺 門前そば処 藤木庵
name_ja: 信州善光寺 門前そば処 藤木庵
cuisine: 信州そば (Shinshu soba)
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町67番地
geocode_note: Matched via GSI address search on 長野県長野市大字長野大門町６７番地; coordinates place it ~560m south of Zenkō-ji gate, within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 大門町 (Daimon-cho)
vegan_detail: All dipping sauces (mori-tsuyu and kake-tsuyu) are made with two-year-aged dried bonito and mackerel dashi, plus kelp, mirin, sake, and sugar. No vegan broth alternative is offered. Tororo (yam) and walnut sauce toppings are plant-based, but they accompany the same fish-based broth. No vegan option is identifiable from the menu.
gf_detail: The standard noodle (toichi-soba) contains 9% wheat flour as a binder; even the 100% buckwheat juwari soba is made in an open kitchen where wheat flour is in constant use. The dipping sauce uses standard soy sauce (which contains wheat). The restaurant's own allergy notice states that buckwheat flour can contaminate dishes that do not normally contain it due to on-site stone milling, and that customers with any allergy must notify staff — but the kitchen is not set up for allergy separation. Not safe for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (last order 14:30); closed Tuesdays and Thursdays; additional irregular closures — check the business calendar on fujikian.co.jp
closed_days: Tuesdays and Thursdays (open on national holidays; additional irregular closures)
seats_text: 64 seats across two floors: 7 counter, 23 table, 14 raised platform, 20 tatami. No reservations accepted; uses the EPARK web waiting system to hold your place in line before arriving.
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Zenkō-ji gate-town soba-ya with tatami seating and a stone-mill visible inside; comfortable for Japanese-speaking visitors, but the English website and tourism profile mean international guests are not uncommon.

bio.background: Founded in 1827 (Bunsei 10) during the reign of the 11th Tokugawa shogun, Fujikian has operated continuously for eight generations on the stone-paved main approach to Zenkō-ji. The restaurant began as a simple eating house serving pilgrims katsudon alongside soba, then gradually focused entirely on handmade soba under later proprietors. Current eighth-generation owner Koichi Fujisawa took the reins in 1999 after a career in banking, bringing a rigorous, quantified approach to a craft long governed by feel and intuition.
bio.origin: 大門町, Nagano City — the historic gate-town approach to Zenkō-ji
bio.specialty: Toichi-soba (とい一そば) — a stone-milled blend of 91% Kirishita buckwheat and 9% wheat binder, served with three dipping options: classic bonito-kelp broth, walnut sauce, and tororo (mountain yam) sauce. A limited daily quantity of juwari (100% buckwheat) soba is also made.
bio.philosophy: Fujisawa studied under soba master Yasuo Katakura at Ichasoan to replace intuition-based technique with measured, reproducible craft. His guiding principle: the essence of soba lies in the quality and freshness of the buckwheat. The restaurant mills flour daily from whole buckwheat berries sourced exclusively from Hiroyuki Ishikawa, a single farmer in Shinano-machi who grows Kirishita soba in the region's heavy morning fog and strong temperature swings.

anecdotes.text: Koichi Fujisawa left a banking career in 1999 to become the eighth-generation owner, deliberately studying quantified soba technique under master Yasuo Katakura rather than relying on inherited feel — an unusual move in a tradition-bound craft.

The stone mill sits in the coolest corner of the shop; milled flour rests overnight before use so it develops a subtle sweetness, and the water ratio and cutting method are adjusted each morning based on how the flour looks and feels that day.

The restaurant's signature Gokuraku Soba Toichi (ごくらく蕎麦とい一) presents one bowl of 91% buckwheat noodles with three separate dipping sauces — broth, walnut, and tororo — so a single order becomes a three-way tasting of how the same noodle changes character.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's own posted notice states that because wheat flour is used in many dishes and a noodle-making area with stone milling is inside the shop, even dishes that do not normally contain wheat flour may be contaminated. Not suitable for celiac or wheat-allergic diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The mori-tsuyu and kake-tsuyu dipping broths are made with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat. Tamari (wheat-free) soy sauce does not appear to be used based on the published ingredient list (soy sauce, bonito, mackerel, kelp, mirin, sugar, sake).

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dipping broths use two-year-aged dried bonito (本枯節) and mackerel stock. There is no fish-free broth option on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant asks that anyone with allergies (soba/buckwheat, wheat, or other allergens) inform staff before ordering. The standard tea served is buckwheat tea, which must also be flagged for buckwheat-allergic guests.

positives.text: The juwari soba (100% buckwheat, no wheat binder) is available in limited daily quantity and contains no wheat in the noodle itself — though the dipping sauce and kitchen cross-contamination remain concerns.

caveats: Payment policy discrepancy: Tabelog lists cash only, but the official English site and a 2024 blog report credit cards, PayPay, and electronic money accepted. The official site is treated as authoritative here (cash_only=false). Hours discrepancy: older sources list Tuesday-only closure; current official site confirms Tuesday + Thursday closure plus irregular calendar closures — always verify on fujikian.co.jp before visiting. Tamari/GF soy sauce claims that appeared in some aggregated search summaries could not be confirmed by any primary source; the published menu ingredient list shows standard soy sauce.

cuisine: French (フレンチ) — contemporary French cuisine, omakase course only, seasonal Shinshu ingredients
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野東之門町328
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 長野県長野市大字長野東之門町３２８番地 [138.188782, 36.658001]; ~400m from Zenkoji, well within 1.61km radius; within target box.
neighborhood: 東之門町 (Higashimonten-cho), Zenkoji temple district
seats_text: 18 seats; table and counter seating with views of the herb and flower garden
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00–15:00 (last order 13:00, ~2.5h); Dinner 18:30–23:00 (last order 19:30, ~3.5h); Tuesday–Saturday only
closed_days: Monday, Sunday; irregular additional closures (~5 per month)
cultural_comfort_note: Intimate 18-seat kominka restaurant staffed by a Japanese chef; no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff — basic Japanese or prior arrangement advisable.
vegan_detail: Omakase-only French courses built around fish and meat; no vegan course exists. The restaurant asks guests to notify in advance of any food preferences or allergies, so it may be possible to request a plant-based substitution for individual elements, but a fully vegan course is not offered. Hidden animal-derived components are standard in French cuisine (butter, cream, dashi-adjacent stocks, eggs in pastry).
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free protocol found. Classic French omakase routinely uses wheat flour in sauces (roux, beurre blanc thickeners), pastry, bread courses, and possibly soy sauce in some modern French-Japanese preparations. The restaurant requests advance allergy notification, so communication before booking is essential. No dedicated fryer info available; cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out. Not suitable for celiac without detailed pre-visit discussion.
caveats: Hours and closed days show minor discrepancies across sources (Tabelog lists 11:30-13:00/18:00-20:00; official site and Hitosara list 12:00-15:00/18:30-23:00) — official site hours used as primary. Tabelog score 3.17 based on 37 reviews as of research date. Confirm current hours and irregular closures before visiting.

bio.background: Born in Nagano Prefecture in 1977. After culinary school in Tokyo, he spent 12 years apprenticing at Hôtel de Mikuni — one of Japan's most celebrated French kitchens. He then served as head chef at Izu Auberge Arcanaise and Yakushima Sankara Hotel & Spa before returning to his home prefecture.
bio.specialty: Omakase French courses built around hand-selected seasonal Shinshu ingredients — lunch is 7 courses (¥9,900), dinner 10 courses (¥18,700); a 10% service charge applies.
bio.origin: Nagano Prefecture, Japan
bio.philosophy: The name 'la rencontre' is French for 'encounter' — the chef's guiding idea is that every meal is a meeting: between seasonal ingredients, between French technique and Shinshu terroir, and between chef and guest. He sources produce personally and explores unexpected pairings to create dishes available nowhere else.

anecdotes.text: Seshimo trained for 12 years at Hôtel de Mikuni in Tokyo — the flagship of chef Kiyomi Mikuni, widely credited with defining modern French-Japanese cuisine in Japan — before running kitchens at two luxury resort properties.

The restaurant occupies a kominka — a traditional Japanese farmhouse — estimated to be 150 years old, renovated into an intimate 18-seat dining room with counter seats overlooking an herb and flower garden.
Reservations must be made by the day before; same-day bookings are not accepted because the chef personally sources ingredients each morning based on confirmed guests.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls is publicly available. French omakase kitchens share all preparation surfaces; wheat flour is used in pastry, sauces, and bread — standard cross-contact risk applies.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No specific mention of soy sauce use, but modern French-Japanese fusion techniques may incorporate wheat-containing soy sauce. This cannot be confirmed or ruled out without direct inquiry.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All courses include fish and meat components; butter, cream, and eggs are central to classic French technique. Vegan cross-contact in a shared kitchen is unavoidable without specific accommodation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant explicitly asks guests to notify in advance of any food intolerances or allergies ('苦手な食材やアレルギーがございましたら、必ず事前にお申し付けください'). Same-day requests cannot be accommodated as ingredients are sourced the morning of service.

positives.text: Advance allergy communication is built into the reservation process; the chef personally sources all ingredients, which may allow for substitutions when arranged well ahead of time.

lead: ザ フジヤ ゴホンジン
name_ja: ザ フジヤ ゴホンジン（藤屋御本陳）
cuisine: Italian
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町80
geocode_note: Coordinates from web search cross-reference (Yahoo Maps / search result citation); GSI address-search returned incorrect results for 大門町, so coordinates sourced from third-party map data. Location is approximately 0.48 km south of Zenkoji, well within the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 大門町、善光寺門前
seats_text: 21 seats in the dining room; private rooms also available for groups up to 120
closed_days: No regular closing day (年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: English menus and English-speaking staff available; the restaurant is accustomed to international visitors given its location near Zenkoji.
vegan_detail: The restaurant explicitly states it cannot accommodate vegetarian diets at lunch. For dinner, a la carte selections may be available and course menus can sometimes be modified with advance notice — but this requires a phone reservation and individual discussion. No dedicated vegan menu exists. Dishes are Italian in style but rely on meat, seafood, dairy, and eggs as standard ingredients; hidden animal-based stocks are expected.
gf_detail: The restaurant's own FAQ states it cannot accommodate gluten-free diets at lunch. For dinner, some a la carte items may be naturally lower in gluten, but no gluten-free bread or focaccia substitutes are offered and course menus cannot be modified for GF requirements. Italian pasta cuisine means wheat is central to the kitchen. Cross-contamination risk is high given pasta is a core menu item. Guests with celiac disease should not dine here without a direct phone conversation with staff well in advance, and even then confidence cannot be high.

bio.background: Founded in 1648 during the Keian era as an official honjin — a licensed lodging reserved for feudal lords on their mandatory pilgrimages between their domains and Edo. By 1776, the Fujii family had secured a monopoly on the honjin role for the Zenkoji station, hosting the Maeda clan and other daimyo. Over the Meiji and Taisho eras the inn welcomed a roll call of Japanese luminaries: statesman Ito Hirobumi, Enlightenment scholar Fukuzawa Yukichi, industrialist Shibusawa Eiichi, and the surveyor Ino Tadataka all slept under its roof. The current building was completed in 1925 by master carpenter Shida Shozaemon — the same craftsman who reconstructed Zenkoji's Niomon Gate — in a striking hybrid style: Art Deco tiled reinforced concrete on the exterior, traditional sukiya woodwork within. In 1997 it became the first building in Nagano City to be listed as a national registered tangible cultural property. After the 1998 Nagano Winter Olympics reduced demand for traditional lodging, the 17th-generation proprietor Fujii Daishiro reinvented the property in 2006 as THE FUJIYA GOHONJIN, a fine-dining Italian restaurant and wedding venue. The kitchen draws on Shinshu mountain produce and fresh seafood sourced from the Japan Sea coast and Tsukiji, presenting it as upscale Italian cuisine inside a century-old Art Deco landmark.
bio.origin: 長野市大門町 / Nagano, Daimon-cho
bio.specialty: Seasonal Italian courses built around Shinshu local ingredients; the Chef's Lunch changes every few weeks and includes wagyu beef upgrades and house-made pasta
bio.philosophy: 不易流行 (fueki ryuko) — maintaining unchanging core values of Japanese hospitality while continuously evolving; the stated purpose is 'creating even greater happiness in moments of happiness'

anecdotes.text: The 1925 building was constructed by Shida Shozaemon, the master carpenter who also rebuilt Zenkoji's famous Niomon Gate, making the Fujiya and the temple gate architecturally linked by the same hand.

In 1997, Fujiya became the first building in Nagano City to receive national registered tangible cultural property status, recognizing its rare Art Deco exterior fused with traditional sukiya interior.
Statesman Ito Hirobumi, scholar Fukuzawa Yukichi, and industrialist Shibusawa Eiichi are among the Meiji-era figures recorded as having stayed at the original Fujiya inn.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Pasta — made with wheat flour — is the central product of this Italian kitchen. Cross-contamination with gluten must be assumed to be high. The restaurant's own FAQ confirms it cannot accommodate gluten-free diets at lunch and offers no course-menu modifications for GF at dinner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Italian cuisine style; soy sauce is not a primary concern here, but wheat-containing pasta, bread/focaccia, and flour-based sauces are used throughout the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan or vegetarian menu exists. Meat, seafood, dairy, and eggs are core to most dishes. Vegetarian accommodation is explicitly declined at lunch.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests with food allergies are asked to inform staff (食材アレルギーをお持ちの方はスタッフにお申しつけください). Allergy accommodations and religious dietary needs can be discussed, but require a phone reservation rather than online booking.
Staff can provide ingredient details during your visit, but the FAQ makes clear that structural GF or vegetarian accommodation is very limited.

positives.text: English menus and English-speaking staff are available, making allergy communication somewhat easier for non-Japanese speakers.
For dinner, some a la carte items may be naturally suitable depending on selection; direct phone contact before visiting is the recommended path for any dietary need.

caveats: Hours sourced from Tabelog and Nagano CVB; the Nagano CVB listing shows slightly different lunch start time (11:30 vs 11:00) — confirm directly before visiting.
