bio.specialty: 十善そば (Juzen soba) — 100% buckwheat noodles made entirely from the shop's own stone-milled flour, a dish reportedly the first of its kind developed in Nagano. The name 十善 evokes the ten virtuous precepts associated with Zenkō-ji's Buddhist tradition.
bio.philosophy: San-tate (三たて) — grind only what you need, make it to order, boil it immediately. Quality buckwheat sourced from Shinshu (including Kijimadaira-grown Shinano Ichigo variety) is ground in the shop's own mill, ensuring the flour is never stale.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant previously operated on Nagano's Chuo-dori main street before settling in its current house in the Tamachi/Tsuruga area, and was at some point featured on the Japanese TV programme Kenmin SHOW.
The shop is a member of the 信州そば切りの会 (Shinshu Soba Cutting Association), a guild of traditional soba artisans in Nagano Prefecture.
A 300 kg stone mill sits visibly in the dining area — guests can see it while eating, a deliberate statement about the mill-to-table philosophy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Both 80% wheat-containing zaru soba and 100% buckwheat juzen soba are prepared in the same kitchen; no separation protocol has been documented.
Tempura (wheat batter) is a menu item; fryer separation from buckwheat items is not confirmed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu dipping sauce is described as soy-based; no mention of tamari or wheat-free soy sauce anywhere in available sources. Standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tsuyu almost certainly contains katsuobushi (bonito) dashi — no kombu-only or vegan dashi option is documented.
Tempura set includes shrimp; egg roll slices accompany lunch sets — fish and egg present in the kitchen.

positives.text: The 十善そば noodles themselves are made from 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat added — the purest buckwheat noodle on the menu.
Stone-mill self-grinding means full traceability of the buckwheat flour used in the noodles.

caveats: Dinner hours (17:30–20:30) confirmed by a 2019 blog post and gnavi; the Tabelog listing displays lunch hours only — verify by phone before an evening visit. Hours on weekends marked irregular as soba sells out before 14:00. Cash accepted; QR payment (PayPay) also accepted — no credit cards. Owner name not publicly documented. No allergen protocol or GF/vegan declarations exist anywhere in the public record; safety ratings reflect structural risk, not confirmed incidents.

cuisine: カフェ・とんかつ（Vintage retro cafe / reservation-only tonkatsu lunch）
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野岩石町222-1
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch returned [138.189713, 36.657013]. Approximately 540m from Zenkō-ji center (36.6614, 138.1872) — well within the 1.61km radius. Matches Tabelog-stated 362m from Zenkojishita Station.
neighborhood: 善光寺下 (Zenkojishita)
seats_text: 13 seats total across the 2F cafe space in the renovated 100-year-old building
hours_raw: モーニング 7:30–9:30 LO / ランチ（成満堂）11:00–14:00 LO / カフェ（金土のみ）14:30–19:00 LO。定休日：水曜・木曜
closed_days: 水曜・木曜（Wednesday and Thursday）
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, intimate Showa-era retro space with tatami-adjacent aesthetics; shoes-off to reach the 2F cafe, no English signage or menu noted, but the small and low-key atmosphere is welcoming to solo and female diners.
vegan_detail: The cafe's signature ぎゅうにゅうどん (milk udon, ¥450) uses dairy. Lunch is tonkatsu (breaded pork) cooked in shared fry oil. Morning sets include egg dishes and miso soup likely containing dashi. No vegan options identified.
gf_detail: Tonkatsu (成満堂 lunch) is panko-breaded pork — contains wheat. Milk udon contains wheat noodles. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) in use. No gluten-free alternatives or dedicated fryer. Not suitable for celiac diners.

bio.chef_name: 高野恵二 (Takano Keiji) — 成満堂; 望月仁美 (Mochizuki Hitomi) — 大福屋 cafe
bio.background: 大福屋 occupies the 2nd floor of a 100-year-old former dormitory near Zenkō-ji, with a used bookstore on the ground floor. The cafe opened in December 2016. The same building hosts 成満堂 (Seimanndou), a reservation-only tonkatsu lunch counter run by Keiji Takano, a former railway company employee who retired at 50 after years of Tokyo–Nagano commuting. After retirement he completed the 88-temple Shikoku pilgrimage and trained under a veteran tonkatsu chef before opening 成満堂 roughly in 2016.
bio.specialty: ぎゅうにゅうどん (milk udon, ¥450) — the cafe's signature breakfast/morning item. At lunch, 成満堂 serves 信州産豚肉使用ロースかつ定食 (Shinshu pork loin cutlet set, ¥1,500), prepared by an initial high-temperature sear followed by extended low-temperature resting at 65–70°C for around 15 minutes to achieve optimal tenderness.
bio.philosophy: Takano has said that 'the temperature at which meat tastes best is around 67°C' — his whole method is built around patience and precise heat, rejecting the shortcuts of commercial kitchens. The cafe side embraces a nostalgic Showa-era slowness: used books, cheap sweets, and hot milk in cute mugs.

anecdotes.text: Takano initially planned to do volunteer decontamination work in Fukushima after quitting the railway. A retiring colleague took him to a renowned tonkatsu restaurant; the owner there taught him the basics on the spot and encouraged him to start his own. He then walked the 88-temple Shikoku pilgrimage before opening 成満堂.
By 2019 customer traffic had doubled year-over-year, with the clientele including students, elderly regulars, and solo female diners — an uncommon demographic mix for a tonkatsu specialist.

The building identifier on the cafe's Twitter handle (@dai298_monzen) references the address 222 (dai = 大 = big/no.1 prefix, 298 = ni-kyu-hachi) and 門前 (monzen = in front of the temple gate), nodding to its location in Zenkō-ji's monzenmachi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tonkatsu is panko-breaded and deep-fried; the fryer is shared and there is no information suggesting a dedicated gluten-free fryer or separate cooking area. Wheat contamination of all fried items should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is the default at this type of establishment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All morning sets include egg, dairy (Obuse milk), and likely katsuo/iriko dashi in miso soup. The signature milk udon uses dairy. No plant-based alternatives noted.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling procedures or allergen menu documentation found in any source reviewed. With only 13 seats and full reservation-only operation, direct inquiry by phone before visiting is strongly advisable.

positives.text: Fully reservation-only format (since May 2021) means small, controlled service — the chef prepares each piece to order and monitors cooking precisely, which allows for meaningful pre-visit allergy conversations.

caveats: Most detailed blog coverage dates from 2019–2022; the most recent Tabelog review found is June 2022. No closure notice was found, and the listing remains active with no 掲載保留 flag, but current operating status should be confirmed by phone (026-235-5477 or 080-4915-2763) before visiting. The milk udon is confirmed as a cafe specialty (¥450) via web-komachi, though it may be seasonal or subject to change. "Milk udon specialty" in the target brief aligns with the confirmed menu item ぎゅうにゅうどん.

cuisine: Soba (buckwheat noodles), Shinshu local ingredients
neighborhood: 元善町 (Zenkoji approach)
address_ja: 長野県長野市長野元善町482
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 長野県長野市大字長野元善町４８２番地 returned [138.187775, 36.659546]; ~0.17km from Zenkoji target point, well within 1.61km radius.
seats_text: Large two-floor restaurant with counter seats, low tables with cushions, and Western-style chairs
hours_raw: 10:00–15:00 daily; irregular holidays (不定休)
closed_days: Irregular (不定休) — Tripadvisor notes closed Wednesdays, Tabelog lists no fixed closed day; verify locally before visiting
cultural_comfort_note: Tripadvisor notes English menu and English-speaking staff available, making it accessible to non-Japanese visitors.
vegan_detail: The menu prominently features duck soba (鴨せいろ), herring soba (にしんそば), and tempura — no vegan-specific options advertised. Dipping broth (tsuyu) at traditional soba restaurants almost universally contains bonito flake dashi (katsuo). No vegan claim or accommodation found on any source reviewed. If visiting with a group, ask whether a simple mori soba can be served with kombu-only dashi — this is not guaranteed here.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese soba noodles typically blend wheat flour with buckwheat (not juwari/100% buckwheat); no juwari or gluten-free noodle claim found for this restaurant. Tempura is on the menu (wheat batter). Dipping tsuyu uses wheat-based soy sauce. No dedicated GF handling, separate fryer, or allergy protocol mentioned anywhere. Celiac diners should not assume safety — inquire directly and verify noodle composition before ordering.

bio.background: A longstanding soba restaurant on the shopping street (仲見世通り) leading to Zenkoji Temple, operated by a small local company (有限会社山城屋). The shop doubles as a souvenir retail space selling oyaki (stuffed buns), regional snacks, and Shinshu food products.
bio.specialty: Hand-rolled soba made with Shinshu local ingredients, served in both hot and cold forms; signature sets include golden chicken nanban soba (信州黄金シャモなんばんそば) and Shinshu salmon hand-rolled sushi paired with soba.
bio.philosophy: Emphasizes freshness and regional provenance, inviting guests to enjoy hand-made soba and dishes prepared with Shinshu (Nagano) ingredients.

anecdotes.text: Figure skating world champion Yuzuru Hanyu reportedly dined at Yamashiroya during a 2015 visit to Nagano.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is served at this restaurant, indicating wheat flour frying is happening in the kitchen. No information about a dedicated fryer or separation from soba preparation was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is made with wheat-containing soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu). No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned at this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi in traditional soba broth is typically bonito-based (katsuo dashi). Duck and herring soba are on the menu. No kombu-only or vegan dashi option is advertised.

positives.text: English menu and English-speaking staff reported available, which may help communicate dietary needs.

caveats: Hours conflict: Tabelog lists no fixed closed day (不定休), while Tripadvisor notes closed Wednesdays — verify by phone (026-232-4604) before visiting. Official site (yamashiroya-soba.jp) had a TLS certificate error and could not be fetched directly; URL confirmed from multiple third-party sources. Bio confidence is low — no founding date, owner name, or generational history could be confirmed from fetched sources.

cuisine: Soba (buckwheat noodles)
neighborhood: 元善町 (Motoyoshicho), Zenkoji approach, Nagano City
address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町486
geocode_note: GSI and Nominatim returned only city-level coordinates for this address. Coordinates estimated from known neighborhood position of 元善町 on the Zenkoji sando approach, consistent with the reported 5-minute walk from Zenkoji main hall and 588m from Zenkojishita Station. Within the target radius.
gf_detail: Soba here is nihachi (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour), so the noodles contain gluten. Tsuyu dipping broth is made with katsuo-bushi (bonito/wheat-containing soy sauce likely used). Tempura dishes (tenzaru, ebi tempura) use wheat batter. Shared cooking environment with no GF protocol confirmed. No dedicated GF options exist; celiac or wheat-allergic diners should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Broth confirmed made with katsuo-bushi (bonito flakes). All signature soba dishes (zaru, tororo, nameko) are served with this bonito dashi. Signature side dishes include tamago-yaki (egg roll). No vegan dashi alternative mentioned anywhere. Not vegan-friendly.
seats_text: 70 seats across table seating and raised tatami platform sections. Customers order via a food ticket machine at the entrance before being seated.
hours_raw: 月火水金土日 10:30〜16:00、木曜定休
closed_days: 木曜日 (Thursdays)
cultural_comfort_note: Staff do not speak foreign languages, but an English menu is available; the food ticket system at the entrance is self-explanatory, and the traditional tatami-and-table layout is straightforward for any visitor.

bio.background: Founded in Taisho 7 (1918) by a proprietor originally from Owari (present-day Aichi Prefecture) — hence the name 尾張屋. The shop began as a noodle manufacturer selling udon, then pivoted to soba as Nagano's buckwheat culture took hold. Now in its third generation under Suzuki Kenta, who took the reins despite having worked previously at a ryokan inn rather than in his family's kitchen, stepping up when no other successor was available.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture
bio.specialty: 石臼挽き二八手打ちそば — hand-made nihachi soba (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat) ground fresh on stone mills using primarily Nagano-prefecture-grown buckwheat blended with Hokkaido varieties. The dipping broth is built on carefully selected katsuo-bushi (bonito flakes). A beloved side: the shop's signature tamago-yaki (elongated egg roll served on an ice-cream stick with grated daikon), sold for ¥100.
bio.philosophy: Streamlined preparation to let the soba's natural aroma and silky throat-feel (nodo-goshi) speak for itself. The third-generation owner trimmed the menu deliberately to maintain quality with a small team, while keeping the approach path to Zenkoji steeped in over a century of flavor.

anecdotes.text: The founder came from Owari (Aichi Prefecture) and named the shop after his home region — an unusual origin for a pilgrimage-town soba house surrounded by locally rooted Nagano families.
The shop originally operated as a noodle manufacturer making udon, then switched to soba as buckwheat became the defining product of Nagano's culinary identity.
A blogger visiting in 2025 singled out the young staff member's hospitality — meticulous word choice and anticipatory service — as unexpectedly refined for a casual ticket-machine soba shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Nihachi soba contains 20% wheat flour; noodles are not gluten-free. No dedicated GF station or protocol has been reported at this restaurant.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping broth (tsuyu) is made with katsuo-bushi and almost certainly standard wheat-containing soy sauce, as is universal at traditional Japanese soba shops. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All soba broth is bonito-based. No kombu-only or vegan dashi option is indicated. Tamago-yaki (egg) is a signature side. Cross-contact with animal products throughout the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff do not speak foreign languages; no allergy-handling protocol has been documented for this restaurant. Diners with serious allergies should call ahead (026-232-5347) with a Japanese-speaking contact.

positives.text: The restaurant holds an English menu, making basic communication slightly easier for foreign visitors, though dietary restriction conversations would require Japanese.

caveats: Founding year in target brief is listed as 1914, but all sources consistently confirm Taisho 7 = 1918. Official website (sp.raqmo.com/owariya/) returned an SSL error and could not be fetched. Coordinates are estimated — GSI geocoder could not resolve street-level for 元善町486 and returned only city-level. Cash-only status is ambiguous: Tabelog and Hotpepper show e-money and QR payments accepted but no credit cards; Nagano CVB page claims major credit cards accepted — Tabelog/Hotpepper considered more reliable. cash_only set to false (non-cash options available). Payment may have changed; confirm on visit.

cuisine: Gelato bar and espresso café; handmade Italian-style gelato using Shinshu regional ingredients
address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町484
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch for 長野県長野市元善町484; coordinates land within the target box. Distance from Zenkō-ji approx 1.56km — within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 元善町 / Zenkō-ji Nakamise-dori
vegan_detail: Gelato is dairy-based (Shinshu milk is the signature ingredient); no vegan-specific options have been documented. Some fruit sorbets may be dairy-free but this is unconfirmed. Ask staff about specific flavors on the day.
gf_detail: Gelato is made without artificial additives or colorants using natural ingredients. No dedicated gluten-free labeling or certification found. Pastries and cookies on the menu contain gluten. Gelato base itself is likely wheat-free but cross-contamination from shared kitchen/display cannot be ruled out without asking staff. No dedicated fryer information available (not a fried-food venue).
hours_raw: Tue–Sun 10:30–17:00; closed Mondays. Some sources list 11:00–17:30 on weekends — hours may vary seasonally.
closed_days: Monday
seats_text: 22 seats inside a renovated Meiji-era townhouse
cultural_comfort_note: The owners are an Australian-Japanese couple; staff are described as very friendly and welcoming, and the café has a strong track record with international visitors to Zenkō-ji.

bio.background: An Australian man and his Japanese wife relocated from Sydney to Nagano in 2010, drawn by the cultural richness of the Zenkō-ji pilgrimage town. They spent 2011 studying traditional gelato-making in northern Italy before opening Caffe Terra in May 2011.
bio.specialty: Shinshu Rich Milk gelato — made with milk from the Kurohime Highlands — is the signature flavor, rotating alongside seasonal fruit varieties such as organic blueberries from Shinano Town, Kawanakajima white peaches, and Takayama village grapes.
bio.philosophy: The name 'Terra' (Italian for earth/land) reflects a commitment to honoring local ingredients; a secondary layer of wordplay nods to the Buddhist temple just steps away (tera = temple in Japanese). Gelato is made daily in-house without artificial additives or colorants, letting the quality of the ingredient speak for itself.
bio.origin: Sydney, Australia → Nagano City (2010); gelato training in northern Italy (2011)

anecdotes.text: Matt trained as a coffee roaster and blender, sourcing beans roasted in Melbourne to use in espresso drinks — an unusual choice that brings an Australian café culture sensibility to the Zenkō-ji temple approach.

The Nakamise shopping street's official directory lists Caffe Terra as 新店舗準備中 (new store in preparation), and a late-2025 Tabelog review mentioned the shop appeared to be closing soon — the current status of the original location is uncertain and should be confirmed before visiting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information found on dedicated preparation surfaces or equipment for gelato. Pastries and cookies are also served in the same space, raising the possibility of shared utensils or cross-contact. Confirm with staff before ordering if celiac-level safety is required.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Gelato and espresso drinks are the core menu; soy sauce is not a component. However, pastries and cookies on the rotating menu will contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free labeling has been documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard gelato is dairy-based (Shinshu milk is the signature ingredient). No dairy-free or vegan gelato options have been confirmed. Fruit sorbets may be dairy-free but this has not been verified in any source.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Owners are internationally experienced (Australian-Japanese) and accustomed to a wide range of customers; communication in basic English is likely possible. No formal allergy protocol has been documented — ask directly about specific flavors and ingredients on the day of your visit.

positives.text: Gelato is made in-house daily without artificial additives, colorants, or preservatives, using identifiable natural ingredients — a positive baseline for allergen transparency.

caveats: STATUS UNCERTAIN: The Zenkō-ji Nakamise official shopping-street directory lists this shop as 新店舗準備中 (new store in preparation), and a November 2025 Tabelog review noted it appeared to be closing soon. The Tabelog listing itself does not show a formal 閉店 or 掲載保留 flag as of this research, and the shop appears in current aggregator listings as open. Strongly recommend calling ahead (026-217-5541) or checking Instagram (@caffe_terra_nagano) before visiting. Hours also vary across sources (some show 10:30–17:00, others 11:00–17:30 on weekends) — treat all hours as approximate until confirmed directly.

cuisine: French Bistro, Curry, Café
neighborhood: 大門町 (Daimon-cho), Nagano City
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町67-1
geocode_note: Chō-level geocode via geocoding.jp returned 36.656614, 138.187707 for 長野市大門町67-1. GSI AddressSearch returned unrelated 大岡 results; geocoding.jp used as fallback. Location is ~540m from Zenkō-ji, well within 1.61km radius.
vegan_detail: Standard French bistro menu centered on meat and fish — lunch sets feature chicken or salmon; à la carte includes beef curry, hamburger steak, hayashi rice, and gratin. No vegan offerings or plant-based alternatives documented anywhere. Bread accompanies most sets (likely contains eggs/dairy). Call ahead to ask if anything can be made without animal products; do not assume.
gf_detail: French bistro cuisine with no documented gluten-free accommodations. Standard menu items (hayashi rice, curry, gratin, bread) typically contain wheat flour. Sauces and roux in French/Western cooking are conventionally wheat-based. No dedicated GF options, fryer information, or allergy-handling policy found in any source. Celiac diners should call ahead in Japanese; meaningful GF accommodation is not expected here.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular; no fixed weekly closure day listed)
seats_text: Seating capacity not published; interior of a registered historic brick building — intimate setting with stained glass windows.
cultural_comfort_note: Operated by a local city-development corporation; all menus and signage are in Japanese, and no English-language accommodation is documented — basic Japanese or a translation app is advisable.

bio.background: 楽茶れんが館 occupies a registered tangible cultural property: a red brick Western-style building completed in April 1912 (Meiji 45) as the headquarters of the Shinano Chugyu Baba Transportation Company, one of the oldest Western-style brick structures in Nagano's Zenkoji temple precinct. Over the decades it served as the Zenkoji Post Office (1959–1989) and then as Nagano City's local products hall (1991–2002) before being renovated into its current form as a restaurant and café. The building was formally designated a national registered tangible cultural property in 2007. It is managed by Machizukuri Nagano Co., Ltd., a city-development organisation.
bio.specialty: Slow-simmered special beef curry (特製ビーフカレー), served with salad and fukujin pickles — the dish that draws most repeat visitors. Dinner full-course by Chef Kitamura (minimum 6 guests, ¥12,000) is the flagship experience.
bio.philosophy: A calm, unhurried retreat in a historically significant space — the emphasis is on thoughtful French-influenced cooking inside architecture that tells the story of Nagano's modernisation era.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture

anecdotes.text: The 1912 brick building originally housed a horse-and-cattle transport company (信濃中牛馬合資会社), making it one of the few surviving Meiji-era Western brick structures in the Zenkoji approach area. Its stained glass windows are a particular draw for visitors who stumble in expecting an ordinary café.

Chef Hideo Kitamura spent 18 years as executive chef at Okushiga Kogen Hotel before taking the kitchen here; he also holds a concurrent position at Contemporary in Karuizawa, lending the dinner course a resort-level pedigree unusual for a neighbourhood bistro.
The slow-simmered beef curry — just ¥1,100 at lunch — gradually builds heat as you eat, making it a beloved everyday option for locals and pilgrims walking the Zenkoji approach.

caveats: Hours (10:00–21:00) are from the official Machizukuri Nagano site but multiple aggregators show variations (10:00–20:00; one temporary COVID-era 10:00–18:00 reduction). Confirm by phone before visiting. Cash only confirmed via Tabelog. Dinner full-course requires minimum 6 guests and advance reservation. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations are documented in any source reviewed.

lead: 西洋料理店 もりたろう
name_ja: 西洋料理店 もりたろう
cuisine: Western / Pasta / Pizza — Shinshu pork specialties
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野東町125-3 ぱてぃお大門
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 長野県長野市大字長野東町125-3 returned 138.188934, 36.655315 — approx 694m from Zenkō-ji, well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 東町・ぱてぃお大門
seats_text: 7-seat intimate counter/table room inside a restored Edo-period warehouse at Patio Daimon
closed_days: 月曜定休（月曜祝日の場合は翌火曜休）
cultural_comfort_note: No English signage or menu confirmed; ordering is via QR code in Japanese — a few words of Japanese or pointing will get you through.
vegan_detail: Menu centres on Shinshu pork, pasta (likely egg-based), and pizza with dairy. No vegan menu or labelling found. Hidden animal ingredients likely throughout: miso-based sauces, butter, cream. Inquire directly, but expect very limited options.
gf_detail: Western restaurant serving pasta, pizza, and pork steak — wheat is central to most dishes. No gluten-free menu or labelling found on any source. Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) almost certainly used in marinades. No dedicated fryer information available. Celiacs should contact the restaurant (026-237-3939) before visiting; dining safely here will be very difficult without advance coordination.

bio.background: Moritaro sits tucked inside Patio Daimon (ぱてぃお大門), a beautifully restored cluster of Edo-period kura (storehouses) a short walk from Zenkō-ji Temple. The complex — described as a hidden alley with a small warm light glowing at its end — was renovated to house artisan shops, galleries, and a handful of intimate restaurants. Moritaro occupies one of those kura spaces, its calm, dimly lit interior drawing largely a local adult clientele, especially women, who return for the carefully made, generous plates.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture
bio.specialty: 信州特選ポーク香草ステーキ (Shinshu Premium Pork Herb Steak, 250g) — thick-cut Shinshu Orein pork seared with aromatic herbs and finished with olive oil; described as exceptionally tender with a clean, light finish despite its richness.
bio.philosophy: Handmade Western cooking built on Northern Shinshu (北信濃) ingredients, touched by the seasons. The kitchen is small and output deliberate — generous portions, careful craft, no hurry.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant appeared on TBS's popular travel-food programme バナナマンのせっかくグルメ (Banana Man's Sekkaku Gourme) on 31 July 2022, bringing regional and national attention to the Shinshu pork herb steak. By 2026 the dining room was filling to capacity by 11:30 AM on weekdays, with a waitlist forming before noon.
A 2026 visitor noted that despite the tiny 7-seat room, dishes arrived within about ten minutes of ordering via QR code — a pace that keeps the intimate space feeling unhurried rather than cramped.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Pasta and pizza are core menu items — wheat flour is used throughout the kitchen. Cross-contamination risk for celiacs is high and no source mentions any separation protocols.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Marinades for the pork steak and miso-based dishes likely use standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Butter, cream, and meat stock are expected throughout; the kitchen is not set up for plant-based cooking and no vegan accommodation is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen chart, or documented allergy-handling procedure found across Tabelog, official site, or any blog. Phone enquiry (026-237-3939) before visiting is strongly recommended for any dietary restriction.

positives.text: Credit cards accepted (VISA, Master, JCB); wheelchair accessible with no steps; non-smoking; children and strollers welcome — broadly accessible venue even if dietary accommodation is unconfirmed.

caveats: Geocode resolved to chō-level (大字長野東町) rather than exact lot, placing the pin ~694m from Zenkō-ji. No allergen menu or gluten-free documentation found on any source; all dietary safety ratings are conservative estimates. Chef/owner name not publicly documented. Hours and closed-day rules confirmed across multiple sources but should be verified before visiting.

name_ja: MACCHA HOUSE 抹茶館 善光寺仲見世通り店
cuisine: 抹茶スイーツカフェ
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野元善町482
geocode_note: GSI address-search matched 長野県長野市大字長野元善町４８２番地 at [138.187775, 36.659546]; ~212m from Zenkoji temple — well within target radius.
neighborhood: 善光寺仲見世通り / 善光寺下駅
seats_text: 37席（1F・2F）、2Fの窓側カウンターから善光寺を望める
hours_raw: 10:00〜19:00（ラストオーダー閉店30分前）、不定休
closed_days: 不定休
cultural_comfort_note: A tourist-facing chain café on the main Zenkoji approach street; signage and ordering are straightforward even without Japanese, and the venue regularly serves international visitors.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options or labeling anywhere on the menu or official site. Signature items contain wheat: tiramisu uses mascarpone cream layered on sponge/ladyfinger (wheat), and no dedicated fryer is indicated. Cross-contamination risk is high in a small café kitchen. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around dairy-heavy desserts: tiramisu uses mascarpone, soft-serve ice cream is dairy, matcha lattes use milk, and red bean sweets may contain egg. No plant-milk substitutes or vegan claims are mentioned on the official site or any review source consulted.

bio.background: MACCHA HOUSE is a matcha specialty chain that opened its first Japan location in Kyoto Kawaramachi in May 2015. The Zenkoji Nakamise-dori branch, the brand's first Nagano Prefecture outpost, opened on May 1, 2023, taking over the space formerly occupied by soba restaurant Momotose. The brand has since grown to roughly a dozen locations across Japan plus one in Singapore.
bio.specialty: 宇治抹茶のティラミス — a silky mascarpone tiramisu dusted with premium matcha powder, sourced from Morihan, a Kyoto tea house operating since the early 19th century. Also notable: concentrated matcha soft-serve and seasonal matcha shaved ice.
bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan
bio.philosophy: Using only carefully selected, highly fragrant matcha chosen by skilled Kyoto tea masters from Morihan, to create sweets and beverages that showcase the depth of Japanese tea culture for a wide audience.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies the former site of the beloved local soba restaurant Momotose, and the transition was noted with some nostalgia by Nagano residents even as they welcomed the new matcha destination.

Second-floor counter seats that face the window give diners a direct view toward Zenkoji Temple while they sip their matcha lattes — a detail that has made the spot popular for solo visitors on temple pilgrimage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination controls is published. The kitchen produces tiramisu (wheat-containing sponge/ladyfinger), soft serve, and shaved ice in a compact two-floor café setup — shared equipment is very likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce is used in a matcha dessert menu, but wheat appears structurally in tiramisu and parfait components (sponge cake / ladyfingers). No allergen sheet is published online.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy is pervasive across the menu (mascarpone, soft-serve ice cream, milk for lattes). No non-dairy alternatives are offered, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official store page states only 'for details, please inquire with staff' — no written allergen matrix or dedicated allergy protocol is described.

caveats: Hours listed as 10:00-19:00 daily with irregular closures (不定休) — verify before visiting. No allergen matrix published; wheat and dairy are structural to the entire menu. Chain brand, not an independent local operation.

cuisine: 古民家カフェ・和食定食・かき氷
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字鶴賀権堂町2390-1
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch returned coordinates for 長野県長野市大字鶴賀権堂町２３９０番地. Distance from Zenkoji reference point (36.6614, 138.1872) is approximately 853m — within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 権堂町 / 権堂駅徒歩圏
seats_text: Counter, table, and sofa seating for about 16 guests in a compact renovated old-house space.
closed_days: 水曜・木曜（祝日の場合は営業の場合あり）
cultural_comfort_note: No English-language presence; menus, Instagram, and all signage are Japanese-only, but the casual counter atmosphere is welcoming to anyone who can point and order.
vegan_detail: The daily rotating set meal (一汁八菜) is built around washoku fundamentals — miso soup, dashi-simmered vegetables, and egg dishes — making hidden bonito dashi (かつおだし) very likely. One blog noted a shirasu (whitebait) egg omelette among side dishes. No vegan-designated menu exists. Plant-based diners should ask in advance; partial accommodation may be possible given the wide spread of vegetable small dishes, but the kitchen has made no documented vegan commitment.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or protocol documented anywhere. Regular soy sauce (wheat-containing) is almost certainly used in the washoku-style set meals and marinades. Tempura (wheat batter) appears as a side dish. One source noted pumpkin karaage uses potato starch and rice flour, which is an incidental GF-friendly coating, but this is not offered as a GF dish. The kitchen has not published any cross-contamination controls. Celiac diners should not eat here without direct confirmation from the kitchen.

bio.background: Yamada-san trained at a formal Japanese restaurant in Shinjuku, Tokyo, honing professional washoku technique before returning to Nagano to open his own place.
bio.origin: Opened March 20, 2018 in a renovated old machiya townhouse in the Gondo shopping district near Zenkoji.
bio.specialty: Year-round kakigori (shaved ice) and a daily-changing one-soup, eight-side-dish Japanese set meal built around Nagano Prefecture seasonal vegetables.
bio.philosophy: Yamada-san's core conviction is that winter is actually the best season for kakigori — he ate extensively through Tokyo's shaved-ice scene, became convinced that the scarcity of year-round kakigori in Nagano was a gap worth filling, and built the cafe around that idea alongside serious washoku lunches.

anecdotes.text: Ice is temperature-managed to approximately -8°C on the surface before shaving, producing a notably soft, airy texture rather than the coarse crunch of typical machine ice.
Lunch reservations for the 11:30 seating fill quickly; the daily menu is announced each morning only on Instagram Stories, making spontaneous visits a small adventure.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No cross-contamination controls have been documented. The kitchen produces tempura (wheat batter) and other wheat-containing dishes alongside everything else, with no published separation procedures.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce is used throughout the washoku set meals, marinades (e.g., ginger pork shogayaki), and dipping preparations. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce has been mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi-simmered vegetable dishes and miso soup are core to every set meal; bonito dashi (katsuo) is standard in washoku cooking and is not flagged as absent. Egg dishes (tamagoyaki, shirasu omelette) are regularly listed among the rotating side dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One reviewer noted that staff accommodated a request to reduce rice and miso soup portions and offered refills on request, suggesting general flexibility, but no allergy-specific protocol has been documented.

positives.text: The pumpkin karaage side dish uses potato starch and rice flour as its coating — incidentally gluten-free frying, though not offered as a dedicated GF item.
The set meal format naturally includes many vegetable-based small dishes, giving partial accommodation more room than a single-dish restaurant would.

caveats: Hours vary by day and the owner posts the daily menu only to Instagram Stories each morning — confirmed hours should be checked against @polka.cafe2390 before visiting. Closed days may shift when national holidays fall on Wednesday or Thursday. No allergen menu exists; all dietary questions require direct contact with the kitchen at 026-225-9197.

lead: パスタと自然派ワイン こまつや
name_ja: パスタと自然派ワイン こまつや
cuisine: イタリアン・パスタ・自然派ワイン
address_ja: 長野県長野市長野西之門町500-8
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 長野県長野市大字長野西之門町500番地. Approximately 344m southwest of Zenko-ji — well within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 西之門町 / 善光寺門前
seats_text: 約10席。大きな木のカウンターとテーブル席。カウンターからオーナーの調理が見える。予約必須。
hours_raw: ランチ 11:30〜15:00（LO 14:30）。ディナー 18:00〜22:00（LO 21:00）、金土日祝のみ通常ディナー営業。平日ディナーは4名以上のパーティーのみ対応。定休日：毎週水曜、第2・第4火曜（または第2・第4木曜。要確認）。
closed_days: 毎週水曜、第2・第4火曜（情報源により第2・第4木曜とも）。要電話確認。
cultural_comfort_note: 英語メニューあり、スタッフが少し英語対応可との口コミあり。ただし小さな家族経営店のため基本は日本語。
vegan_detail: 有機野菜の前菜盛り合わせや野菜系パスタが季節によりあり得るが、メニューの主軸は肉・魚介のパスタと乳製品（マスカルポーネ、ゴルゴンゾーラ等）。明示的なヴィーガンメニューは存在しない。大豆ミルクのオーガニックコーヒーがTripAdvisor口コミで言及されている。食事制限がある場合は事前に電話で確認を。
gf_detail: 信州産地粉の手打ちパスタが看板メニューであり、小麦が料理の中心。グルテンフリーメニューや代替パスタの情報は一切確認できない。フォカッチャも提供しており、グルテン回避は事実上不可能。腹腔病・重篤な小麦アレルギーの方には適さない。

bio.chef_name: 廣政真也（オーナーシェフ）・綾（パートナー、デザート・サービス担当）
bio.background: 山口県出身の廣政真也氏は、かつてインディーズバンドのドラマーとして全国ツアーをこなし、CDもリリースするミュージシャンだった。その後、D&DEPARTMENTの東京・大阪のカフェで修業し、「ロングライフデザイン」の哲学——流行を追わず質と誠実さで長く愛される場をつくるという考え方——を吸収した。パートナーの綾さんの家族が長野市西之門町で営んでいた江戸時代創業の荒物雑貨店「小松屋」の屋号と古民家を引き継ぎ、2009年にイタリアンレストランとしてオープン。軒先には今もツバメの巣が残る。
bio.origin: 長野市西之門町（善光寺門前裏通り）
bio.specialty: 信州産有機野菜と地粉を使った手打ちパスタ。日替わりで肉・魚介・月替わりの3種類を提供。パチェリにゴルゴンゾーラクリームソースと旬野菜を合わせた一皿が代表作のひとつ。
bio.philosophy: 「できる限り自然に近い食材を」がテーマ。信州産の有機野菜・牧草豚・天然魚介を積極的に使用し、ワインは全て自然派ワイン（長野産を中心）のみ扱う。子供が生まれてからさらにオーガニックへの意識が深まったという。平日客の約80%が地元の常連客であり、「家の台所」のような雰囲気を目指している。
roles: 自然派ワインキュレーター

anecdotes.text: 店内には「こまつや図書」と呼ばれる本の貸し出しコーナーがあり、デザインや料理の本が並ぶ。小布施の「まちじゅう図書館」に触発されたもので、本を介して客同士の会話が生まれることを意図している。
廣政氏はバンド時代の後、D&DEPARTMENTでの修業を経て「本物のおいしさ」を追求するようになり、自然派ワインと有機食材への傾倒が深まっていった。
「長野ワインを扱うのはオープン当初から地産地消にこだわっていたので自然な流れでした」と廣政氏。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 手打ちパスタ（小麦）が厨房の中心であり、調理環境全体に小麦粉が使用されている。グルテン交差汚染を避けることは不可能とみられる。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油の使用有無は不明。イタリアン料理のため通常は醤油を多用しないが、信州味噌を使用するとの情報がある（味噌は小麦を含む製品もあり）。アレルギーがある場合は事前確認が必要。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 豚肉（自家製パンチェッタ）・魚介・乳製品（マスカルポーネ、ゴルゴンゾーラ）が同じ厨房で調理される。動物性食材との接触を避けることは困難。

staff_allergy_handling.text: TripAdvisorの口コミによると、メニューに記載のないソーセージをパスタから省くなど、リクエストに応じた変更対応の実績がある。ただし体系的なアレルギー対応は未確認。

positives.text: 信州産有機野菜を積極使用。自然派ワインのみ扱い。添加物・加工食品を使わない素材本位の料理姿勢。有機オリーブオイルと自家製パンを提供。

caveats: 定休日が情報源により「第2・第4火曜」と「第2・第4木曜」で食い違う。訪問前に必ず電話確認を（026-235-4040）。ディナーの通常営業は金土日祝のみで、平日ディナーは4名以上のグループ予約のみ。現金払いはTabelog記載だが、Rettyにはd払い・PayPay記載あり。公式サイトが403エラーで取得できなかったため最新情報は直接確認を。

lead: とんかつ専門店 からり
name_ja: とんかつ専門店 からり
cuisine: とんかつ・丼
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野大門町44-1 大門町フォレスト1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI address search on 大門町44番地; coordinates place the restaurant approximately 720 m south of Zenkoji main hall, well within the 1.61 km target radius.
neighborhood: 大字長野・権堂エリア（善光寺門前）
seats_text: Counter, tables, and raised platform seating
closed_days: 木曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A small, serious tonkatsu specialist near Zenkoji with no English signage or menu noted; staff are described as warm and attentive, but communication will be in Japanese.
vegan_detail: Entirely centered on pork — tonkatsu, pork loin sets, and fried shrimp. No plant-based mains exist. Miso soup is served as standard. Nothing on the menu or any source suggests a vegan option.
gf_detail: Tonkatsu is breaded with wheat flour and panko (wheat breadcrumbs) — wheat is the core of the product, not an incidental ingredient. Miso soup typically contains wheat-based shoyu. Fried shrimp shares oil. No source mentions any GF accommodation, alternative breading, or allergy protocol. Celiac or severe wheat-allergic diners cannot safely eat here.

bio.background: Karari opened on 8 August 2019 in a ground-floor space of the Daimoncho Forest building, a short walk from Zenkoji temple's main gate. The name 'からり' is a Japanese onomatopoeia for a light, crisp sound — evoking the ideal tonkatsu fry.
bio.origin: 長野市大門町、善光寺門前
bio.specialty: Thick-cut loin cutlet made with 'Wapork Mochipork' (和豚もち豚), a branded domestic pork prized for its sweet-flavored fat and mochi-like texture. The restaurant's motto is '塩だけで旨い' — delicious with just salt — reflecting confidence that the meat needs no sauce to shine.
bio.philosophy: Quality ingredient selection over elaboration: premium domestic pork sourced from the Wapork Mochipork brand, rice from northern Nagano described as a rare local variety, and a simple preparation philosophy that lets the pork speak for itself.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers frequently note that staff proactively offer unlimited refills of rice, miso soup, and cabbage — a generous touch at a restaurant in the ¥1,000–¥2,000 lunch range.
One reviewer described the mochipork loin as 'soft enough to eat without teeth,' capturing the unusually tender texture that the branded pork is known for.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tonkatsu is coated in wheat flour and panko; wheat is intrinsic to the dish, not a cross-contamination risk — it is the primary allergen present. No dedicated fryer for GF items exists or is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen breakdown is published. Standard Japanese miso soup and sauces served alongside tonkatsu routinely contain wheat-based shoyu; no GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork is fried in the same oil used for all items including shrimp. No plant-based items are on the menu, so vegan cross-contact is moot — the menu is entirely animal-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No source mentions any allergy inquiry protocol or staff training in allergen handling. The restaurant does not appear to have published any allergen chart or accommodation policy.

caveats: Hours from Tabelog and official site differ slightly (Tabelog: 14:30 / 20:00 close; official site: 15:00 / 20:30 close) — last order is 30 min before closing per official site, so effective eating window may be longer than Tabelog indicates. Verify directly before visiting. No chef/owner identity confirmed from any public source.

cuisine: さつまいもスイーツ・食べ歩き
address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町484 善光寺仲見世通り
geocode_note: GSI returned city-level coordinates only (138.194824, 36.648537). Coordinates estimated from 元善町 / 善光寺仲見世通り context: the street runs at approximately 36.6599, 138.1876, confirmed by multiple mapping sources placing 仲見世通り at 36.659895, 138.187587. Address 484 is on this approach to Zenkoji, well within the 1.61 km target radius (~180 m from Zenkoji gate).
neighborhood: 元善町 / 善光寺仲見世通り
seats_text: Primarily a walk-up takeout stall on Nakamise-dori; limited or no eat-in seating. The official store page lists takeout format.
hours_raw: 平日・土日・祝祭日 10:30〜16:30、不定休
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular — check Instagram @oimoyasan2022 before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: Walk-up street stall with no staff interaction required beyond ordering; no English menus confirmed, but items are visible and pointing suffices.
vegan_detail: Many items are plant-based by nature — roasted sweet potato (熟成焼き芋), candied sweet potato (大学芋/蜜大学芋), and imo-kenpi chips are typically vegan. Gelato (モンブランジェラート) uses Shinshu dairy milk, so is not vegan. Sweet potato shakes likely contain milk. Savory kenpi flavors include Shinshu miso (麦麹 barley-koji miso is common — ask) and chili. No dedicated vegan labeling; select items carefully and confirm with staff.
gf_detail: Sweet potatoes themselves are GF, and simple preparations (roasted sweet potato, plain daigaku-imo, plain kenpi) are likely GF. However, Shinshu miso seasoning on kenpi may contain barley; gelato/shake base ingredients are not confirmed GF. No dedicated GF labeling, no allergen chart available online. Cross-contamination from shared preparation surfaces unknown. Celiacs should ask before ordering any seasoned or blended item.

bio.background: 信州おいも工房 opened in March 2022 on Zenkoji's historic Nakamise-dori approach street in Nagano City. It is operated by おいも日和 (Oimo Biyori), a small Nagano-based company whose flagship store is in Azumino. The shop was conceived as a way to bring locally grown Shinshu sweet potatoes directly to visitors of one of Japan's most famous temples.
bio.specialty: Ultra-thin imo-kenpi (善光寺ケンピ) in distinctive Shinshu flavors such as Hachimanya Isogoro chili and Shinshu miso, plus honey-glazed sweet potato (蜜大学芋), roasted sweet potato shakes, and dual-cream monblanc gelato (蜜芋ふたいろ♪モンブランジェラート).
bio.philosophy: The company cultivates sweet potatoes from the soil up — starting from their own farm and partnering with contract growers across northern Nagano — and ages harvested potatoes for 60 or more days to concentrate their natural sweetness before any product is made.

anecdotes.text: The owner hand-built custom machinery to slice kenpi to an extraordinary 3 mm thickness — far thinner than typical imo-kenpi — giving the snacks a distinctive crisp texture that became the shop's calling card.
Opened on March 19, 2022, the shop was positioned on Nakamise-dori specifically to bring farm-fresh Shinshu sweet potato sweets to the steady flow of pilgrims and tourists visiting Zenkoji.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen chart or cross-contamination information is available online. The shop uses shared preparation equipment; dedicated fryer status is unknown. Celiacs cannot assume safety without asking staff directly.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce-based items identified on the menu. Shinshu miso seasoning used on some kenpi flavors may contain barley (麦麹), which is not GF. Confirm before ordering miso-flavored items.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Gelato and shakes are made with Shinshu dairy milk and are prepared at the same counter as plant-based items. No information on dedicated utensils for vegan preparation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information found about English-language allergy communication or dedicated allergy protocols. The shop is a small walk-up stall; allergy inquiries in Japanese are advisable before ordering.

positives.text: Core menu items — roasted sweet potato (熟成焼き芋) and plain honey daigaku-imo — are single-ingredient preparations with no obvious gluten sources, making them among the safer choices for those avoiding wheat.
Sweet potatoes are naturally gluten-free and the shop's entire concept is built around them, meaning the majority of menu items start from a GF-compatible base ingredient.

caveats: Payment: Tabelog lists card/e-money accepted, but one 2022 opening-period source noted cash only — the Tabelog data is treated as more current. Hours are confirmed as 10:30–16:30 daily (including weekends/holidays) but closure days are irregular (不定休); verify via Instagram @oimoyasan2022 before visiting. Geocoordinates are estimated from street context (元善町 / 仲見世通り) because GSI returned only city-level precision for this address. No allergen information is published by the shop.

cuisine: 郷土料理 — おやき・豆菓子 (regional Nagano oyaki and bean confections)
address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町483
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned city-level coordinates for 長野県長野市; address is on the Zenkoji Nakamise-dori approach road (元善町), which is well within 1.61km of the temple. Coordinates fall within target bounding box.
neighborhood: Zenkoji Nakamise-dori (善光寺仲見世通り), Nagano City
seats_text: No indoor seating listed; this is a storefront walk-up shop selling oyaki and bean confections to take away. Customers queue at the counter.
hours_raw: 09:00–17:00 daily; no regular closing days (無休). Occasional Wednesday closures have been noted (e.g., specific Wednesdays in June on their online shop).
closed_days: No regular closing day; occasional ad-hoc closures announced on their online shop.
cultural_comfort_note: A street-side takeaway counter with no English signage noted; staff interaction is minimal (point and pay), but ordering is straightforward as oyaki are displayed and priced clearly.
vegan_detail: Several oyaki fillings are vegetable-based (nozawana pickled greens, eggplant, kabocha, mushroom medley, mixed vegetables) and may be vegan in filling, but the dough is standard wheat-flour based and soy sauce is used in seasonings — hidden animal-derived ingredients (bonito dashi in soy sauce or fillings) cannot be ruled out without staff confirmation. The signature negi-miso cheese oyaki contains dairy. No vegan labeling or certification.
gf_detail: Oyaki dough is made with wheat flour (小麦粉) as a primary ingredient — this is a wheat-based product by definition. No gluten-free options are offered or mentioned. Not safe for celiac or gluten-intolerant diners.

bio.background: つち茂 (Tsuchimo) is a Nagano landmark established in the Edo period (1700s), now in its 18th generation of the founding family. Originally a souvenir and bean confectionery shop on the Zenkoji Nakamise-dori pilgrimage approach, the shop added freshly baked oyaki to its lineup roughly 30 years ago during the Heisei era, which has since become its calling card.
bio.specialty: Freshly baked oyaki — especially the signature negi-miso cheese (ねぎ味噌チーズ) variety, grilled on an iron griddle and served hot at the counter. Traditional nozawana-filled oyaki is also a staple.
bio.philosophy: A three-part small business — souvenir shop, bean merchant, and oyaki bakery — that has served pilgrims and visitors to Zenkoji for roughly three centuries, balancing tradition with crowd-pleasing modern flavors like negi-miso cheese.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture

anecdotes.text: The shop is currently run by the 18th-generation proprietor, making it one of the longest continuously operated family businesses on the Zenkoji approach road.
Tsuchimo's shop mascot is a dog named Kentaro (健太郎), who features on their packaging and online shop — a charming detail that regulars look out for.

The negi-miso cheese oyaki, priced around ¥230–¥250, is described by visitors as unexpectedly rich with melted cheese inside a chewy wheat dough — a modern twist that draws queues alongside the traditional nozawana variety.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oyaki dough is wheat-flour based by recipe — this is not a cross-contamination risk but a fundamental ingredient issue. The product is not suitable for anyone avoiding gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is used in oyaki fillings (confirmed in general oyaki ingredient descriptions for similar Nagano shops); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No allergen segregation information is available. Multiple fillings are prepared in the same shop including dairy-containing (cheese) and fish-based seasonings. Cross-contact between fillings cannot be excluded.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy handling protocol, or dietary labeling is listed on the shop's website or any review source. Staff interaction is minimal at this walk-up counter format. Diners with serious allergies should contact the shop directly before visiting.

positives.text: Vegetable-filled oyaki varieties (nozawana, eggplant, kabocha, mushroom) are plant-based in their primary filling ingredients, which may suit some dietary preferences — but full ingredient disclosure is not publicly available.

caveats: Geocoordinates are city-level precision from GSI; the actual shop is on Zenkoji Nakamise-dori (元善町), confirmed within the 1.61km target radius. Hours listed as 09:00-17:00 daily with occasional ad-hoc Wednesday closures; verify current status before visiting. No seat count available — this is a walk-up takeaway counter. Payment: cards not accepted, but electronic money (IC cards) and QR code payments are accepted — not strictly cash-only.

lead: トラットリア ディベルテンテ
name_ja: トラットリア ディベルテンテ
cuisine: Italian (Shinshu local ingredients, gibier/game meat, Neapolitan-style pizza)
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野横沢町717
neighborhood: Zenkōji-shita / Yokosawa-cho
geocode_note: GSI address-search matched 長野県長野市大字長野横沢町７１７番地 at [138.185715, 36.66267]; 194m from Zenkō-ji center, well within 1.61km radius; inside target lat/lng box.
seats_text: 24 seats; counter with pizza oven view, table seating; wheelchair/stroller accessible; 4 parking spaces
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:30 (LO 14:00), Dinner 17:30–20:30 (LO 20:00); closed Wednesdays; hours may vary — confirm before visiting
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: English service status is unknown; the restaurant is a small owner-operated trattoria where interaction is primarily in Japanese, though a warm, casual atmosphere makes it approachable.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan options are listed. The menu features Italian cuisine focused on Shinshu local produce, venison, pork (Shinshu Taro Pork), and cheese-topped pizzas. Pasta dishes may use egg-based noodles and meat-based sauces. Dairy (cream cheese pizza, quiche) and meat are central to the menu. Possible to ask about vegetable-forward pasta dishes given the strong seasonal-produce focus, but nothing is advertised as vegan.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options are advertised. The kitchen uses a blend of Shinshu wheat flour and Italian imported flour for its Neapolitan pizzas, and standard pasta is served. No dedicated GF menu or GF claims found across any source. The restaurant is a general Italian kitchen — wheat is central. Those with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; cross-contamination risk is high given active pizza and pasta production.

bio.background: Owner-chef Iwata Shunsuke spent over 14 years working at hotels in Nagano Prefecture, building expertise across French, Italian, pastry and bread-making, teppanyaki, and banquet cuisine. He opened Trattoria DiverTente in June 2019 as his independent venture, choosing Italian as the vehicle for an accessible, casual neighborhood restaurant near Zenkō-ji Temple's western gate.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture, Japan
bio.specialty: Shinshu-sourced venison (gibier) dishes and Neapolitan-style pizza made with a proprietary blend of local Shinshu wheat and Italian imported flour
bio.philosophy: Iwata's guiding principle is 'approachable × enjoyable × delicious.' He intentionally avoids rigid recipes, shaping each dish around whatever seasonal Shinshu ingredients are at their peak — zucchini and wild vegetables in spring/summer, eggplant and sweet potatoes in autumn. The restaurant name 'Divertente' means 'fun' in Italian, and reflects his goal that dining here should feel relaxed and joyful for everyone.

anecdotes.text: Iwata partners with a local licensed meat-processing facility to source properly handled venison, bringing the same care to his 200g+ venison hamburger (enriched with aromatic vegetables and olive oil) and venison rump steak (served with a mixed berry sauce of currants and blackberries) as he would to fine-hotel banquet cookery.

The restaurant sits one minute's walk from Zenkō-ji Temple's rear gate, in a distinctive white-and-green building with an Italian flag — a quiet alternative to the main Omotesandō shopping street that many visitors never find.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls. Active pizza and pasta production means wheat flour is in continuous use throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Italian cuisine focus means soy sauce is unlikely in main dishes, but no explicit confirmation that wheat-based soy sauce is absent from all preparations. Confirm with staff for any dish that may use Japanese-inflected seasoning (e.g., the Shinshu miso pizza).

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (cream cheese, quiche) and meat (venison, pork) are core menu components; no segregated vegan prep surfaces are mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or trained allergy protocol has been described in any source reviewed. Phone reservation (026-466-6650) is the recommended channel to discuss dietary needs before visiting.

positives.text: Strong seasonal, locally sourced produce ethos — the owner explicitly shapes menus around available vegetables, which may allow for ad-hoc vegetable-forward dishes when asked.

caveats: Hours vary and the restaurant has operated different schedules across sources (some sources list dinner LO as 20:00, others as 22:00; one source listed Monday/Tuesday/Wednesday as closed days during a specific period). Always phone ahead (026-466-6650) to confirm current hours and availability, particularly for dinner. The official website (trattoria-divertente.com) was unreachable at time of research — cross-referenced multiple secondary sources instead.

cuisine: 懐石・日本料理
address_ja: 長野県長野市東之門町2502 城山公園地内
geocode_note: Coordinates from geocoding.jp for 東之門町2502; approximately 410m from Zenkoji Temple, well within the 1.61km radius. GSI address-search did not return a precise match for 東之門町; geocoding.jp returned lat 36.659335, lng 138.191017.
neighborhood: 善光寺下・城山公園
seats_text: 73席（カウンター含む）。大広間は50〜100名、中広間は10〜30名収容の宴会スペースあり。
closed_days: 水曜日（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: English menus are available according to the Nagano City tourism board, and the restaurant regularly serves tourists visiting Zenkoji Temple.
vegan_detail: Traditional kaiseki cuisine — nearly every course will use dashi made from katsuobushi (bonito flakes) or niboshi, and soy sauce containing wheat. No vegan menu is offered. Guests with strict plant-based requirements should contact the restaurant well in advance; accommodation is uncertain.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese kaiseki relies heavily on wheat-based soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) in marinades, sauces, and seasoning throughout the meal. Dashi is typically fish-based. No gluten-free menu or dedicated GF handling is advertised. Cross-contamination risk is high in a traditional kaiseki kitchen. Guests with celiac disease should call ahead, and even then safe accommodation cannot be assumed.

bio.background: Founded in Meiji 38 (1905) within Shiroyama Park, a hilltop park adjacent to Zenkoji Temple, 萬佳亭 is one of Nagano city's oldest surviving ryotei. The restaurant's name is rooted in a piece of local lore: when Emperor Meiji surveyed the Zenkoji Plain during his provincial tour, he reportedly exclaimed 「絶景かな」(what magnificent scenery), and the establishment took its spirit from that moment of imperial wonder.
bio.origin: 長野市東之門町・城山公園（明治38年創業）
bio.specialty: 四季の信州食材を用いた山楼海鮮懐石
bio.philosophy: Seasonal kaiseki showcasing the bounty of Shinshu (Nagano Prefecture) — mountain and sea ingredients brought together in refined multi-course presentation, served with panoramic views of the Zenkoji Plain.

anecdotes.text: The name 萬佳亭 derives from Emperor Meiji's exclamation of wonder at the view of the Zenkoji Plain during a regional tour; the restaurant has traded on that panoramic legacy since 1905.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling information is published. A traditional kaiseki kitchen uses wheat-based soy sauce throughout multiple courses; cross-contamination is a structural risk rather than an exception.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese kaiseki cuisine uses koikuchi soy sauce (wheat-containing) in marinades, dipping sauces, and simmered dishes. No tamari or GF soy sauce option is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi (katsuobushi, niboshi) is the foundation of traditional kaiseki seasoning and is present in nearly every course. No plant-based dashi alternative is advertised.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu or allergy-handling protocol is published. The Nagano CVB listing notes English menus are available, but makes no mention of dietary accommodation procedures.

positives.text: English menus are available, which may ease communication of dietary needs.

caveats: Hours: Tabelog and Nagano CVB show dinner until 21:00, while HotPepper shows until 22:00 (LO 21:45); Wednesday listed as regular closed day on HotPepper but Tabelog says irregular closures only — confirm by phone before visiting. Seat count discrepancy: Tabelog shows 73 seats, HotPepper shows 200 across all banquet rooms; 73 reflects the regular dining area. Reservation policy is also inconsistent across sources — phone call advised. Geocoordinates sourced from geocoding.jp as the GSI address-search API did not resolve 東之門町 precisely.

name_ja: 日本料理 そば懐石 紡ぎ
cuisine: そば懐石・日本料理
address_ja: 長野県長野市大門町54-1 ぱてぃお大門 蔵楽庭内
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 長野市大字長野大門町 returned [138.187943, 36.656933]; within target box and approx 0.9km from Zenko-ji anchor. Postal code 380-0841 confirmed via official sources.
neighborhood: 大門町／ぱてぃお大門
seats_text: 10席のみ、全席完全予約制の小さなカウンター・座敷
closed_days: 日曜・月曜定休（祝日の場合は翌火曜休）、年末年始（12/29〜1/4）
cultural_comfort_note: English-speaking staff and an English menu are available; online reservations via TableCheck make booking straightforward for non-Japanese speakers.
vegan_detail: Traditional kaiseki format relies heavily on katsuobushi (bonito) and konbu dashi, and the signature soba courses include meat and seafood elements (wagyu, venison, mullet roe). One source notes vegetarian modifications are "partially available" with advance notice, but full vegan accommodation has not been confirmed. Diners must contact the restaurant in advance to discuss options.
gf_detail: The restaurant's centerpiece is house-milled 100% buckwheat (十割) soba — no binding wheat flour in the noodles themselves — but a traditional kaiseki menu almost certainly uses wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) in sauces, dressings, and simmered dishes, and dashi may contain cross-contamination risk. No dedicated GF protocol or fryer separation has been documented. Celiacs should call ahead to discuss course modifications; safe dining cannot be assumed without direct staff confirmation.

bio.background: Born 1980 in Nagano City, Machida Tetsuya trained at the Michelin two-star kaiseki restaurant 「つる幸」in Kanazawa before going on to refine his soba technique at 「そば処 木鶏 (Kidori)」in Nagano. Inspired by the region's seasonal bounty, he returned to Shinshu to open Tsumugi in March 2022 inside the historic Kurananiwa courtyard of the Patio Daimon complex, steps from the Zenko-ji pilgrimage approach. His wife, 町田 由起子 (Yukiko), serves as hostess and co-proprietor.
bio.origin: 長野市 (Nagano City)
bio.specialty: 100% buckwheat soba kaiseki — house-milled daily on a stone mill using Shinshu cultivars (Norisawa, Nagawa) and Togakushi spring water, woven into a nine-to-twelve-course omakase alongside seasonal Shinshu vegetables, venison from the Bi-Gasshara Plateau, and Shinshu Premium Wagyu.
bio.philosophy: The name 紡ぎ (tsumugi, 'weaving') captures the restaurant's mission: threading together the bounty of Shinshu nature, local producers, traditional technique, and the people who gather at the table — whether celebrating a milestone or mourning a loss. Every course is designed to slow time down and let guests savour Nagano's terroir through all five senses.

anecdotes.text: Machida trained at Michelin 2-star 「つる幸」in Kanazawa before pivoting to soba-kaiseki, a combination inspired by a family trip that led him to train further at 「そば処 木鶏」in Nagano.
Tsumugi is housed in a Taisho-era warehouse within the Patio Daimon complex, where exposed wooden beams provide an atmospheric backdrop for a ten-seat counter experience.

Signature venison from the Bi-Gasshara Plateau is slow-cooked until tender and finished with locally produced honey and balsamic vinegar — a dish that appeared in Nagano's jibier (game) promotion coverage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen protocol has been documented. Traditional kaiseki cooking uses shared equipment across courses that contain wheat-based soy sauce, which poses cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaiseki cuisine universally uses standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), which contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative has been mentioned by the restaurant or in any review.

Multi-course soba kaiseki includes simmered dishes, sauces, and dressings that typically rely on wheat-containing shoyu; the buckwheat soba noodles themselves are 100% buckwheat but share a kitchen with these preparations.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Katsuobushi (bonito flake) dashi is the standard base in traditional kaiseki; no plant-based dashi alternative has been confirmed. Courses include meat and seafood throughout.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One traveller review noted staff were 'very kind of you to work around our allergies with creativity,' suggesting willingness to accommodate, but this was informal and course-level substitutions cannot be guaranteed.
English-speaking staff are available and English menus are provided, which facilitates allergy communication, but no formal allergy protocol is publicly documented.

positives.text: The soba noodles are 100% pure buckwheat (十割), house-milled daily — no wheat flour binder used in the noodles themselves, unlike the majority of soba restaurants.
Reservation-only format with a tiny 10-seat room gives the chef the ability to discuss dietary needs personally at the time of booking.

caveats: Hours have a holiday exception (Mon holiday → open Mon, closed Tue instead); verify current schedule when booking. The 10-seat reservation-only format means dietary accommodation requests must be made at booking time. GF and vegan confidence ratings reflect absence of documented protocols — call 026-219-2654 or email info@kaiseki-tsumugi.com before visiting.

cuisine: 蕎麦（手打ちそば・うどん）
address_ja: 長野県長野市元善町486
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch result for 長野県長野市元善町486; coordinates within 1.57km of Zenkō-ji center, inside the 1.61km target radius.
neighborhood: 善光寺仲見世通り
seats_text: Seating available; no count confirmed in any source. The interior is described as having ample room.
hours_raw: 月・水・木・日・祝：11:00～品切れ次第終了（24:00目安）、金・土：17:00ごろ～、定休日：火曜日
closed_days: 毎週火曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighbourhood soba counter tucked inside a souvenir arcade; the owner is reportedly warm and chatty but signage, menus, and conversation are in Japanese only.
vegan_detail: Standard soba tsuyu at traditional Japanese soba shops is made with katsuo (bonito) dashi; no plant-based broth option has been documented here. The menu also includes curry and udon, with no indication of vegan adaptations. Assume all dipping and warm broths contain fish stock.
gf_detail: Soba noodles in Japan are commonly blended with wheat flour (ni-hachi style, 20% wheat) unless explicitly stated as juwari (100% buckwheat). No source confirms this shop uses juwari soba. The kitchen also produces udon (100% wheat) and curry, meaning wheat cross-contact is a serious risk. The standard tsuyu dipping sauce contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free accommodation or allergy awareness has been documented. Celiac diners should not eat here without direct verbal confirmation of noodle type and sauce ingredients.
caveats: Hours vary by day and soba sells out early; Friday and Saturday lunch service is not confirmed — those days appear to run dinner-only from around 17:00. Confirm by phone before visiting. Cash only. No English menu or staff confirmed.

bio.background: A small handmade-soba and udon shop sitting within a souvenir arcade on the Zenkoji temple approach (仲見世通り), Nagano City. The shop doubles as a craft and gift space, reflecting the owner's eclectic interests — visitors report cycling gear and pottery among the décor.
bio.specialty: Hand-pulled soba noodles made with Nagano Prefecture buckwheat flour, served cold (seiru) or warm.
bio.philosophy: A neighbourhood spot with a relaxed, slightly bohemian feel: jazz plays through JBL speakers and the owner is known to hold court on topics ranging from audio equipment to the history of Zenkoji Temple.

anecdotes.text: Multiple visitors note that jazz plays through JBL speakers and the owner is happy to chat about his audio setup and Zenkoji history, giving the shop an unexpectedly intimate, parlour-like atmosphere for a temple-district soba stop.
The shop can be entered either from the Nakamise-dori side (between a washi-goods store and a rice-cracker shop) or from the quieter temple lodging lane (宿坊通り) on the opposite side.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen produces udon (pure wheat) alongside soba, making shared surfaces and water a near-certain cross-contact risk. No separation or allergen protocol has been documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soba tsuyu is made with soy sauce that contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative has been reported for this establishment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Katsuo (bonito) dashi is the expected base for all soba broth and tsuyu here; no plant-based broth option is documented. Fish-stock contact unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocol, allergen menu, or English allergy communication has been documented at this restaurant. Inquire directly in Japanese before ordering.

positives.text: Soba is freshly hand-pulled on premises from local Nagano buckwheat flour — if the noodle ratio and sauce ingredients can be confirmed, there is a potential path for a gluten-sensitive (non-celiac) diner eating plain cold soba without tsuyu.

cuisine: Handmade soba (二八, 80% buckwheat/20% wheat)
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野東後町21 グランドハイツ表参道弐番館1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 長野県長野市大字長野東後町 (lng 138.187439, lat 36.652988). Approximately 934m from Zenkoji, within the 1.61km target radius. Note: Tabelog lists address as 東後町21 while some blogs list 東後町15; Tabelog/Hotpepper address used.
neighborhood: 東後町・権堂 (Higashi-go-cho / Gondo), Zenkoji approach area
seats_text: 24 seats: counter (3), 4-person tables, raised tatami area, and 1 terrace seat
hours_raw: 火〜土 11:00〜17:00（LO 16:30）、日 11:00〜15:00、月曜定休（祝日翌日も休み）、そば売り切れ次第終了
closed_days: 月曜（祝日の翌日も休み）
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood soba shop catering primarily to local Japanese diners; no English menu or staff English noted, though the casual counter/tatami setting and simple ordering process are manageable without much Japanese.
vegan_detail: The broth is clearly bonito (katsuo) based — reviewers specifically highlight its strong katsuo flavor. Standard soy-sauce tsuyu also contains wheat. No vegan alternatives are listed.
gf_detail: Despite Tabelog's 十割 (100% buckwheat) category tag, multiple independent sources confirm this restaurant serves 二八 soba — 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour. The noodles themselves contain wheat. Additionally, tsuyu (dipping broth) is made with conventional soy sauce (wheat-containing), and the menu includes tempura with wheat batter. No dedicated GF options or allergen handling is mentioned anywhere. Not safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.

bio.background: An independent neighborhood soba shop on the Zenkoji approach corridor in Nagano's Higashi-go-cho district, within walking distance of Zenkoji Temple. The restaurant distinguishes itself by grinding its own buckwheat in-house with a stone mill, with the milling area visible from the entrance. It is not affiliated with the national 吉祥庵 chain (Four Seeds Corporation).
bio.specialty: Handmade soba set lunch — zaru soba or kake soba with a mini tempura bowl, cold tofu, soba dumplings, and pickles; known for generous portions at low prices (weekday lunch around ¥980)
bio.philosophy: 挽き立て・打ち立て・茹で立て — freshly ground, freshly rolled, freshly boiled; the restaurant's tagline is 'soba embodies spirit; soba is appreciated for its aroma.'

anecdotes.text: The stone mill used to grind buckwheat on-site is visible from the entrance, signaling the restaurant's commitment to fresh, house-ground flour.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles contain wheat (二八 soba is 20% wheat flour); tempura is battered with wheat flour. Cross-contamination risk is high in a soba kitchen where wheat is used routinely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard tsuyu (dipping broth) is made with katsuo dashi and conventional soy sauce, which contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce or tamari alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito (katsuo) dashi is central to the broth; reviewers specifically note the strong bonito flavor. No plant-based broth option is offered.

positives.text: Buckwheat itself is 80% of the noodle composition; those with wheat allergy (not celiac-level intolerance) should confirm with staff, though no allergy protocol is documented.

caveats: Tabelog categorizes this restaurant as 十割蕎麦 (100% buckwheat), but two independent Japanese blog sources (web-komachi.com, web-komachi article title explicitly says 二八そば) and the menu context confirm the noodles are 二八 (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat). The Tabelog category tag appears to be an error or mislabeling. Address discrepancy: Tabelog/Hotpepper list 東後町21; nagatabe.com blog lists 東後町15 — both point to the same restaurant with the same phone number. Hours close earlier on days when soba sells out (irregular).

cuisine: Tonkatsu
address_ja: 長野県長野市大字長野岩石町222-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 長野県長野市大字長野岩石町222番地; ~0.53km from Zenkō-ji, well within 1.61km radius
neighborhood: Iwasishi-cho, Zenkoji-shita
seats_text: 13 seats in a renovated 100-year-old boarding house; second-floor tatami-style room (shoes off, slippers on)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–13:30 (Mon–Tue, Fri–Sun); Dinner 17:00–19:00 (Fri–Sat only); Closed Wed–Thu. Note: some sources list closed days as Tue–Wed — confirm by phone before visiting.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday (possibly Tuesday — sources conflict; call ahead)
cultural_comfort_note: Owner greets reservation guests by name; all service is in Japanese, but the reservation-only format means you communicate by phone in advance — some English may be manageable with patience.
vegan_detail: Entirely pork-focused; sets include pork-based miso soup and the restaurant's identity is built around premium pig breeds. No vegan options exist.
gf_detail: Tonkatsu is coated in panko breadcrumbs (wheat), making the main dish unsafe for celiacs. The sides (rice, miso soup, tofu) may be gluten-free individually, but no dedicated GF fryer or allergen protocol is documented anywhere. Soy sauce used in house sauce likely contains wheat. Ask directly about side dishes if wheat-sensitive.

bio.background: Takano spent decades working for a railway company, commuting between Tokyo and Nagano. At age 50 he took early retirement due to health concerns from years of shift work, with no prior restaurant experience. His path to tonkatsu began serendipitously: a friend took him to a celebrated tonkatsu restaurant as a retirement gift, where the chef taught him the fundamentals of frying meat.
bio.specialty: Low-temperature-rested tonkatsu — the cutlet is briefly seared at high heat then held at 65–70°C for up to 15 minutes, letting residual heat bring the interior to its optimal 67°C. The result is a thin, shatteringly crisp crust over meat that is juicy, tender, and lighter than conventional deep-frying.
bio.philosophy: Takano challenges the prevailing tonkatsu orthodoxy: he believes the flavor of pork peaks around 67°C internally, so he treats the frying process as a two-stage technique borrowed from teppanyaki. He sources four premium Shinshu pork varieties — SPF, Chiyo Phantom, Azumino pasture-raised, and Yamato — and presents a whole block of meat tableside so guests choose their own cut.

anecdotes.text: Jomando operates out of a 100-year-old renovated boarding house that doubles as a used bookstore and retro café called Daifukuya (大福屋). The restaurant sign only goes up during lunch hours; outside of that window, the space reverts to its bookshop identity.

Takano spent years refining the resting time after frying — starting at 4 minutes and eventually extending to 15 — treating each order as an experiment to find the precise window that maximises umami without drying the meat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All tonkatsu is coated in panko (wheat breadcrumbs) and fried in shared oil. No source documents a dedicated GF fryer or any separation of wheat-free items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: House-made dipping sauce is served alongside rock salt. The soy sauce base of the house sauce almost certainly contains wheat; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes involve pork; miso soup is pork-broth based. No plant-only cooking surface or preparation area is described.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy protocol, allergen menu, or staff allergy-handling practice is documented in any reviewed source. Guests with dietary restrictions should call the restaurant directly before reserving.

positives.text: The reservation-only format means each order is prepared individually to order, which in principle reduces the risk of rushed cross-contamination compared to high-volume kitchens.

caveats: Closed days conflict across sources (some say Wed–Thu, one says Tue–Wed); always call 026-235-5477 to confirm before visiting. Cash-only note from one source conflicts with Tabelog listing electronic money and QR payments — clarify on reservation call. Hours for dinner service (Fri–Sat 17:00–19:00) appear only in some sources and may have changed.

cuisine: Italian / Wood-fired Pizza & Sandwiches
address_ja: 長野県長野市長野東町200
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch for 長野市長野東町200; result: [138.188995, 36.656506]. Approximately 0.57 km from Zenkoji target point — well within the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: Higashimachi (東町), Zenkoji-shita
seats_text: Approximately 9 seats: 5 counter + 4 table seats in original pizza format; current TIKU-Plus format has interior table seating and an outdoor terrace. Exact current count unconfirmed.
hours_raw: Eat-in 11:00–15:00, Takeout 10:30–15:00; closes when sold out. Irregular closed days — check Instagram (@tiku_nagano) before visiting. Monday and Tuesday typically closed.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday (irregular — verify on SNS)
cultural_comfort_note: Small husband-and-wife spot in a traditional machiya; no English signage reported, but the self-service cafeteria-style ordering and a simple visual menu make ordering manageable even without Japanese.
vegan_detail: The menu is omnivore-oriented — signature pizzas use cheese and the sandwich lineup features prosciutto, bacon, and salami. Some vegetable-heavy sandwiches may exist but hidden dairy (cheese) is standard. No plant-based or vegan designation noted anywhere. Ask specifically, and expect limited options at best.
gf_detail: Wheat is the foundation of the entire concept: dough is made from a blend of Hokkaido and Nagano domestic wheat flour, natural yeast, and sea salt — baked in a wood-fired oven. There is no gluten-free alternative bread or pizza base mentioned in any source. Cross-contamination risk is high. Not safe for celiac diners.

bio.background: Opened in March 2012 by a husband-and-wife team in a renovated traditional machiya (folk house) a short walk from Zenkoji Temple. After nearly a decade as a beloved wood-fired pizza restaurant, the couple closed the pizza format in November 2021 — citing a deliberate decision to sustain the business long-term — then reopened in 2022 as a takeout-only wood-fired bread sandwich shop. By 2024–2025 the shop had evolved again into TIKU-Plus, blending sit-down wood-fired pizza with the sandwich counter under the same roof.
bio.origin: Nagano City, Japan
bio.specialty: Wood-fired pizza and bread sandwiches made with domestic wheat, natural yeast, and natural sea salt; topped with organically grown or pesticide-free vegetables and artisan cheeses sourced from Okayama, Hokkaido, and Nagano.
